entries.title: Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: casehub-engine Vert.x event-bus handlers lack @Blocking — JPA consumer calls fail from IO thread
entries.body:
casehub-engine Vert.x event-bus handlers lack @Blocking — JPA consumer calls fail from IO thread

## Symptom

Calling `CaseHub.startCase()` in a `@QuarkusTest` (or in production with JPA-backed SPIs) throws:

```
io.quarkus.runtime.BlockingOperationNotAllowedException:
  Cannot start a JTA transaction from the IO thread.
```

Propagated through the Vert.x event bus as:
```
(RECIPIENT_FAILURE,8185) io.quarkus.runtime.BlockingOperationNotAllowedException:
  Cannot start a JTA transaction from the IO thread
```

The error surfaces as a `CompletionStage` failure in `startCase()` — not in any test or application code that is obviously on the IO thread.

## Root Cause

`CaseHub.startCase()` publishes a message to the Vert.x event bus. The engine's handler that processes this message — which activates the case binding and calls `WorkerProvisioner.provision()` — runs on the Vert.x IO thread by default (not annotated with `@Blocking`).

When `provision()` calls `WorkerContextProvider.buildContext()`, which in turn calls a JPA-backed `CaseLineageQuery.findCompletedWorkers()` annotated with `@Transactional(REQUIRED)`, the JTA infrastructure detects the IO thread and throws.

The casehub-engine's own tests (`WorkBrokerEndToEndTest`) don't hit this because they use inline `Worker.function()` lambdas — those don't call JPA.

## Fix

**Consumer-side (immediate):** Change `@Transactional(REQUIRED)` to `@Transactional(TxType.SUPPORTS)` on read-only JPA methods called from the provision/buildContext chain. `SUPPORTS` joins an existing transaction if one is active; otherwise proceeds without starting one — safe from any thread context.

```java
@Override
@Transactional(TxType.SUPPORTS)
public List<WorkerSummary> findCompletedWorkers(UUID caseId) {
    // JPA SELECT — safe without a transaction
}
```

**Upstream fix (casehub-engine):** Add `@Blocking` to the event-bus handler method that calls `WorkerProvisioner.provision()`, so it dispatches to the Quarkus worker thread pool instead of the IO event loop.

**Test workaround:** If the full `startCase()` round-trip is needed in tests, bypass it by firing `CaseLifecycleEvent` directly via CDI `Event<CaseLifecycleEvent>.fireAsync()` — this exercises the ledger capture and lineage query pipeline without going through the IO-threaded engine handler.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The stack trace shows the exception inside `startCase()` with the Vert.x error code `RECIPIENT_FAILURE,8185` — nothing in the trace mentions the engine's event-bus handler or `@Blocking`. A skilled developer would add `@Blocking` to their own application code, not look for its absence in a library's internal handler. The casehub-engine's own tests pass (using inline lambdas), so the bug is invisible until a consumer implements JPA-backed SPIs.

**See also:** [GE-20260428-fd7a65](GE-20260428-fd7a65.md) — @Transactional(SUPPORTS) makes JPA reads callable from any thread including Vert.x IO thread

**See also:** GE-20260428-9311f8 — casehub-engine SPI bean collision when engine is indexed | GE-20260420-f0a37a — Vert.x eventBus.publish() vs send() fan-out semantics

## Variant — Silent Failure (in-memory store, no exception)

When the `@ConsumeEvent` handler calls a `@Transactional` service but an **in-memory** store alternative is active (no JPA in the call chain), the `BlockingOperationNotAllowedException` is **not thrown**. Instead, the transaction simply does not commit — the handler appears to run, the observable in-memory state changes _before_ the call succeed (e.g. `PlanItem.markRunning()` which is lock-free), but the `@Transactional` work (e.g. `WorkItemService.create()`) is silently rolled back or never committed.

**Symptom:** `PlanItem` transitions to RUNNING (in-memory, no transaction needed), but the `WorkItem` is never created. No exception, no log warning. `workItemStore.scanAll()` returns empty.

**Diagnosis:** Add `@ConsumeEvent(value = ..., blocking = true)` to the handler. If the WorkItem then appears, the transaction context was the issue.

**Fix:** `@ConsumeEvent(value = EventBusAddresses.HUMAN_TASK_SCHEDULE, blocking = true)` on any `@ConsumeEvent` handler that calls `@Transactional` services.

**Why it's worse than the explicit exception variant:** The JPA variant throws a visible exception. The in-memory variant silently completes — tests that only assert on `PlanItem` status pass, and the missing WorkItem is only discovered if the test also queries the store.

entries.title: Reactive @Tool calling resolveChannel() blocks Vert.x I/O thread — @Blocking required even when method body looks non-blocking
entries.body:
Reactive @Tool calling resolveChannel() blocks Vert.x I/O thread — @Blocking required even when method body looks non-blocking

## Reactive @Tool calling resolveChannel() blocks Vert.x I/O thread

### Context

`ReactiveQhorusMcpTools` in casehub-qhorus returns `Uni<T>` from `@Tool` methods.
A new tool `setChannelTypeConstraints` was implemented without `@Blocking` because
the method body returned a `Uni<ChannelDetail>` — it looked reactive. The method
called `resolveChannel(channel)` from the base class `QhorusMcpToolsBase`, which
internally calls the **blocking** `ChannelService.findById()` or `findByName()`
(JPA / Panache statics). quarkus-mcp-server dispatches `@Tool` methods on the
Vert.x I/O thread by default. Blocking JPA on the I/O thread produces no
exception — just a Vert.x blocked-thread warning and latency stall under load.

### Symptom

No compile error, no test failure, no exception at runtime. In development: a
Vert.x blocked-thread log warning. Under load: request latency degrades as the
I/O thread stalls waiting for JPA.

### Root cause

`resolveChannel()` is defined in `QhorusMcpToolsBase` and appears to be a simple
routing utility — its signature `UUID resolveChannel(String channel)` gives no
indication that it does JPA lookups. The blocking nature is invisible from the
tool method body. A developer implementing a reactive tool that needs UUID-or-name
resolution correctly returns `Uni<T>` but forgets `@Blocking` because the tool
method itself has no blocking code — only the inherited helper does.

### Fix

Add `@Blocking` to every reactive `@Tool` method in `ReactiveQhorusMcpTools` that
calls `resolveChannel()`:

```java
@Tool(name = "set_channel_type_constraints", ...)
@Blocking                           // ← required: resolveChannel() makes blocking JPA calls
public Uni<ChannelDetail> setChannelTypeConstraints(
        @ToolArg(name = "channel") String channel,
        /* other args */) {
    UUID channelId = resolveChannel(channel);  // blocking — JPA inside base class
    return channelService.setTypeConstraints(channelId, ...)
            .flatMap(ch -> messageStore.countByChannel(ch.id)
                    .map(count -> toChannelDetail(ch, count.longValue())));
}
```

**Reference pattern:** `project_channel` in the same class already uses `@Blocking`
for the same reason (calls `resolveChannel()` before `projectAndRender()`).

### Why this is non-obvious

The method body returns `Uni<T>` and chains reactive operations — it reads as
non-blocking. The blocking call is hidden two layers deep: the tool calls a base
class helper, which calls a blocking CDI service, which calls JPA. There's no
compiler warning, no Quarkus build-time error, and no runtime exception — only
a Vert.x thread-blocked warning that's easy to miss in development logs.

### Why this fix

`@Blocking` on the tool method moves execution to a worker thread before
`resolveChannel()` is invoked — the JPA call runs on the worker thread where
blocking is safe. The `Uni<T>` return still composes correctly on the reactive
pipeline after `@Blocking` completes.

An alternative would be to refactor `resolveChannel()` to use
`ReactiveChannelStore` and return `Uni<UUID>`, then chain it into the reactive
pipeline without `@Blocking`. That's the structurally correct fix but requires
changing the base class and all 39 tool signatures. `@Blocking` is the practical
fix for the pattern as it currently stands.

entries.title: Resolve CDI ambiguity between two competing @DefaultBean implementations by introducing a concrete non-default bean
entries.body:
Resolve CDI ambiguity between two competing @DefaultBean implementations by introducing a concrete non-default bean

## Context

Two `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` beans from different JARs both implement the same interface. CDI can't resolve which to use and throws `AmbiguousResolutionException`. Standard approaches don't help:

- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types` is silently ignored for beans from Jandex-indexed JARs (see GE-20260601-848232)
- Marking one as `@Alternative` requires changes to a JAR you don't own
- `@Priority` on `@Alternative` has CDI 2.0 global-scope problems

## Technique

Add a concrete `@ApplicationScoped` bean to your project (production or test sources as appropriate) that implements the conflicting interface:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
public class ProjectGroupMembershipProvider implements GroupMembershipProvider {
    @Override
    public Set<GroupMember> membersOf(String groupName) {
        return Set.of(); // or delegate to preferred implementation
    }
}
```

CDI selects the most-specific non-default bean. Since your bean is NOT `@DefaultBean`, it wins over both competing `@DefaultBean` implementations and the `AmbiguousResolutionException` is resolved. No changes to any JAR required.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

Most developers reach for `exclude-types` first (fails silently for Jandex-indexed JARs), then try `@Priority` on one of the `@DefaultBean` beans (requires modifying third-party JARs), then consider `@Alternative` + `selected-alternatives` (same problem). The CDI suppression rule — "a non-default bean beats all @DefaultBean candidates for the same type" — is the correct lever, and a thin adapter class is the minimal mechanism that activates it without touching any dependency.

**See also:** [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for Jandex JARs | [GE-20260601-3dbc80](GE-20260601-3dbc80.md) — exclude-types as technique for @DefaultBean ambiguity

**See also:** [GE-20260601-7a3b38](GE-20260601-7a3b38.md) — @DefaultBean invisible when supertype JAR has no Jandex index

**See also:** [GE-20260616-d70e7e](GE-20260616-d70e7e.md) — selected-alternatives silently accepts non-@Alternative beans

entries.title: runtime Maven scope on a shared mock library silently expands CDI scan, breaking @DefaultBean suppression
entries.body:
runtime Maven scope on a shared mock library silently expands CDI scan, breaking @DefaultBean suppression

## Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` startup fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException` for an SPI type that has a `@DefaultBean` implementation:

```
Build failure: Build failed due to errors
  [error]: AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type
  GroupMembershipProvider and qualifiers [@Default]
    - CLASS bean [target=MockGroupMembershipProvider]
    - CLASS bean [target=NoOpGroupMembershipProvider]
```

The `@DefaultBean` on `NoOpGroupMembershipProvider` is not suppressing the ambiguity.

## Context

A shared library jar contains mock implementations alongside production code and is declared `<scope>runtime</scope>` in the consuming module's `pom.xml`. The scope was changed from `test` to `runtime` to satisfy a different CDI injection point at production build time. The side effect — bringing the mock into the CDI scan — was not anticipated.

## Root Cause

Maven's `runtime` scope is available at both production runtime AND test runtime. Quarkus CDI discovery scans all classpath entries at `@QuarkusTest` startup, including runtime-scoped jars. `@DefaultBean` suppression only activates when there is exactly one non-default bean. When two plain `@ApplicationScoped` beans implement the same interface, ArC raises ambiguity regardless of `@DefaultBean`.

## Fix

Exclude the unwanted mock from CDI scan in test `application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.example.platform.mock.MockGroupMembershipProvider
```

Add to `src/test/resources/application.properties`. Create the file if it doesn't exist. Apply to every module that uses runtime-scoped shared mock jars with `@QuarkusTest`.

## Why Non-Obvious

`@DefaultBean` reads as "there's always a winner." But `NoOpGroupMembershipProvider` is itself a plain `@ApplicationScoped` bean — it competes equally with the mock. The triggering scope change was made for a completely unrelated injection point, making the connection non-obvious. No compile-time warning; failure only surfaces at CDI container startup.

**See also:** [GE-20260604-81a6a6](GE-20260604-81a6a6.md) — @DefaultBean @Unremovable required when injection point lives in a different Maven module

entries.title: Two @DefaultBean beans for the same type → Quarkus AmbiguousResolutionException, not Unsatisfied
entries.body:
Two @DefaultBean beans for the same type → Quarkus AmbiguousResolutionException, not Unsatisfied

## What happened

Two `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` implementations of the same SPI type appeared on the CDI
classpath simultaneously (one from a platform mock library, one from a persistence testing module).
Quarkus reported **"Ambiguous dependencies"** and refused to start — even though both beans
explicitly said "I am a default, defer to anyone else".

## Why this is non-obvious

`@DefaultBean` is documented as "yield to any other bean of the same type". With two
`@DefaultBean` beans and no non-default implementation, neither bean yields — the contract
("yield to someone else") cannot resolve because there IS no "someone else" with higher
precedence. The error message is **Ambiguous** (same as two regular `@ApplicationScoped`
beans conflicting), not **Unsatisfied** (what you'd expect if both were supposedly yielding).
Developers often assume two `@DefaultBean` beans resolve cleanly because they both "opt in"
to being overridden.

## Root cause

CDI/ARC `@DefaultBean` semantics: a default bean is only eligible when no other bean at
the same or higher priority is eligible. With two `@DefaultBean` beans and no higher-priority
bean, the tie-break is undefined — ARC treats this as an ambiguous injection point.

## Fix

Exclude the unwanted `@DefaultBean` implementation via `application.properties`:
```properties
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.platform.mock.MockCurrentPrincipal,\
  io.casehub.platform.mock.MockGroupMembershipProvider,\
  io.casehub.platform.mock.MockPreferenceProvider
```

This removes the conflicting bean from CDI discovery entirely, leaving the desired
`@DefaultBean` as the sole implementation. Prefer excluding the one that returns null or
uninitialized values rather than the one with correct sentinel behaviour.

### Why this fix

`@Alternative @Priority(N)` would also work, but requires activating it via
`selected-alternatives` — that is a heavier configuration change and makes the
intent less clear. `exclude-types` is surgical: "this specific class should not
participate in CDI at all in this context."

### Alternative — use `Instance<T>` with `isUnsatisfied()` to avoid the conflict entirely

Rather than excluding a conflicting bean after the fact, don't provide a competing `@DefaultBean` in the extension at all. Inject `Instance<T>` and check `isUnsatisfied()` at call time:

```java
@Inject Instance<PreferenceProvider> preferenceProviderInstance;

int threshold;
if (preferenceProviderInstance.isUnsatisfied()) {
    threshold = DEFAULT_VALUE;  // no provider on classpath
} else {
    threshold = preferenceProviderInstance.get().resolve(...).getOrDefault(KEY).value();
}
```

`Instance<T>` always satisfies its own injection point (the container injects the `Instance` holder, not `T` directly). When zero beans of type `T` exist, `isUnsatisfied()` returns `true`; when one is active, `.get()` returns it. No `@DefaultBean` is added to the extension, so no conflict can arise.

**Best for:** library/extension authors who control their own code and want to make a platform SPI optional without contributing a competing `@DefaultBean`.

**Limitation:** requires call-site guards (`isUnsatisfied()` before `.get()`); callers that inject `T` directly still get an ambiguous point if two `@DefaultBean` beans exist from other sources.

## Constraints

Applies when both `@DefaultBean` implementations reach the CDI container's scanning phase
(i.e. both are indexed via `quarkus.index-dependency` or are in the app's own sources).
In two-repo / multi-module projects, platform library JARs can silently contribute
`@DefaultBean` mocks that conflict with internal no-ops.

## Alternatives considered

- Mark one bean `@Alternative @Priority(1)` instead of `@DefaultBean` — works but changes
  the semantics of the "winning" bean and requires `selected-alternatives` configuration.
- Remove one of the `@DefaultBean` implementations from the classpath entirely — clean, but
  may not be possible if it comes from a third-party dependency.

## Invalidation triggers

Would change if Quarkus ARC adds a deterministic tie-break for two `@DefaultBean` beans
(e.g. favour the one in the current module's sources). Track Quarkus changelog for ARC
`@DefaultBean` resolution changes.

**See also:** [GE-20260515-fd3156](GE-20260515-fd3156.md) — @DefaultBean must be on @Produces method not on the class | [GE-20260529-ab148d](GE-20260529-ab148d.md) — multiple CDI constructors without @Inject → UnsatisfiedResolutionException | [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for Jandex-indexed JARs

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean

**See also:** [GE-20260609-26ffa5](GE-20260609-26ffa5.md) — @ApplicationScoped POJO + @Produces for same type causes CDI ambiguous bean resolution

**See also:** [GE-20260515-99cf39](GE-20260515-99cf39.md) — config-driven @Produces @DefaultBean for engine-internal strategy selection

**See also:** [GE-20260614-337397](GE-20260614-337397.md) — quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension registers @Default ChatModel, clashing with @DefaultBean fallback | [GE-20260604-b3d2ef](GE-20260604-b3d2ef.md) — quarkus-flow CDI extension already registers WorkflowApplication — adding @Produces causes ambiguity

**See also:** [GE-20260615-6c4767](../casehub-engine/GE-20260615-6c4767.md) — adding custom CDI qualifier to @ApplicationScoped removes @Default

**See also:** GE-20260627-51e402 — @Alternative totally suppresses @DefaultBean (not just deprioritized, gone from container)

entries.title: ChatModel.doChat(ChatRequest) is the override point for test doubles — not chat(ChatRequest)
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it
entries.body:
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it

### What happened
A field validator banning BiDi control characters to prevent prompt injection implemented a standard check: reject codepoints 0–31 (C0), 127–159 (C1), U+200E/200F (LRM/RLM), U+202A–202E (LRE/RLE/PDF/LRO/RLO), and U+2066–2069 (LRI/RLI/FSI/PDI). Code review caught that U+061C was missing. The validator would have passed strings containing the Arabic Letter Mark.

### Root cause
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark, ALM) was added in Unicode 6.3 as a BiDi control character. It is in the Arabic block (U+0600–U+06FF), not in any standard control character range (C0: 0x00–0x1F, C1: 0x80–0x9F). It has no visible glyph and controls BiDi text direction.

Standard documentation and implementations of "ban control characters" focus on C0/C1 ranges. The dedicated BiDi control ranges (LRM/RLM/LRE etc.) are more widely known. U+061C predates none of these — it was simply added later and sits in an unexpected block.

### Fix
Add explicit check for U+061C alongside other BiDi controls:

```java
private static boolean isBanned(final int cp) {
    if (cp <= 0x001F) return true;                      // C0 control chars
    if (cp >= 0x007F && cp <= 0x009F) return true;     // DEL + C1 control chars
    if (cp == 0x061C) return true;                      // ALM — Arabic Letter Mark (BiDi, Unicode 6.3)
    if (cp == 0x200E || cp == 0x200F) return true;     // LRM, RLM
    if (cp >= 0x202A && cp <= 0x202E) return true;     // LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO
    if (cp >= 0x2066 && cp <= 0x2069) return true;     // LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI
    if (cp == 0x200B || cp == 0xFEFF) return true;     // ZWSP, BOM/ZWNBSP
    if (cp == 0x200C || cp == 0x200D) return true;     // ZWNJ, ZWJ
    if (cp == 0x2028 || cp == 0x2029) return true;     // LINE SEP, PARA SEP
    return false;
}
```

### Why this is non-obvious
The Unicode BiDi control characters everyone knows (LRM, RLM, LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO, LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI) are all in the General Punctuation block (U+2000–U+206F). U+061C is in the Arabic block. No standard "control character" resource bundles it with the others. The Unicode BiDi Algorithm spec mentions it, but security-focused implementations typically enumerate the well-known ranges without reading the full BiDi spec for outliers.

entries.title: CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface
entries.body:
CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface

## Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` fails at boot with `AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type X`. The production code compiles and the beans look correct.

## Context

Defining multiple `@ApplicationScoped` implementations of the same SPI interface (e.g. three `SkillProfileProvider` implementations) so users can choose which to activate. All three are unqualified, so CDI cannot resolve which one to inject at a single injection point.

## What was tried

Adding all three as `@ApplicationScoped` beans, assuming CDI would pick the first one or throw a clear build-time error.

## Root cause

CDI requires exactly one unambiguous bean per injection type when no qualifier is used. Multiple unqualified `@ApplicationScoped` beans implementing the same interface all compete — CDI cannot resolve the injection point and throws at container startup, not at compile time.

## Fix

Designate exactly one implementation as the default (leave it unqualified `@ApplicationScoped`). Mark all others with `@Alternative`:

```java
// Default — CDI injects this when no override is present
@ApplicationScoped
public class WorkerProfileSkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }

// Not active by default — user activates via @Alternative @Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
public class CapabilitiesSkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }

@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
public class ResolutionHistorySkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }
```

Users override the default at their own application level:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
public class MyCustomProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The error only appears at `@QuarkusTest` boot — compile succeeds silently. The fix (marking non-default implementations `@Alternative`) is easy once known, but the CDI resolution rules around `@Alternative` vs unqualified beans are non-obvious. The natural instinct is to let the user pick via config, not via CDI qualifiers.

**See also:** GE-20260415-884e48 — @Alternative @Priority(n) globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0 | GE-20260417-c59817 — quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives activates @Alternative beans in application.properties | GE-20260421-1cfae6 — @Produces @DefaultBean provides consumer-replaceable CDI defaults without @Alternative

**See also:** [GE-20260428-9311f8](GE-20260428-9311f8.md) — casehub-engine @ApplicationScoped no-op SPI beans collide with consumer implementations when engine is indexed | [GE-20260427-97650e](GE-20260427-97650e.md) — CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface

**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab "Use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites without Mockito"
**See also:** GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in test-helper jar are invisible without Jandex index

## Update — Variant: two @DefaultBean impls for the same type

`@DefaultBean` in Quarkus Arc means "use this if no non-default bean for this type exists."
When **two** `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` beans implement the same interface, Arc cannot
resolve the injection point — both are default, neither wins — and throws
`AmbiguousResolutionException` as soon as any production bean injects that type. The symptom
only surfaces once an `@Inject` for the interface is added; before that the ambiguity exists
silently in the CDI container.

**Scenario:** Two library modules each provide a `@DefaultBean CurrentPrincipal` for
"uncontested" environments (`MockCurrentPrincipal` from casehub-platform, `QhorusInboundCurrentPrincipal`
from casehub-qhorus). Adding `@Inject CurrentPrincipal` to any production bean in the app
module triggers the error — the injection point forces CDI to resolve and it finds two equals.

**Fix — exclude one at application level:**

```properties
# application.properties — exclude the stub; the runtime principal wins
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.platform.mock.MockCurrentPrincipal
```

**Test fix — use quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives to force a third, definitive impl:**

```properties
# src/test/resources/application.properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=io.casehub.platform.testing.FixedCurrentPrincipal
```

**Why @DefaultBean differs from @Alternative:** `@DefaultBean` is not in the CDI spec — it is
a Quarkus Arc extension that says "this bean loses to any explicit non-default bean." But when
two `@DefaultBean` beans compete, there is no winner — Arc throws rather than picking arbitrarily.
The `@Alternative` pattern (one winner, rest marked alternative) is the correct SPI design for
exactly-one-at-a-time. `@DefaultBean` is for extension-provided stubs that yield to consumer
overrides, not for multiple peers. Two `@DefaultBean` peers should be refactored so that only one
module provides the default; the other moves to `@Alternative`.

entries.title: Two @DefaultBean beans for the same type → Quarkus AmbiguousResolutionException, not Unsatisfied
entries.body:
Two @DefaultBean beans for the same type → Quarkus AmbiguousResolutionException, not Unsatisfied

## What happened

Two `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` implementations of the same SPI type appeared on the CDI
classpath simultaneously (one from a platform mock library, one from a persistence testing module).
Quarkus reported **"Ambiguous dependencies"** and refused to start — even though both beans
explicitly said "I am a default, defer to anyone else".

## Why this is non-obvious

`@DefaultBean` is documented as "yield to any other bean of the same type". With two
`@DefaultBean` beans and no non-default implementation, neither bean yields — the contract
("yield to someone else") cannot resolve because there IS no "someone else" with higher
precedence. The error message is **Ambiguous** (same as two regular `@ApplicationScoped`
beans conflicting), not **Unsatisfied** (what you'd expect if both were supposedly yielding).
Developers often assume two `@DefaultBean` beans resolve cleanly because they both "opt in"
to being overridden.

## Root cause

CDI/ARC `@DefaultBean` semantics: a default bean is only eligible when no other bean at
the same or higher priority is eligible. With two `@DefaultBean` beans and no higher-priority
bean, the tie-break is undefined — ARC treats this as an ambiguous injection point.

## Fix

Exclude the unwanted `@DefaultBean` implementation via `application.properties`:
```properties
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.platform.mock.MockCurrentPrincipal,\
  io.casehub.platform.mock.MockGroupMembershipProvider,\
  io.casehub.platform.mock.MockPreferenceProvider
```

This removes the conflicting bean from CDI discovery entirely, leaving the desired
`@DefaultBean` as the sole implementation. Prefer excluding the one that returns null or
uninitialized values rather than the one with correct sentinel behaviour.

### Why this fix

`@Alternative @Priority(N)` would also work, but requires activating it via
`selected-alternatives` — that is a heavier configuration change and makes the
intent less clear. `exclude-types` is surgical: "this specific class should not
participate in CDI at all in this context."

### Alternative — use `Instance<T>` with `isUnsatisfied()` to avoid the conflict entirely

Rather than excluding a conflicting bean after the fact, don't provide a competing `@DefaultBean` in the extension at all. Inject `Instance<T>` and check `isUnsatisfied()` at call time:

```java
@Inject Instance<PreferenceProvider> preferenceProviderInstance;

int threshold;
if (preferenceProviderInstance.isUnsatisfied()) {
    threshold = DEFAULT_VALUE;  // no provider on classpath
} else {
    threshold = preferenceProviderInstance.get().resolve(...).getOrDefault(KEY).value();
}
```

`Instance<T>` always satisfies its own injection point (the container injects the `Instance` holder, not `T` directly). When zero beans of type `T` exist, `isUnsatisfied()` returns `true`; when one is active, `.get()` returns it. No `@DefaultBean` is added to the extension, so no conflict can arise.

**Best for:** library/extension authors who control their own code and want to make a platform SPI optional without contributing a competing `@DefaultBean`.

**Limitation:** requires call-site guards (`isUnsatisfied()` before `.get()`); callers that inject `T` directly still get an ambiguous point if two `@DefaultBean` beans exist from other sources.

## Constraints

Applies when both `@DefaultBean` implementations reach the CDI container's scanning phase
(i.e. both are indexed via `quarkus.index-dependency` or are in the app's own sources).
In two-repo / multi-module projects, platform library JARs can silently contribute
`@DefaultBean` mocks that conflict with internal no-ops.

## Alternatives considered

- Mark one bean `@Alternative @Priority(1)` instead of `@DefaultBean` — works but changes
  the semantics of the "winning" bean and requires `selected-alternatives` configuration.
- Remove one of the `@DefaultBean` implementations from the classpath entirely — clean, but
  may not be possible if it comes from a third-party dependency.

## Invalidation triggers

Would change if Quarkus ARC adds a deterministic tie-break for two `@DefaultBean` beans
(e.g. favour the one in the current module's sources). Track Quarkus changelog for ARC
`@DefaultBean` resolution changes.

**See also:** [GE-20260515-fd3156](GE-20260515-fd3156.md) — @DefaultBean must be on @Produces method not on the class | [GE-20260529-ab148d](GE-20260529-ab148d.md) — multiple CDI constructors without @Inject → UnsatisfiedResolutionException | [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for Jandex-indexed JARs

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean

**See also:** [GE-20260609-26ffa5](GE-20260609-26ffa5.md) — @ApplicationScoped POJO + @Produces for same type causes CDI ambiguous bean resolution

**See also:** [GE-20260515-99cf39](GE-20260515-99cf39.md) — config-driven @Produces @DefaultBean for engine-internal strategy selection

**See also:** [GE-20260614-337397](GE-20260614-337397.md) — quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension registers @Default ChatModel, clashing with @DefaultBean fallback | [GE-20260604-b3d2ef](GE-20260604-b3d2ef.md) — quarkus-flow CDI extension already registers WorkflowApplication — adding @Produces causes ambiguity

**See also:** [GE-20260615-6c4767](../casehub-engine/GE-20260615-6c4767.md) — adding custom CDI qualifier to @ApplicationScoped removes @Default

**See also:** GE-20260627-51e402 — @Alternative totally suppresses @DefaultBean (not just deprioritized, gone from container)

entries.title: quarkus-work (full) brings JpaWorkloadProvider that clashes with any other WorkloadProvider bean
Resolve CDI ambiguity between two competing @DefaultBean implementations by introducing a concrete non-default bean
entries.body:
Resolve CDI ambiguity between two competing @DefaultBean implementations by introducing a concrete non-default bean

## Context

Two `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` beans from different JARs both implement the same interface. CDI can't resolve which to use and throws `AmbiguousResolutionException`. Standard approaches don't help:

- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types` is silently ignored for beans from Jandex-indexed JARs (see GE-20260601-848232)
- Marking one as `@Alternative` requires changes to a JAR you don't own
- `@Priority` on `@Alternative` has CDI 2.0 global-scope problems

## Technique

Add a concrete `@ApplicationScoped` bean to your project (production or test sources as appropriate) that implements the conflicting interface:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
public class ProjectGroupMembershipProvider implements GroupMembershipProvider {
    @Override
    public Set<GroupMember> membersOf(String groupName) {
        return Set.of(); // or delegate to preferred implementation
    }
}
```

CDI selects the most-specific non-default bean. Since your bean is NOT `@DefaultBean`, it wins over both competing `@DefaultBean` implementations and the `AmbiguousResolutionException` is resolved. No changes to any JAR required.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

Most developers reach for `exclude-types` first (fails silently for Jandex-indexed JARs), then try `@Priority` on one of the `@DefaultBean` beans (requires modifying third-party JARs), then consider `@Alternative` + `selected-alternatives` (same problem). The CDI suppression rule — "a non-default bean beats all @DefaultBean candidates for the same type" — is the correct lever, and a thin adapter class is the minimal mechanism that activates it without touching any dependency.

**See also:** [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for Jandex JARs | [GE-20260601-3dbc80](GE-20260601-3dbc80.md) — exclude-types as technique for @DefaultBean ambiguity

**See also:** [GE-20260601-7a3b38](GE-20260601-7a3b38.md) — @DefaultBean invisible when supertype JAR has no Jandex index

**See also:** [GE-20260616-d70e7e](GE-20260616-d70e7e.md) — selected-alternatives silently accepts non-@Alternative beans

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

## @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

### Stack
Quarkus 3.31.1, CDI 4.0

### Symptom
Multiple beans implement the same SPI interface. Each alternative is annotated with
`@Alternative @Priority(10)` as well as `@ApplicationScoped`. On Quarkus startup, the
application fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException` — all implementations are active
simultaneously.

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ContextDiffStrategy
and qualifiers [@Default]: [TopLevelContextDiffStrategy, JsonPatchContextDiffStrategy, 
NoOpContextDiffStrategy]
```

### Context
SPI pattern where one default bean and two `@Alternative` beans implement an interface.
The intent was to activate one alternative at a time via
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`.

### What was tried
Adding `@Priority(10)` alongside `@Alternative` — expected this to be a ranking hint
that would help CDI resolve ambiguity. Instead, `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is what
globally activates the bean in CDI 4.0, making all three implementations simultaneously
active and causing the ambiguous resolution error.

### Root cause
In CDI 4.0 (Jakarta EE 10), `@Alternative @Priority(n)` is a shorthand for globally
activating an alternative across the entire application — no `beans.xml` or
`selected-alternatives` config needed. When ALL alternatives carry this annotation,
they are ALL active, which breaks injection points expecting a single bean.

### Solution 1 — Remove @Priority, use selected-alternatives config
**Approach:** Strip `@Priority` from all alternatives; activate one via config.
**Pros:** Per-profile/per-module control without code changes; the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching.
**Cons:** Requires a config entry per activation; all alternatives default to inactive.

Remove `@Priority` from all `@Alternative` beans. Activate a specific alternative only
via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`:

```java
// WRONG — @Priority globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0
@Alternative
@Priority(10)
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }

// CORRECT — @Alternative only; activation controlled by config
@Alternative
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }
```

```properties
# application.properties — activate the desired alternative
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.MyAlternativeImpl
```

### Solution 2 — Use different @Priority values to establish precedence
**Approach:** Keep `@Alternative @Priority` but assign distinct priority values; CDI resolves to the highest.
**Pros:** No config entry needed; the default (lowest priority) is always active, the override (highest priority) wins when present on the classpath. Works naturally for optional-module patterns where a higher-capability implementation should auto-replace the baseline.
**Cons:** All alternatives are always globally active (the core CDI 4.0 behaviour). The "default" must have the lowest priority; adding a third implementation requires careful priority assignment.

```java
// Default — always active; lowest priority loses when a higher one is present
@Alternative
@Priority(0)
@ApplicationScoped
public class ClaimFirstStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }

// Override — higher priority wins; active only when on the classpath
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
public class SemanticWorkerSelectionStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }
```

CDI injects the `@Priority(1)` bean when both are present; falls back to `@Priority(0)` when only
the default module is on the classpath. No `beans.xml` or `selected-alternatives` required.

**When to choose Solution 2 over Solution 1:** When the override is an optional module (not always
on the classpath) and the default must always be present. Config-based activation (Solution 1)
requires an explicit entry even for the default case; priority-based resolution (Solution 2) makes
the default zero-configuration.

### Why this is non-obvious
The `@Priority` annotation is commonly used alongside `@Alternative` in CDI tutorials
and examples from the CDI 2.x era, where `@Priority` on an alternative was a *ranking*
hint. In CDI 4.0 the semantics changed — `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is now an
*activation* mechanism (equivalent to listing it in `beans.xml` with a priority). The
Quarkus docs for `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` don't warn about this interaction.

### Why this fix
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` gives per-profile/per-module control via config,
which is the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching (same as how `PanningStrategyLoopControl`
and other engine SPIs are activated). It doesn't require code changes to switch backends.

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** GE-20260427-97650e "CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface"
**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab — use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md)

### Update — Test-refactoring trap: two classes declaring the same @Alternative

During a test refactoring, if two `@QuarkusTest` classes *simultaneously* declare an
`@Alternative @Priority(2)` inner class implementing the same type, Quarkus boot fails
with `AmbiguousResolutionException` even though `mvn test-compile` passes cleanly. The
compile succeeds because both classes are syntactically valid; the CDI conflict only
surfaces at application startup.

entries.title: Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException

### Symptom

After removing `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from one CDI bean, a different bean
silently becomes the active implementation. No `AmbiguousResolutionException`, no
warning, no log. Tests that depend on the removed bean's behaviour fail with
unrelated-looking assertion errors.

### Context

Three implementations of the same port interface coexist in the build:

| Bean | Annotations | Role |
|------|------------|------|
| `PrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @DefaultBean` | Layer 1 naive baseline |
| `QhorusPrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(1)` | Layer 3 qhorus messaging |
| `PrReviewCaseService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(2)` | Layer 5 engine (active) |

This is the CDI displacement pattern for progressive layer integration — each layer
adds an implementation; CDI priority determines which is active at runtime.

### What was tried (didn't work)

Removed `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from `PrReviewCaseService` to fix an anti-pattern
documented in ARC42STORIES §8 ("CDI priority gymnastics"). Expected: the plain
`@ApplicationScoped` bean would still displace the `@DefaultBean` baseline via
Quarkus's `@DefaultBean` semantics.

### Root cause

CDI resolution rules interact in a non-obvious way:

1. `@DefaultBean` beans are suppressed when any non-`@DefaultBean` bean exists ✅
2. Activated `@Alternative` beans (with `@Priority`) take precedence over regular beans ⚠️
3. After removing `@Alternative` from `PrReviewCaseService`, it becomes a regular bean
4. `QhorusPrReviewService` (`@Alternative @Priority(1)`) is still an activated alternative
5. **Rule 2 applies**: the activated alternative wins over the regular bean
6. `QhorusPrReviewService` becomes the active implementation — silently

The mental model "I'm just removing unnecessary annotations" is wrong. The annotations
are load-bearing: they participate in a priority chain where every bean's annotation
set matters.

### Fix

All implementations in the `@Alternative` chain must be addressed together, not one at
a time. To properly fix the anti-pattern:

- Either make ALL non-baseline implementations use `@DefaultBean` displacement
  (remove `@Alternative @Priority` from all of them and ensure only one non-`@DefaultBean` exists)
- Or keep the `@Alternative @Priority` chain intact and accept it as the mechanism
  for layer coexistence

Do not remove `@Alternative` from one bean without checking what other `@Alternative`
implementations exist for the same interface.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reason about CDI alternatives in pairs: "my bean vs the default."
The three-way interaction (DefaultBean + Alternative(1) + Alternative(2)) is rarely
encountered, and the silent resolution change (no exception, wrong bean) gives no
diagnostic signal. The symptom (test assertion failure on unrelated data) is
completely disconnected from the cause (CDI annotation change).

### Why this fix

The obvious approach — "just remove the anti-pattern annotation" — is wrong because
it treats the annotations as decoration rather than as a load-bearing priority chain.
The fix requires understanding the full set of implementations and their annotation
interactions before changing any single one.

### Alternatives considered

- Remove `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from only the active bean → silently breaks resolution (this gotcha)
- Remove `@Alternative` from ALL implementations and use `@DefaultBean` displacement → requires ensuring exactly one non-`@DefaultBean` impl, and retiring or `@DefaultBean`-ing the intermediate layers
- Keep `@Alternative @Priority` chain as-is → accepted as the working mechanism until a coordinated cleanup addresses all implementations together

**See also:** GE-20260626-c21b02 — @DefaultBean suppression by any Instance<T> peer, a different silent failure in the same CDI displacement space.

entries.title: @Typed required when CDI bean implements a framework-owned interface to prevent AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Typed required when CDI bean implements a framework-owned interface to prevent AmbiguousResolutionException

### Symptom

Quarkus fails to start with:

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type com.framework.SomeInterface
```

Occurs when a custom CDI bean implements an interface that a framework component already
provides a bean for.

### Context

Implementing a framework SPI interface (e.g. `AgentChannelBackend` in casehub-qhorus)
to create a custom backend or handler. The framework ships its own `@ApplicationScoped`
implementation of the same interface.

### Root cause

CDI sees two beans satisfying the same interface type — the framework's default bean and
the custom implementation. Without disambiguation, CDI cannot choose. Unlike `@DefaultBean`
(which requires the custom bean to displace it), `@Typed` restricts which types the custom
bean is visible as, removing it from contention for the framework's injection point.

### Fix

Add `@Typed` to the custom bean, listing only the interfaces it should be discoverable as:

```java
// ❌ CDI sees this as AgentChannelBackend — conflicts with framework's QhorusChannelBackend
@Dependent
public class PushAgentDispatch implements AgentDispatchMechanism, AgentChannelBackend {
    // ...
}

// ✅ CDI only sees this as AgentDispatchMechanism and PushAgentDispatch
@Dependent
@Typed({AgentDispatchMechanism.class, PushAgentDispatch.class})
public class PushAgentDispatch implements AgentDispatchMechanism, AgentChannelBackend {
    // ...
}
```

Always include the concrete class itself in `@Typed` to preserve direct-type injection.

### Why this is non-obvious

The error message names the interface type but gives no hint that `@Typed` exists or
would fix it. Developers typically try `@Alternative`, `@DefaultBean`, or `@Priority`
— all wrong in this case. `@Typed` is rarely needed in application code (it's mainly
a framework-authoring tool) and doesn't appear in most Quarkus tutorials. The Java
`implements` declaration is the cause: removing one interface from `implements` would
fix it, but `@Typed` does this at the CDI level without changing the implementation
hierarchy.

**See also:** [GE-20260531-1ec900](GE-20260531-1ec900.md) — Quarkus ARC registers non-CDI abstract class as @Dependent bean when it declares @Observes and implements a CDI SPI

entries.title: CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface
entries.body:
CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface

## Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` fails at boot with `AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type X`. The production code compiles and the beans look correct.

## Context

Defining multiple `@ApplicationScoped` implementations of the same SPI interface (e.g. three `SkillProfileProvider` implementations) so users can choose which to activate. All three are unqualified, so CDI cannot resolve which one to inject at a single injection point.

## Fix

```java
// Default — CDI injects this when no override is present
@ApplicationScoped
public class WorkerProfileSkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }

// Not active by default — user activates via @Alternative @Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
public class CapabilitiesSkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }

@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
public class ResolutionHistorySkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }
```

Users override the default at their own application level:

```java

@Alternative
@Priority(1)

```

## Why this is non-obvious

The fix (marking non-default implementations `@Alternative`) is easy once known, but the CDI resolution rules around `@Alternative` vs unqualified beans are non-obvious. The natural instinct is to let the user pick via config, not via CDI qualifiers.

**See also:** GE-20260415-884e48 — @Alternative @Priority(n) globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0 | GE-20260417-c59817 — quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives activates @Alternative beans in application.properties | GE-20260421-1cfae6 — @Produces @DefaultBean provides consumer-replaceable CDI defaults without @Alternative

**See also:** [GE-20260428-9311f8](GE-20260428-9311f8.md) — casehub-engine @ApplicationScoped no-op SPI beans collide with consumer implementations when engine is indexed | [GE-20260427-97650e](GE-20260427-97650e.md) — CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface

**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab "Use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites without Mockito"

## Update — Variant: two @DefaultBean impls for the same type

`@DefaultBean` in Quarkus Arc means "use this if no non-default bean for this type exists."
When **two** `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` beans implement the same interface, Arc cannot
resolve the injection point — both are default, neither wins — and throws
`AmbiguousResolutionException` as soon as any production bean injects that type. The symptom
only surfaces once an `@Inject` for the interface is added; before that the ambiguity exists
silently in the CDI container.

**Scenario:** Two library modules each provide a `@DefaultBean CurrentPrincipal` for
"uncontested" environments (`MockCurrentPrincipal` from casehub-platform, `QhorusInboundCurrentPrincipal`
from casehub-qhorus). Adding `@Inject CurrentPrincipal` to any production bean in the app
module triggers the error — the injection point forces CDI to resolve and it finds two equals.

**Fix — exclude one at application level:**

```properties
# application.properties — exclude the stub; the runtime principal wins
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.platform.mock.MockCurrentPrincipal
```

**Test fix — use quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives to force a third, definitive impl:**

```properties
# src/test/resources/application.properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=io.casehub.platform.testing.FixedCurrentPrincipal
```

**Why @DefaultBean differs from @Alternative:** `@DefaultBean` is not in the CDI spec — it is
a Quarkus Arc extension that says "this bean loses to any explicit non-default bean." But when
two `@DefaultBean` beans compete, there is no winner — Arc throws rather than picking arbitrarily.
The `@Alternative` pattern (one winner, rest marked alternative) is the correct SPI design for
exactly-one-at-a-time. `@DefaultBean` is for extension-provided stubs that yield to consumer
overrides, not for multiple peers. Two `@DefaultBean` peers should be refactored so that only one
module provides the default; the other moves to `@Alternative`.

entries.title: quarkus-flow CDI extension already registers WorkflowApplication — adding @Produces causes ambiguous dependency
entries.body:
quarkus-flow CDI extension already registers WorkflowApplication — adding @Produces causes ambiguous dependency

## Symptom

Quarkus startup fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type io.serverlessworkflow.impl.WorkflowApplication and qualifiers [@Default]` when you add a custom CDI producer for `WorkflowApplication`.

## Context

When integrating quarkus-flow into a Quarkus module that needs to control `WorkflowApplication` lifecycle (e.g. ensuring a singleton shared across workflow executions), it's natural to write a `@Produces @ApplicationScoped WorkflowApplication` bean.

## What was tried (didn't work)

Adding `WorkflowApplicationProducer`:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
public class WorkflowApplicationProducer {
    @Produces @ApplicationScoped
    public WorkflowApplication produce() {
        return WorkflowApplication.builder().build();
    }
}
```

Quarkus startup throws `AmbiguousResolutionException` — two `@ApplicationScoped WorkflowApplication` beans exist.

## Root cause

The `quarkus-flow` extension includes a `WorkflowApplicationRecorder` that registers `WorkflowApplication` as a CDI bean via the Quarkus build-time extension mechanism (`WorkflowApplicationRecorder.class` visible in the quarkus-flow jar). The extension recorder fires before CDI deployment, injecting its own `WorkflowApplication` singleton into the container. A user-written `@Produces` bean creates a second one.

## Fix

Remove the custom producer entirely. The `WorkflowApplication` singleton is managed by the quarkus-flow extension and is available for `@Inject` without any producer. To customise the application, investigate the quarkus-flow extension configuration properties instead.

## Why this is non-obvious

The quarkus-flow jar does not contain a `beans.xml` with an obvious CDI bean declaration. The registration happens via `WorkflowApplicationRecorder` — a Quarkus build-time recorder, which is invisible to standard CDI scanning and the `AmbiguousResolutionException` message names the type but not which class introduced the conflict.

**See also:** [GE-20260614-337397](GE-20260614-337397.md) — quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension @Default ChatModel clashes with @DefaultBean | [GE-20260601-fcf0d9](GE-20260601-fcf0d9.md) — two @DefaultBean beans → AmbiguousResolutionException

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set

### Symptom

`org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value for entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId`

The test code explicitly sets `cle.tenancyId = "test-tenant"`. Java field access confirms the value is set. The error is impossible — except it isn't.

### Context

JPA JOINED inheritance with a base class (`LedgerEntry`) and subclass (`CaseLedgerEntry`). Both declare a `tenancyId` field with `@Column(name = "tenancy_id", nullable = false)`. In JOINED inheritance, these map to separate columns on separate tables (`ledger_entry.tenancy_id` and `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`).

### What was tried (didn't work)

1. Setting `tenancyId` on the entity before calling `save()` — still fails
2. Using `em.persist()` directly instead of the repository's `save()` — still fails
3. Setting the base field via reflection workaround — works but is wrong
4. Clean rebuild (`mvn clean`) to rule out stale bytecode — still fails

### Root cause

Java field shadowing creates two separate fields: `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` and `CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId`. They share the same name but are different fields in the JVM.

Hibernate bytecode enhancement (active by default in Quarkus 3.x) intercepts field access on entity classes. When code does `cle.tenancyId = "value"`, the enhancement transparently routes to the subclass field. When code does `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId`, the enhancement ALSO routes to the subclass field — making the shadowing invisible to Java code.

But `em.persist()` reads field values via reflection on the actual declared fields. `LedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId")` reads the base class field — which was never set (all writes were routed to the subclass by enhancement). The `nullable = false` constraint on the base table column fires.

### Diagnostic

```java
CaseLedgerEntry cle = new CaseLedgerEntry();
cle.tenancyId = "from-subclass";

// Java access (enhanced — both route to subclass):
cle.tenancyId                           → "from-subclass"
((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId           → "from-subclass"

// Reflection (raw — reveals the actual field state):
CaseLedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId").get(cle) → "from-subclass"
LedgerEntry.class.getDeclaredField("tenancyId").get(cle)     → null  // THE BUG
```

### Fix

Remove the shadowing field from the subclass. JPA JOINED inheritance maps the base class field to the base table automatically — the subclass does not need its own declaration.

```java
// BEFORE (broken):
public class CaseLedgerEntry extends LedgerEntry {
    @Column(name = "tenancy_id", nullable = false)
    public String tenancyId;  // SHADOWS LedgerEntry.tenancyId
}

// AFTER (correct):
public class CaseLedgerEntry extends LedgerEntry {
    // tenancyId inherited from LedgerEntry — no shadowing
}
```

Migration: drop the `tenancy_id` column from the join table (it's redundant with `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`).

### Why this is non-obvious

Hibernate's bytecode enhancement makes the shadowing completely invisible to Java code. Both `cle.tenancyId` and `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId` return the same value. The error message names the subclass entity but the actual null is on the base class — and you can't see it without reflection. The symptom appears impossible: "the field is null but I literally just set it."

### Why this fix

Removing the shadowing field is the only correct fix. Workarounds (setting the base field via reflection, using a special save method) are fragile — they couple application code to JPA internals and break when the entity hierarchy changes. The field belongs on the base class; the subclass inherits it.

### Alternatives considered

- **Reflection workaround in test helpers** — sets the base field directly. Works but couples tests to the field shadowing structure and masks the underlying design bug.
- **Setting both fields explicitly** — impossible via normal Java code because enhancement intercepts the access. Would require reflection on every write path.
- **Hibernate `@Access(AccessType.PROPERTY)` with getter/setter** — might route correctly but changes the access strategy for all fields and is a much larger change.

### Caveats

- This only triggers when bytecode enhancement is active. Without enhancement (e.g. plain Hibernate outside Quarkus), both `cle.tenancyId` and `((LedgerEntry) cle).tenancyId` access their respective declared fields — the shadowing is visible and the developer would naturally set both.
- The fix requires a Flyway migration to drop the redundant column from the join table.

**See also:** [GE-20260609-58e5d9](GE-20260609-58e5d9.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null

## Context

A JPA JOINED inheritance hierarchy where the child entity redeclares a field already declared in
the parent entity — both with `@Column(nullable = false)`. The child table gets a denormalized copy
of the column (e.g. for a local index to avoid joins). This pattern is intentional for performance
but creates a hidden trap for external writers.

Example from casehub platform:
- `LedgerEntry.tenancyId @Column(name="tenancy_id", nullable=false)` — in `ledger_entry` table
- `CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId @Column(name="tenancy_id", nullable=false, length=64)` — in `case_ledger_entry` table

## Symptom

```
org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value
  for entity io.casehub.ledger.model.CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId
```

Thrown even though the code explicitly sets `entry.tenancyId = "tenant-1"` before calling
`em.persist()`. The value is visibly non-null; the error says it is null.

## Root cause

Java field access is statically resolved by the reference type at compile time. When `entry`
is declared as `CaseLedgerEntry`:

```java
entry.tenancyId = value;   // resolves to CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (shadow field)
```

This writes to the child's own field (mapped to `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`). The parent's
field `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` (mapped to `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`, also `nullable=false`) is
never written. Hibernate's nullability check validates BOTH the child's property AND the parent's
property — the parent's property is null → `PropertyValueException`.

## Fix

Set both fields explicitly from any external code that touches the entity:

```java
entry.tenancyId = tenancyId;                    // CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (case_ledger_entry)
((LedgerEntry) entry).tenancyId = tenancyId;    // LedgerEntry.tenancyId (ledger_entry)
```

The explicit cast forces the Java compiler to emit `PUTFIELD LedgerEntry.tenancyId` — a
separate bytecode instruction targeting the parent class's field declaration, distinct from
the child's shadow field.

The correct long-term fix: remove the field redeclaration from the child entity. Use a
`@SecondaryTable` or a Hibernate `@Formula` to maintain the index without the shadow field.

## Why this is non-obvious

The call site `entry.tenancyId = value` looks complete. The entity compiles and CDI-deploys
without warnings. The failure surfaces at `em.persist()` with a message naming the child entity
and property — which ARE set — not the parent entity and property — which are NOT set.

The Hibernate nullability check message `entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId` refers to the
property-path in the entity class hierarchy, not the Java field that is actually null. This
leads to the investigation going in the wrong direction (inspecting the child's field assignment
rather than the parent's).

Any code that uses direct field access from outside the entity (common in JPA entities without
getters/setters) is silently broken when this pattern is in play. Only tests that actually
perform a database persist — not those that simply assign and read back — will catch it.

**See also:** [GE-20260612-1f4ed8](GE-20260612-1f4ed8.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement causes NOT NULL failure

entries.title: ChatModel.doChat(ChatRequest) is the override point for test doubles — not chat(ChatRequest)
Hibernate named query gains a parameter in one repo — sibling repo call site silently unbound until runtime
entries.body:
Hibernate named query gains a parameter in one repo — sibling repo call site silently unbound until runtime

### Symptom

```
org.hibernate.QueryParameterException: No argument for named parameter ':tenancyId'
  at ReactiveSqmQueryImpl.executeReactiveUpdate
```

All message dispatches fail with HTTP 500 when `casehub.ledger.hash-chain.enabled=true`.

### Context

`LedgerMerkleFrontier.deleteBySubjectAndLevel` is a `@NamedQuery` defined in `casehub-ledger`.
The ledger repo added a `:tenancyId` predicate to the query. `ReactiveLedgerEntryJpaRepository.save()`
in `casehub-qhorus` (a sibling repo) calls this named query but was never updated to bind the new parameter.
The call site compiled and deployed without error — the unbound parameter only surfaces at runtime
when the query executes.

### Root cause

JPA named queries are resolved by name at runtime, not by signature at compile time. When the
query definition in one repo gains a new parameter, every call site in every sibling repo that
uses the same named query must be manually updated. There is no compile-time or build-time
enforcement — neither the Java compiler nor the Maven build reports the mismatch.

### Fix

Bind the new parameter at every call site that uses the named query:

```java
session.createNamedQuery("LedgerMerkleFrontier.deleteBySubjectAndLevel")
    .setParameter("subjectId", e.subjectId)
    .setParameter("level", node.level)
    .setParameter("tenancyId", e.tenancyId)  // ← add when query gains this param
    .executeUpdate()
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The build succeeds. The CDI wiring is correct. The error only appears on a specific code
path (`hash-chain.enabled=true`) that may not be covered by standard CI tests. The exception
message names the parameter (`':tenancyId'`) but gives no indication of which call site is
missing the binding — a classpath-wide search is required to find the stale call site.

**See also:** [GE-20260617-54b75b](GE-20260617-54b75b.md) — Hibernate Reactive Panache repo.update() converts positional ?N params to named params — unbound at runtime

entries.title: U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it
entries.body:
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it

### What happened
A field validator banning BiDi control characters to prevent prompt injection implemented a standard check: reject codepoints 0–31 (C0), 127–159 (C1), U+200E/200F (LRM/RLM), U+202A–202E (LRE/RLE/PDF/LRO/RLO), and U+2066–2069 (LRI/RLI/FSI/PDI). Code review caught that U+061C was missing. The validator would have passed strings containing the Arabic Letter Mark.

### Root cause
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark, ALM) was added in Unicode 6.3 as a BiDi control character. It is in the Arabic block (U+0600–U+06FF), not in any standard control character range (C0: 0x00–0x1F, C1: 0x80–0x9F). It has no visible glyph and controls BiDi text direction.

Standard documentation and implementations of "ban control characters" focus on C0/C1 ranges. The dedicated BiDi control ranges (LRM/RLM/LRE etc.) are more widely known. U+061C predates none of these — it was simply added later and sits in an unexpected block.

### Fix
Add explicit check for U+061C alongside other BiDi controls:

```java
private static boolean isBanned(final int cp) {
    if (cp <= 0x001F) return true;                      // C0 control chars
    if (cp >= 0x007F && cp <= 0x009F) return true;     // DEL + C1 control chars
    if (cp == 0x061C) return true;                      // ALM — Arabic Letter Mark (BiDi, Unicode 6.3)
    if (cp == 0x200E || cp == 0x200F) return true;     // LRM, RLM
    if (cp >= 0x202A && cp <= 0x202E) return true;     // LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO
    if (cp >= 0x2066 && cp <= 0x2069) return true;     // LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI
    if (cp == 0x200B || cp == 0xFEFF) return true;     // ZWSP, BOM/ZWNBSP
    if (cp == 0x200C || cp == 0x200D) return true;     // ZWNJ, ZWJ
    if (cp == 0x2028 || cp == 0x2029) return true;     // LINE SEP, PARA SEP
    return false;
}
```

### Why this is non-obvious
The Unicode BiDi control characters everyone knows (LRM, RLM, LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO, LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI) are all in the General Punctuation block (U+2000–U+206F). U+061C is in the Arabic block. No standard "control character" resource bundles it with the others. The Unicode BiDi Algorithm spec mentions it, but security-focused implementations typically enumerate the well-known ranges without reading the full BiDi spec for outliers.

entries.title: Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails
entries.body:
Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails

### Symptom

```
org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException: column "tenancy_id" violates not-null constraint
```

Entities persisted via a static factory or utility method fail on INSERT even though the owning
parent entity has `tenancyId` set correctly.

### Context

`LedgerMerkleTree.append(digest, currentFrontier, subjectId)` is a pure static utility that
builds new `LedgerMerkleFrontier` nodes. It has no knowledge of tenancy context. The caller
in `ReactiveLedgerEntryJpaRepository.save()` sets `tenancyId` on the `LedgerEntry` being saved
but forgot to propagate it to the frontier nodes returned by `append()`. The nodes go into
`session.persist()` with `tenancyId = null`, violating the NOT NULL constraint.

### Root cause

Static factory and utility methods that create JPA entities operate outside any tenancy context.
Multi-tenancy fields (`tenancyId`, `tenantKey`, etc.) are caller responsibilities, not factory
responsibilities. The omission is invisible until the `session.persist()` flush fires — and only
on code paths where the NOT NULL column is actually written.

### Fix

Propagate multi-tenancy fields from the parent entity onto all child entities immediately after
the factory call, before any `session.persist()`:

```java
final List<LedgerMerkleFrontier> newFrontier =
    LedgerMerkleTree.append(e.digest, currentFrontier, e.subjectId);
newFrontier.forEach(node -> node.tenancyId = e.tenancyId);  // ← propagate before persist
// ... then session.persist(node) for each
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The parent entity (`LedgerEntry`) has `tenancyId` set correctly and was used for all prior
operations. The factory method looks complete — it takes all the structural parameters it needs.
The tenancy propagation step is easy to forget because it is not part of the factory's contract
and there is no type system or annotation to enforce it.

results.query_text: JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing — subclass field shadows base class column causing Hibernate persist failure with NOT NULL constraint violation

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement: base class NOT NULL fails despite subclass field being set

JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null
entries.body:
JPA JOINED inheritance: child entity redeclaring parent field shadows the parent mapping — setting child field leaves parent nullable=false column null

## Context

A JPA JOINED inheritance hierarchy where the child entity redeclares a field already declared in
the parent entity — both with `@Column(nullable = false)`. The child table gets a denormalized copy
of the column (e.g. for a local index to avoid joins). This pattern is intentional for performance

Example from casehub platform:
