```
org.hibernate.PropertyValueException: not-null property references a null or transient value
  for entity io.casehub.ledger.model.CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId
```

Thrown even though the code explicitly sets `entry.tenancyId = "tenant-1"` before calling
`em.persist()`. The value is visibly non-null; the error says it is null.

## Root cause

Java field access is statically resolved by the reference type at compile time. When `entry`
is declared as `CaseLedgerEntry`:

```java
entry.tenancyId = value;   // resolves to CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (shadow field)
```

This writes to the child's own field (mapped to `case_ledger_entry.tenancy_id`). The parent's
field `LedgerEntry.tenancyId` (mapped to `ledger_entry.tenancy_id`, also `nullable=false`) is
never written. Hibernate's nullability check validates BOTH the child's property AND the parent's
property — the parent's property is null → `PropertyValueException`.

## Fix

Set both fields explicitly from any external code that touches the entity:

```java
entry.tenancyId = tenancyId;                    // CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId (case_ledger_entry)
((LedgerEntry) entry).tenancyId = tenancyId;    // LedgerEntry.tenancyId (ledger_entry)
```

The explicit cast forces the Java compiler to emit `PUTFIELD LedgerEntry.tenancyId` — a
separate bytecode instruction targeting the parent class's field declaration, distinct from
the child's shadow field.

The correct long-term fix: remove the field redeclaration from the child entity. Use a
`@SecondaryTable` or a Hibernate `@Formula` to maintain the index without the shadow field.

## Why this is non-obvious

The call site `entry.tenancyId = value` looks complete. The entity compiles and CDI-deploys
without warnings. The failure surfaces at `em.persist()` with a message naming the child entity
and property — which ARE set — not the parent entity and property — which are NOT set.

The Hibernate nullability check message `entity CaseLedgerEntry.tenancyId` refers to the
property-path in the entity class hierarchy, not the Java field that is actually null. This
leads to the investigation going in the wrong direction (inspecting the child's field assignment
rather than the parent's).

Any code that uses direct field access from outside the entity (common in JPA entities without
getters/setters) is silently broken when this pattern is in play. Only tests that actually
perform a database persist — not those that simply assign and read back — will catch it.

**See also:** [GE-20260612-1f4ed8](GE-20260612-1f4ed8.md) — JPA JOINED inheritance field shadowing + Hibernate bytecode enhancement causes NOT NULL failure

entries.title: Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails
entries.body:
Static entity factory creates JPA child entities without multi-tenancy fields — NOT NULL INSERT fails

### Symptom

```
org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException: column "tenancy_id" violates not-null constraint
```

Entities persisted via a static factory or utility method fail on INSERT even though the owning
parent entity has `tenancyId` set correctly.

### Context

`LedgerMerkleTree.append(digest, currentFrontier, subjectId)` is a pure static utility that
builds new `LedgerMerkleFrontier` nodes. It has no knowledge of tenancy context. The caller
in `ReactiveLedgerEntryJpaRepository.save()` sets `tenancyId` on the `LedgerEntry` being saved
but forgot to propagate it to the frontier nodes returned by `append()`. The nodes go into
`session.persist()` with `tenancyId = null`, violating the NOT NULL constraint.

### Root cause

Static factory and utility methods that create JPA entities operate outside any tenancy context.
Multi-tenancy fields (`tenancyId`, `tenantKey`, etc.) are caller responsibilities, not factory
responsibilities. The omission is invisible until the `session.persist()` flush fires — and only
on code paths where the NOT NULL column is actually written.

### Fix

Propagate multi-tenancy fields from the parent entity onto all child entities immediately after
the factory call, before any `session.persist()`:

```java
final List<LedgerMerkleFrontier> newFrontier =
    LedgerMerkleTree.append(e.digest, currentFrontier, e.subjectId);
newFrontier.forEach(node -> node.tenancyId = e.tenancyId);  // ← propagate before persist
// ... then session.persist(node) for each
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The parent entity (`LedgerEntry`) has `tenancyId` set correctly and was used for all prior
operations. The factory method looks complete — it takes all the structural parameters it needs.
The tenancy propagation step is easy to forget because it is not part of the factory's contract
and there is no type system or annotation to enforce it.

entries.title: JPA JOINED inheritance: filtering by subclass column vs inherited column for same UUID — only inherited uses the index
JPA subclass JPQL query in LedgerEntryRepository silently hides domain entries from consuming apps — IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ violation
entries.body:
JPA subclass JPQL query in LedgerEntryRepository silently hides domain entries from consuming apps — IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ violation

### What happened

`AmlInvestigationResourceTest` and `AmlLedgerChainTest` failed with:

```
Unique index or primary key violation: "IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ ON
LEDGER_ENTRY(SUBJECT_ID, SEQUENCE_NUMBER) VALUES (UUID '...', 1)"
insert into ledger_entry (..., dtype, id) values (..., 'QHORUS_MESSAGE', ?)
```

The qhorus COMMAND dispatch was trying to write `sequenceNumber=1` for a subject (`caseId`) that already had an `AmlCaseOpenedLedgerEntry` at `sequenceNumber=1`. Investigation showed that qhorus's `LedgerWriteService.record()` computed the next sequence number via:

```java
final int sequenceNumber = repository.findLatestBySubjectId(resolvedSubjectId)
        .map(e -> e.sequenceNumber + 1).orElse(1);
```

### Root cause

`repository` was `MessageLedgerEntryRepository`, which implemented `LedgerEntryRepository` with a **subclass-scoped** JPQL query:

```java
// WRONG — only sees QHORUS_MESSAGE dtype rows
"SELECT e FROM MessageLedgerEntry e WHERE e.subjectId = :sid ORDER BY e.sequenceNumber DESC"
```

With JOINED inheritance, `FROM MessageLedgerEntry` only returns rows with `dtype='QHORUS_MESSAGE'`. The `AmlCaseOpenedLedgerEntry` committed earlier (dtype=`AML_CASE_OPENED`) was invisible to this query — so `findLatestBySubjectId` returned `Optional.empty()` and assigned `sequenceNumber=1`, colliding with the domain entry.

The `IDX_LEDGER_ENTRY_SUBJECT_SEQ` constraint is on the base `LEDGER_ENTRY` table — it enforces global uniqueness across ALL subtypes for a given subject. The sequence query must be equally global.

### Fix

Changed to base-class JPQL so all subtypes are visible:

```java
// CORRECT — sees all LedgerEntry subtypes for the subject
"SELECT e FROM LedgerEntry e WHERE e.subjectId = :sid ORDER BY e.sequenceNumber DESC"
```

The proper re-architecture (casehub-qhorus#253) introduced a dedicated `LedgerEntryJpaRepository` using `FROM LedgerEntry` throughout, and `MessageLedgerEntryRepository` no longer implements `LedgerEntryRepository` — it retains only qhorus-scoped queries.

### Why this is non-obvious

qhorus's own tests never mix non-qhorus `LedgerEntry` subtypes in the same subject, so the scoped query always looked correct in qhorus CI. The bug only surfaces in a consuming application that writes both domain entries (`AmlCaseOpenedLedgerEntry`) and dispatches qhorus messages to the same subject (`caseId`). The symptom (unique constraint on sequence number) looks like a race condition, but the actual cause is a dtype-scope bug in the sequence-assignment query.

### Constraints

Applies when: (1) multiple teams write different `LedgerEntry` subclasses to the same `subjectId`, AND (2) the sequence-assignment query is scoped to one subclass. Any `LedgerEntryRepository` implementation that narrows JPQL to a subclass is potentially broken for consumers that share subjects across dtypes.

### Why this fix

A dedicated `LedgerEntryJpaRepository` (separate from `MessageLedgerEntryRepository`) enforces at the type level that cross-dtype operations use the correct base-class query. Patching the subclass JPQL directly would not prevent future regressions when new repositories are added.

entries.title: em.merge() + em.remove() on JOINED inheritance entity throws OptimisticLockException from wrong EntityManager context
entries.body:
em.merge() + em.remove() on JOINED inheritance entity throws OptimisticLockException from wrong EntityManager context

## Symptom

```
jakarta.persistence.OptimisticLockException: Unexpected row count
(expected row count 1 but was 0) [delete from subclass_table where id=?]
```

Deletion succeeds for the base table row but fails for the subclass table row — or vice versa.

## Context

Occurs when:
1. Entities are loaded by one `EntityManager` (e.g., via `repo.listAll()` which uses the repo's CDI-injected EM)
2. Another `EntityManager` (e.g., the service's own `@Inject EntityManager`) calls `em.merge(detachedEntity)` then `em.remove(mergedEntity)`
3. The entity uses `@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.JOINED)` with a base table and one or more subclass tables

## Root Cause

For `JOINED` inheritance, Hibernate must delete rows from **both** the base table and the subclass table, in the correct order. When `em.merge()` re-attaches a detached entity from a different EM context, Hibernate may reconstruct the entity as the base type only — losing the concrete subclass identity. `em.remove()` then issues the DELETE in the wrong order or against the wrong table, resulting in row count 0 for the subclass DELETE.

The error message ("Unexpected row count") suggests optimistic locking or concurrent modification — neither of which is the actual cause. The real issue is polymorphic type resolution on merge.

## Fix

Use `em.find()` in the **current** EntityManager context to obtain a properly typed, managed entity before calling `em.remove()`:

```java
// WRONG — entity loaded by a different EM; merge loses subclass identity
em.remove(em.contains(entry) ? entry : em.merge(entry));

// RIGHT — find in THIS EM context; Hibernate resolves the concrete subclass type
LedgerEntry managed = em.find(LedgerEntry.class, entry.id);
if (managed != null) {
    em.remove(managed);
}
```

`em.find()` issues a polymorphic `SELECT` that returns the concrete subclass type. `em.remove()` on that managed entity correctly deletes the subclass row first, then the base row.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The error message says "Unexpected row count" with a hint about optimistic locking — nothing in the message suggests cross-EM context or polymorphic type resolution. The pattern `em.contains(x) ? x : em.merge(x)` is commonly seen as the "safe" re-attachment idiom and works correctly for single-table entities. For JOINED inheritance it silently loses the subclass type.

### Why this fix

`em.find()` is guaranteed to return the correct polymorphic type because Hibernate uses the discriminator value from the base table row to select the right subclass. This is the same mechanism used when loading entities via JPQL. `em.merge()` does not make this guarantee for entities from a different persistence context.

entries.title: `event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`
entries.body:
`event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`

### Symptom

Type mismatch when bridging `Event.fireAsync()` to a Mutiny `Uni` pipeline:

```java
// Fails — CompletionStage<Event<MyEvent>>, not CompletionStage<VerificationResult>
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(...)))
    // compiler error: cannot convert Uni<Event<MyEvent>> to Uni<VerificationResult>
```

### Context

Quarkus reactive service using CDI `Event<T>` needs to fire an async event and return a `Uni<SomeType>` from the same pipeline.

### Root cause

`Event.fireAsync()` is declared in the CDI spec as returning `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` — it returns the event object itself wrapped in a completion stage, not `CompletionStage<Void>`. Most usage examples ignore the return value entirely, hiding the actual type.

### Fix

Use `.replaceWith()` to discard the `Event<T>` wrapper and substitute the intended result:

```java
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(entryId, actorId, keyRef)))
    .replaceWith(VerificationResult.SUSPECT);
```

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI spec says "returns a CompletionStage" without emphasising the `<Event<T>>` type parameter. Developers writing async event firing naturally assume `CompletionStage<Void>` because they're triggering a side effect and discarding the event payload. The full generic type is only visible in the CDI API Javadoc — not in any tutorials or common usage examples.

**See also:** [GE-20260423-daef97](GE-20260423-daef97.md) — CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync observers — fireAsync() required

**See also:** [GE-20260529-e43076](GE-20260529-e43076.md) — await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

entries.title: Canonical CloudEvent adapter pattern — 7 rules for CDI async adapters
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods
entries.body:
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

## Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

### Symptom

A `@Transactional` method fires a CDI event and also calls an internal dispatch (channel write, database write, queue push). When the internal dispatch throws — rate-limit rejection, policy violation, missing resource — the CDI event silently never fires. The external delivery path is dead with no error.

### Context

Any method that must make two independent deliveries inside a `@Transactional` boundary:
one via CDI `fireAsync()` (external observers, notification systems) and one via an internal
service call. The pattern appears in watchdog alerting, domain event publishing, and audit
side-channels.

### Root cause

Ordering. If `fireAsync()` is placed *after* the internal dispatch and dispatch throws, the
stack unwinds before `fireAsync()` is reached. The two paths are not independent — external
delivery is silently contingent on internal dispatch succeeding.

### Fix

Place `fireAsync()` as the first statement inside the method, before the internal dispatch:

```java
private void fireAlert(Watchdog w, String summary, AlertContext context, Instant now) {
    // External delivery FIRST — not contingent on internal channel success.
    // A rate-limited, paused, or policy-rejected dispatch must not suppress this.
    alertEvents.fireAsync(new WatchdogAlertEvent(
            w.id, w.targetName, w.notificationChannel, summary, now, context));

// Internal dispatch SECOND — failure does not suppress the event already fired.
    Optional<Channel> ch = channelService.findByName(w.notificationChannel);
    if (ch.isEmpty()) {
        return;
    }
    messageService.dispatch(MessageDispatch.builder()
            .channelId(ch.get().id)
            .sender("system:watchdog")
            .type(MessageType.STATUS)
            .content(summary)
            .build());
}
```

The CDI event fires unconditionally (after the guard checks). The internal dispatch is the
conditional secondary path.

### Accepted trade-off

If the outer `@Transactional` rolls back after `fireAsync()` dispatches, the observer runs
for a state change that never committed — a ghost notification. The window is narrow in
practice (only a post-dispatch interceptor or infrastructure failure causes an outer rollback).
For alerting systems: accepted — false-positive alert is better than missed alert. For audit
or ledger systems: not acceptable — use a transactional outbox pattern instead.

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI events feel like side effects. By convention, developers put them at the *end* of a method,
after "the main work" is done. When the event IS the primary delivery path — not a side effect
— this ordering must invert. The distinction only surfaces when the two paths need to be
genuinely independent rather than sequential. Without this insight, a failing internal dispatch
silently kills external delivery with no error or log entry.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-0fe012](GE-20260512-0fe012.md) — fireAsync() dispatches immediately inside @Transactional (the timing gotcha this technique builds on)

entries.title: ChatModel.doChat(ChatRequest) is the override point for test doubles — not chat(ChatRequest)
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it
entries.body:
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it

### What happened
A field validator banning BiDi control characters to prevent prompt injection implemented a standard check: reject codepoints 0–31 (C0), 127–159 (C1), U+200E/200F (LRM/RLM), U+202A–202E (LRE/RLE/PDF/LRO/RLO), and U+2066–2069 (LRI/RLI/FSI/PDI). Code review caught that U+061C was missing. The validator would have passed strings containing the Arabic Letter Mark.

### Root cause
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark, ALM) was added in Unicode 6.3 as a BiDi control character. It is in the Arabic block (U+0600–U+06FF), not in any standard control character range (C0: 0x00–0x1F, C1: 0x80–0x9F). It has no visible glyph and controls BiDi text direction.

Standard documentation and implementations of "ban control characters" focus on C0/C1 ranges. The dedicated BiDi control ranges (LRM/RLM/LRE etc.) are more widely known. U+061C predates none of these — it was simply added later and sits in an unexpected block.

### Fix
Add explicit check for U+061C alongside other BiDi controls:

```java
private static boolean isBanned(final int cp) {
    if (cp <= 0x001F) return true;                      // C0 control chars
    if (cp >= 0x007F && cp <= 0x009F) return true;     // DEL + C1 control chars
    if (cp == 0x061C) return true;                      // ALM — Arabic Letter Mark (BiDi, Unicode 6.3)
    if (cp == 0x200E || cp == 0x200F) return true;     // LRM, RLM
    if (cp >= 0x202A && cp <= 0x202E) return true;     // LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO
    if (cp >= 0x2066 && cp <= 0x2069) return true;     // LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI
    if (cp == 0x200B || cp == 0xFEFF) return true;     // ZWSP, BOM/ZWNBSP
    if (cp == 0x200C || cp == 0x200D) return true;     // ZWNJ, ZWJ
    if (cp == 0x2028 || cp == 0x2029) return true;     // LINE SEP, PARA SEP
    return false;
}
```

### Why this is non-obvious
The Unicode BiDi control characters everyone knows (LRM, RLM, LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO, LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI) are all in the General Punctuation block (U+2000–U+206F). U+061C is in the Arabic block. No standard "control character" resource bundles it with the others. The Unicode BiDi Algorithm spec mentions it, but security-focused implementations typically enumerate the well-known ranges without reading the full BiDi spec for outliers.

EndpointPropertyKeys.URL does not apply to KAFKA — only HTTP, GRPC, MCP, CAMEL, QHORUS
entries.body:
EndpointPropertyKeys.URL does not apply to KAFKA — only HTTP, GRPC, MCP, CAMEL, QHORUS

## Symptom

A `KAFKA` stream endpoint is registered in `EndpointRegistry` with `EndpointPropertyKeys.URL` set to the Kafka broker address. The property is silently stored and silently ignored — no component reads `URL` from a `KAFKA` descriptor.

## Context

When building stream endpoint descriptors for `EndpointRegistry`, the natural impulse is to set `EndpointPropertyKeys.URL` to the broker connection string (e.g. `broker:9092`). This seems consistent with how `URL` is used for HTTP and GRPC endpoints.

## Root cause

`EndpointPropertyKeys.URL` is explicitly scoped to specific protocols. Its Javadoc lists applicability:
- HTTP — service root URL
- GRPC — `host:port` or `grpc://` URI
- MCP — MCP server base URL
- CAMEL — Camel endpoint URI
- QHORUS — Qhorus REST base URL

**KAFKA is absent.** Furthermore, the class-level Javadoc states: "Deployment-local properties (bootstrap servers, TLS config, Camel route IDs) remain as module-defined keys and are not reserved." Bootstrap servers are explicitly classified as deployment-local, not registered in `EndpointPropertyKeys`.

## Fix

For `KAFKA` (and `AMQP`) stream endpoint descriptors, include only `TOPIC` and `STREAM_EVENT_TYPE` in properties. Kafka broker connection is configured via Quarkus standard config (`kafka.bootstrap.servers`) outside the EndpointDescriptor entirely.

```java
// WRONG — URL is ignored for KAFKA protocol
EndpointDescriptor wrong = new EndpointDescriptor(
    path, tenancyId, EndpointType.SYSTEM, EndpointProtocol.KAFKA,
    Map.of(
        EndpointPropertyKeys.URL, "broker:9092",   // silently ignored
        EndpointPropertyKeys.TOPIC, "patient.vitals",
        EndpointPropertyKeys.STREAM_EVENT_TYPE, "io.casehub.iot.temperature"
    ), null, Set.of(EndpointCapability.RECEIVE));

// CORRECT — only TOPIC and STREAM_EVENT_TYPE
EndpointDescriptor correct = new EndpointDescriptor(
    path, tenancyId, EndpointType.SYSTEM, EndpointProtocol.KAFKA,
    Map.of(
        EndpointPropertyKeys.TOPIC, "patient.vitals",
        EndpointPropertyKeys.STREAM_EVENT_TYPE, "io.casehub.iot.temperature"
    ), null, Set.of(EndpointCapability.RECEIVE));
```

## Why this is non-obvious

`URL` feels like a universal "where to connect" key. Every other protocol uses `URL` for the connection address. There is no validation or warning — the wrong key is accepted and stored without complaint. The only way to discover this is to read the Javadoc carefully and notice KAFKA is not in the applicability list.

**See also:** [GE-20260615-388a35](GE-20260615-388a35.md) — EndpointCapability.RECEIVE is wrong for poll streams — use QUERY

entries.title: `event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`
entries.body:
`event.fireAsync()` returns `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` not `CompletionStage<Void>` — Mutiny bridge needs `.replaceWith()`

### Symptom

Type mismatch when bridging `Event.fireAsync()` to a Mutiny `Uni` pipeline:

```java
// Fails — CompletionStage<Event<MyEvent>>, not CompletionStage<VerificationResult>
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(...)))
    // compiler error: cannot convert Uni<Event<MyEvent>> to Uni<VerificationResult>
```

### Context

Quarkus reactive service using CDI `Event<T>` needs to fire an async event and return a `Uni<SomeType>` from the same pipeline.

### Root cause

`Event.fireAsync()` is declared in the CDI spec as returning `CompletionStage<Event<T>>` — it returns the event object itself wrapped in a completion stage, not `CompletionStage<Void>`. Most usage examples ignore the return value entirely, hiding the actual type.

### Fix

Use `.replaceWith()` to discard the `Event<T>` wrapper and substitute the intended result:

```java
return Uni.createFrom().completionStage(
    () -> suspectEvent.fireAsync(new MyEvent(entryId, actorId, keyRef)))
    .replaceWith(VerificationResult.SUSPECT);
```

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI spec says "returns a CompletionStage" without emphasising the `<Event<T>>` type parameter. Developers writing async event firing naturally assume `CompletionStage<Void>` because they're triggering a side effect and discarding the event payload. The full generic type is only visible in the CDI API Javadoc — not in any tutorials or common usage examples.

**See also:** [GE-20260423-daef97](GE-20260423-daef97.md) — CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync observers — fireAsync() required

**See also:** [GE-20260529-e43076](GE-20260529-e43076.md) — await CDI fireAsync() delivery in @ConsumeEvent reactive chain

langchain4j-agentic DeclarativeUtil has a single CDI resolver hook for @ChatModelSupplier only — no equivalent for other supplier types
entries.body:
langchain4j-agentic DeclarativeUtil has a single CDI resolver hook for @ChatModelSupplier only — no equivalent for other supplier types

### What it is
`DeclarativeUtil` in `langchain4j-agentic` exposes a single static list `chatSupplierParameterResolvers` with one registration method `addChatSupplierParameterResolver(ChatSupplierParameterResolver)`. This resolver hook fires only during `@ChatModelSupplier` and `@StreamingChatModelSupplier` processing. All other supplier annotations (`@ToolsSupplier`, `@ChatMemorySupplier`, `@ChatMemoryProviderSupplier`, `@ContentRetrieverSupplier`, `@RetrievalAugmentorSupplier`, `@AgentListenerSupplier`, `@ToolProviderSupplier`) call `checkArguments(method)` which enforces **zero parameters** before invoking the static method directly — no resolver hook exists for these paths.

### Why this matters for framework integrators
Any DI framework (Quarkus, Spring, Guice) wanting to inject CDI/Spring beans into supplier methods is blocked for all supplier types except chat model suppliers. The `@CdiBean` workaround in `quarkus-langchain4j` only works for `@ChatModelSupplier` as a result. Users who need a CDI-managed `ContentRetriever` or `ChatMemory` must use `Arc.container()` or `ApplicationContext.getBean()` inside the static method body — bypassing injection, scope management, and testability.

### How to discover this
Only by decompiling `langchain4j-agentic-1.15.1-beta25.jar` and reading `DeclarativeUtil.class`. The library has no published Javadoc for internal classes and no documentation of the SPI surface area.

```bash
# Locate the JAR
find ~/.m2/repository/dev/langchain4j/langchain4j-agentic -name "*.jar" | grep -v sources | head -1

# The class structure reveals: addChatSupplierParameterResolver() is the only hook
# All other configureAgent() branches call checkArguments() then invokeStatic()
```

### Workaround
For Quarkus: use `SyntheticCreationalContext.getInjectedReference()` in the synthetic bean creator (`AgenticRecorder`) to inject CDI beans at agent construction time, bypassing the static supplier method entirely. This requires detecting at build time that no static supplier method exists for a given type, then adding a CDI injection point for that type.

### Upstream path
An upstream PR to `langchain4j-agentic` should propose a general `SupplierParameterResolver` SPI (not chat-model-specific) and add resolver hooks to all supplier type branches in `configureAgent()`. Frame as a DI-framework-agnostic improvement — Spring users would benefit from the same hook.

### Why this is non-obvious
The library name suggests platform independence (`langchain4j` not `langchain4j-spring`), so the assumption is that all supplier types are equally extensible. The asymmetry between `@ChatModelSupplier` (has a resolver hook) and all other supplier annotations (do not) is not mentioned in any README, Javadoc, or issue tracker. The class is package-private in the library with no stable API guarantee.

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: LangChain4j 1.x: ChatLanguageModel renamed to ChatModel; chat() replaced by doChat() as extension point
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

entries.title: @Alternative on a CDI bean silently suppresses all @DefaultBean beans of the same type — they vanish from the container entirely
quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension registers @Default ChatModel — clashes with @DefaultBean fallback
entries.body:
quarkus-langchain4j Ollama extension registers @Default ChatModel — clashes with @DefaultBean fallback

## Symptom

When the quarkus-langchain4j-ollama extension is on the classpath alongside a
`@DefaultBean ChatModel` implementation, Quarkus throws at startup:

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ChatModel and qualifiers [@Default]
  - CLASS bean [... AgentProviderChatModel] qualifiers=[@Any, @Default]
  - SYNTHETIC bean [... OllamaChatModel] qualifiers=[@Any, @Default]
```

## Context

A custom `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped ChatModel` implementation exists to serve as a
fallback (e.g. a Claude CLI bridge). The expectation is that adding the Ollama extension
places its `ChatModel` higher-priority, which should silence the `@DefaultBean`. Instead,
both are registered with `@Default` and Quarkus cannot choose between them.

## Root cause

`@DefaultBean` in Quarkus ARC means: "this bean is only active if no other `@Default`-
qualified bean of the same type is present." The mechanism works when the competing bean
is `@ApplicationScoped` with no explicit qualifier (which implicitly gets `@Default`), or
when the competing bean is annotated with `@Alternative @Priority(N)`. However, the
quarkus-langchain4j-ollama extension registers its model as a **SYNTHETIC bean with an
explicit `@Default` qualifier** — not as a higher-priority alternative. ARC sees two
explicit `@Default` beans and cannot resolve.

## Fix

Veto the `@DefaultBean` explicitly when the Ollama extension is active by pairing
`@IfBuildProperty` with `-Dcasehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled=false` at build time:

```java
@DefaultBean
@ApplicationScoped
@IfBuildProperty(name = "casehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled",
                 stringValue = "true", enableIfMissing = true)
class AgentProviderChatModel implements ChatModel { ... }
```

```bash
# Run with Ollama — veto the DefaultBean
mvn clean test -Peval,eval-ollama \
  -Dcasehub.eval.claude-provider.enabled=false \
  ...
```

`mvn clean` is required to force fresh augmentation when toggling this property (see
related entry on augmentation caching).

## Why this is non-obvious

`@DefaultBean` is marketed as "the bean that loses to any other bean of the same type."
In practice it only loses to beans that don't also carry an explicit `@Default` qualifier.
Quarkus LangChain4j provider extensions register SYNTHETIC beans with explicit `@Default`
rather than `@Alternative @Priority`, making the clash non-obvious. The `@DefaultBean`
javadoc does not describe this failure mode.

**See also:** [GE-20260601-fcf0d9](GE-20260601-fcf0d9.md) — two @DefaultBean beans for the same type → AmbiguousResolutionException | [GE-20260531-1ec900](GE-20260531-1ec900.md) — Quarkus ARC registers non-CDI abstract class as @Dependent bean

entries.title: LangChain4j EmbeddingModel does not implement TokenCountEstimator — use character-based DocumentSplitters.recursive(int, int)
entries.body:
LangChain4j EmbeddingModel does not implement TokenCountEstimator — use character-based DocumentSplitters.recursive(int, int)

`DocumentSplitters.recursive(int, int, TokenCountEstimator)` requires a `TokenCountEstimator` as the third argument for token-based segment sizing. `EmbeddingModel` does NOT implement `TokenCountEstimator` — they are separate interface hierarchies with no inheritance relationship.

### Symptom

Design spec or implementation assumes `EmbeddingModel` can be passed as the `TokenCountEstimator` argument to `DocumentSplitters.recursive()`. Compilation fails with "incompatible types."

### Context

When building a RAG chunking pipeline, the natural assumption is: "I already have an `EmbeddingModel` that handles tokenization for embeddings — it should work as a `TokenCountEstimator` for splitting too." The naming and domain proximity reinforce this assumption.

### Root cause

`EmbeddingModel` and `TokenCountEstimator` are independent interfaces in LangChain4j. `EmbeddingModel` knows how to embed text into vectors. `TokenCountEstimator` knows how to count tokens in text. Some LLM provider implementations (e.g. `OpenAiEmbeddingModel`) implement both, but the base `EmbeddingModel` interface does not extend `TokenCountEstimator`. ONNX-based models (`OnnxEmbeddingModel`) implement only `EmbeddingModel`.

### Fix

Use the character-based 2-arg overload:

```java
DocumentSplitter splitter = DocumentSplitters.recursive(1000, 200);
```

This splits by character count (paragraph → line → sentence → word fallback chain). Character counts are approximate by nature — the imprecision vs token counts is negligible for RAG chunk sizing.

### Why this is non-obvious

The `EmbeddingModel` interface handles tokenization internally (it must tokenize text before embedding it). A developer reasonably infers it exposes this capability via `TokenCountEstimator`. The class names reinforce the assumption: "Embedding" + "Model" + "Token" + "Count" — all in the same domain. The actual type hierarchy has no inheritance link between them.

### Alternatives considered

- Implementing a custom `TokenCountEstimator` that delegates to the embedding model's internal tokenizer — rejected because ONNX embedding models don't expose their tokenizer publicly
- Using HuggingFace Tokenizers JNI directly as a `TokenCountEstimator` — possible but adds JNI complexity for marginal accuracy improvement over character-based splitting

entries.title: GraalVM tracing agent as sole discovery path for ONNX Runtime + DJL native-image config
entries.body:
GraalVM tracing agent as sole discovery path for ONNX Runtime + DJL native-image config

### The technique
When building GraalVM native images with JNI libraries that have no upstream reachability metadata, the tracing agent in JVM mode is the only viable discovery path for the complete config. No manual inspection of source code or documentation can reliably produce the full set of JNI, reflection, and resource entries.

### How to apply

Run the application in JVM mode with the tracing agent:

```bash
java -agentlib:native-image-agent=config-output-dir=target/agent-config \
  -jar target/quarkus-app/quarkus-run.jar \
  <application-args>
```

Exercise all code paths that use JNI. The agent generates `reachability-metadata.json` capturing every reflection, JNI, and resource access. Copy the output to `src/main/resources/META-INF/native-image/<group>/<artifact>/`.

### Why this is non-obvious
The intuitive approach is to inspect library source for `native` method declarations, `System.loadLibrary()` calls, and reflection usage — then manually register each one. This fails because:

1. **Rust→Java JNI callbacks are invisible** — DJL's tokenizer has Rust code that calls `JNIEnv::NewObject` to create Java objects. These callbacks don't appear in Java source at all.
2. **JNA bridge classes are implicit** — DJL uses JNA, which has its own JNI surface (`com.sun.jna.*`) that must be registered. Dozens of JNA classes need JNI access.
3. **Transitive resource dependencies** — ONNX Runtime bundles multiple `.dylib` files (core, JNI bridge, providers, WebGPU) that all need resource registration.
4. **No upstream metadata exists** — as of June 2026, ONNX Runtime, DJL, LangChain4j, quarkiverse/quarkus-langchain4j, and the GraalVM reachability metadata repo all lack native-image config for these libraries.

### What developers would normally do instead
Manually inspect source, write JNI/reflection config by hand, and iterate on build failures. This works for simple libraries but misses Rust→Java callbacks and implicit JNA requirements, leading to hours of debugging opaque runtime failures (Rust panics, UnsatisfiedLinkError) with no clear diagnostic.

**See also:** [GE-20260606-025601](GE-20260606-025601.md) — ONNX Runtime --initialize-at-run-time must be package-level (the specific gotcha this technique addresses) | [GE-20260606-aab62a](GE-20260606-aab62a.md) — DJL 0.36.0 ships partial native-image metadata | [GE-20260606-7bf5dd](GE-20260606-7bf5dd.md) — DJL CharSpan JNI callback causes Rust panic in GraalVM native image

entries.title: quarkus-langchain4j-core Quarkus extension stalls @QuarkusTest augmentation when no model provider is configured
entries.body:
quarkus-langchain4j-core Quarkus extension stalls @QuarkusTest augmentation when no model provider is configured

## Symptom

`mvn test` hangs indefinitely during Quarkus augmentation when `quarkus-langchain4j-core` is a compile-scope dependency of a non-extension library module and no AI model provider is configured. No error message, no timeout — the build just blocks.

## Context

Occurs when a library module (Jandex-indexed, not a full Quarkus extension) adds `io.quarkiverse.langchain4j:quarkus-langchain4j-core` as a compile dependency to use the `EmbeddingModel` interface. During `@QuarkusTest` augmentation, the Quarkus extension activates and waits for a model provider that never arrives.

## What was tried

Adding `quarkus-langchain4j-core` as a `provided` scope — still stalls because the extension's deployment artifact is resolved transitively during augmentation.

## Root cause

`quarkus-langchain4j-core` is a Quarkus extension (has both runtime and deployment jars). When it appears on the augmentation classpath, Quarkus activates it and expects at least one model provider (OpenAI, Ollama, etc.) to be configured. Without one, augmentation stalls waiting for the CDI bean to resolve.

## Fix

Use `dev.langchain4j:langchain4j-core` (the plain Java library, **not** the Quarkus extension) as the compile dependency. This provides the `EmbeddingModel` interface for compilation without triggering Quarkus extension activation:

```xml
<dependency>
  <groupId>dev.langchain4j</groupId>
  <artifactId>langchain4j-core</artifactId>
  <version>1.13.0</version>
</dependency>
```

For CDI injection, use `Instance<EmbeddingModel>` instead of `@Inject EmbeddingModel`:

```java
@Inject
public MyBean(final Instance<EmbeddingModel> modelInstance) {
    this.model = modelInstance.isResolvable() ? modelInstance.get() : null;
}
```

When no provider is configured, `instance.isResolvable()` returns `false` gracefully. The consuming app brings `quarkus-langchain4j-openai` (or any other provider extension), which satisfies the CDI bean at their own augmentation time.

## Why this is non-obvious

The symptom is a hang with no error — not a compilation failure or a clear exception. The natural instinct is to add the Quarkus extension for its CDI integration, but the correct separation is: library module uses the plain Java API, consuming application brings the Quarkus extension.

### Why this fix

The plain Java `langchain4j-core` provides the same `EmbeddingModel` interface without triggering extension activation. `Instance<EmbeddingModel>` makes the dependency optional at CDI resolution time, so the library works both with and without a configured provider.

**See also:** GE-20260422-3242bf — Use Instance<T> for optional CDI injection — resolves gracefully when no bean exists

### Alternatives considered

- `provided` scope: still triggers augmentation because the extension's deployment artifact is resolved transitively
- Mocking `EmbeddingModel` via `@InjectMock` in every `@QuarkusTest`: works but requires all existing tests to be updated

entries.title: LangChain4j Anthropic: ResponseFormat.JSON without schema throws UnsupportedFeatureException — not a degradation
entries.body:
LangChain4j Anthropic: ResponseFormat.JSON without schema throws UnsupportedFeatureException — not a degradation

## Symptom

Passing `ResponseFormat.JSON` (type only, no schema) to `ChatRequest.builder().responseFormat(...)` for an Anthropic-backed `ChatModel` throws `UnsupportedFeatureException` rather than silently degrading or prompting.

## Context

`InternalAnthropicHelper.validate(ChatRequestParameters)` is called inside `AnthropicChatModel.doChat()` before any API call is made. The validation checks:

```java
if (parameters.responseFormat() != null
        && parameters.responseFormat().type() == ResponseFormatType.JSON
        && parameters.responseFormat().jsonSchema() == null) {
    throw new UnsupportedFeatureException(...);
}
```

This means JSON mode without a schema is explicitly rejected at the LangChain4j layer — it never reaches the Anthropic API.

## Root cause

Anthropic's native API requires a JSON schema to enforce structured output. Unlike OpenAI (which supports a bare JSON mode that produces valid but unstructured JSON), Anthropic has no schema-less JSON mode. LangChain4j's Anthropic integration reflects this correctly by refusing the schemaless request rather than silently ignoring the constraint.

## Fix

Always pair `ResponseFormat.JSON` with a `JsonSchema` when using Anthropic:

```java
ResponseFormat responseFormat = ResponseFormat.builder()
    .type(ResponseFormatType.JSON)
    .jsonSchema(JsonSchema.builder()
        .name("MySchema")
        .rootElement(JsonObjectSchema.builder()
            .addStringProperty("field", "Description")
            .required("field")
            .build())
        .build())
    .build();
```

For a broad catch on provider-specific failures (including older Claude models that don't support structured output at all), wrap the `llm.chat(request)` call in `catch (Exception e)` and degrade gracefully:

```java
try {
    ChatResponse response = llm.chat(request);
    return Optional.of(parse(response.aiMessage().text()));
} catch (Exception e) {
    log.warn("Structured enrichment failed ({}), falling back to structural rendering", e.getMessage());
    return Optional.empty();
}
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The LangChain4j docs describe `ResponseFormat.JSON` as a valid type value. There is no public documentation on which providers support it with or without a schema. The `UnsupportedFeatureException` is thrown before any network call, so there is no HTTP error to debug — it looks like an in-process configuration error with no obvious connection to the Anthropic API's structured output requirements.

The `supportedCapabilities()` mechanism exists on `ChatModel` for high-level AI Services to check before using JSON schema, but `ClaudeMarkdownRenderer`-style code calling the low-level `ChatModel.chat(ChatRequest)` API bypasses that guard entirely.

### Why this fix

`ResponseFormat.JSON` with `JsonSchema` is the only valid combination for Anthropic. Adding a schema is not optional — it is the required API surface.

entries.title: Panache: use getEntityManager().createQuery(..., UUID.class) for typed single-column projections instead of full entity load
entries.body:
Panache: use getEntityManager().createQuery(..., UUID.class) for typed single-column projections instead of full entity load

When a Panache query only needs entity IDs (or any single column), avoid loading full entity rows by using `SomeEntity.getEntityManager().createQuery(..., UUID.class)`. This bypasses Panache's entity materialisation and returns a typed `List<UUID>` directly.

### Context

Panache's `find(...).list()` always materialises full entities. For bulk ID queries (e.g. a scheduler fetching eligible IDs to process), this loads all columns for every row — wasteful when only the ID is needed.

### Technique

```java
// Before — loads full entities, maps to IDs in memory
@Transactional
List<UUID> findEligibleIds(Instant now, int limit) {
    return SponsorNotification
            .<SponsorNotification>find(
                    "status in (?1, ?2) and (nextRetryAfter is null or nextRetryAfter <= ?3)",
                    PENDING, FAILED, now)
            .page(0, limit)
            .list()
            .stream()
            .map(n -> n.id)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

// After — JPQL projection, fetches only the id column
@Transactional
List<UUID> findEligibleIds(Instant now, int limit) {
    return SponsorNotification.getEntityManager()
            .createQuery(
                    "SELECT n.id FROM SponsorNotification n"
                    + " WHERE n.status IN (:s1, :s2)"
                    + " AND (n.nextRetryAfter IS NULL OR n.nextRetryAfter <= :now)",
                    UUID.class)
            .setParameter("s1", PENDING)
            .setParameter("s2", FAILED)
            .setParameter("now", now)
            .setMaxResults(limit)
            .getResultList();
}
```

### Why this is non-obvious

Panache's fluent API (`find`, `list`, `stream`) are the natural entry points. `getEntityManager()` is available as a static method on every Active Record entity but is not visible from Panache's fluent API surface. Most developers reach for `.list()` + stream mapping without knowing the JPA escape hatch exists.

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently
entries.body:
Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently

## Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores — returns empty results silently

### Symptom

Test suite activates `@Alternative @Priority(1)` InMemory stores via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives`. Panache static queries (`Entity.<Entity>listAll()`, `Entity.find(...)`) return zero results with no error, even though data was inserted through the store.

### Root cause

Panache static methods resolve directly against JPA at the persistence layer, bypassing CDI entirely. `@Alternative` implementations intercept calls made through injected CDI interfaces, not through static Panache entry points. With InMemory stores active, the JPA entity tables are empty — Panache statics query empty tables and return zero results silently.

### Fix

In test code that activates InMemory stores, never use Panache statics to query domain entities. Always go through the injected store or service layer:

```java
// Wrong — bypasses CDI alternative, queries empty JPA table
List<Channel> channels = Channel.<Channel>listAll();

// Correct — routes through the injected alternative store
List<Channel> channels = channelStore.findAll();
// or via service/tool layer
var result = tools.listChannels();
```

### Why this is non-obvious

Panache statics look like repository methods and feel like they should respect CDI alternatives — but they don't. No error is thrown, no warning is logged; the query simply returns an empty collection. The only signal is silently wrong test data.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates without quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives

**See also:** [GE-20260519-f33c66](GE-20260519-f33c66.md) — 'Reactive' service classes may use Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative doesn't intercept

**See also:** [GE-20260528-3d3847](GE-20260528-3d3847.md) — @Transactional on @BeforeAll static methods has no effect (same CDI proxy limitation)

**See also:** [GE-20260608-d223af](GE-20260608-d223af.md) — REST Assured GPath $.findAll returns null (not []) — Hamcrest not(empty()) silently passes on null

**See also:** [GE-20260609-49bd08](GE-20260609-49bd08.md) — static Panache entity calls bypass injected store tenant filtering

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives

## Symptom

Test assertions calling `workItemStore.scanAll()` return empty even though the handler log confirms `WorkItemService.create()` ran successfully. `planItem.getStatus()` is `RUNNING` (proving the handler completed), but the store shows nothing.

## Context

Two `WorkItemStore` beans on the test classpath:
- `JpaWorkItemStore @ApplicationScoped` — production bean from `casehub-work`
- `InMemoryWorkItemStore @Alternative @Priority(1)` — test replacement from `casehub-work-testing`

CDI 4.x spec says `@Priority` on an alternative activates it globally and it should win over non-alternatives. In Quarkus ARC 3.32.2 this does not hold when the alternative comes from an external dependency JAR.

## What was tried

- Adding `InMemoryWorkItemStore` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` alone → "Ambiguous dependencies" deployment failure (both beans active simultaneously)
- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore` alone → no effect; `JpaWorkItemStore` still wins
- `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore workItemStore` (concrete type) in the test → bypasses CDI alternative selection; test clears the right store but `WorkItemService` still writes to JPA (H2), so `scanAll()` returns empty

## Root Cause

`@Alternative @Priority(1)` from a dependency JAR is not treated as overriding a non-alternative `@ApplicationScoped` bean by Quarkus ARC. CDI spec §2.6.2 requires `@Priority` alternatives to take precedence, but ARC's resolution appears to treat both as equally active candidates when neither is the local application class. The non-alternative wins or ambiguity is raised.

## Fix

Combine both in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
# Remove the JPA impl from CDI discovery entirely
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.work.runtime.repository.jpa.JpaWorkItemStore

# Explicitly select the in-memory replacement
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.work.testing.InMemoryWorkItemStore,\
  ...other alternatives...
```

With `JpaWorkItemStore` excluded, `InMemoryWorkItemStore` is the only active `WorkItemStore` bean. Explicit `selected-alternatives` listing then activates it. Both `WorkItemService` (via `@Inject WorkItemStore`) and the test (via `@Inject InMemoryWorkItemStore`) now reference the same singleton, and `scanAll()` finds what `create()` stored.

## Why this is non-obvious

CDI 4.x spec (and Quarkus docs on alternatives) state that `@Alternative @Priority(n)` globally activates and prioritises the alternative. A developer familiar with CDI 4.x would expect `@Priority(1)` to simply work. The failure is silent — no warning is emitted, `JpaWorkItemStore` just wins quietly. The symptom (empty `scanAll()`) points toward a different root cause (session caching, test isolation) and sends debugging in the wrong direction.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-a79d0e](GE-20260429-a79d0e.md) — @Alternative @Priority auto-activates within same module | [GE-20260521-4de4f1](GE-20260521-4de4f1.md) — TestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-cee623](GE-20260601-cee623.md) — QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely

**See also:** [GE-20260601-848232](GE-20260601-848232.md) — exclude-types silently does nothing for beans from Jandex-indexed JARs

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean

**See also:** [GE-20260616-d70e7e](GE-20260616-d70e7e.md) — selected-alternatives silently accepts non-@Alternative beans

entries.title: TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE
entries.body:
TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE

## Context

Writing Flyway migrations for a Quarkus module that uses PostgreSQL in production and H2
with `MODE=PostgreSQL` in `@QuarkusTest`. The migration uses `TIMESTAMPTZ` (PostgreSQL's
abbreviated type name for `TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE`).

## Symptom

Flyway migration fails during `@QuarkusTest` startup with a SQL syntax error even though
`MODE=PostgreSQL` is configured. Production (PostgreSQL) works fine.

## Root Cause

`TIMESTAMPTZ` is a PostgreSQL-specific abbreviation. H2 implements only a subset of
PostgreSQL syntax in `MODE=PostgreSQL` — it recognises the full form
`TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE` but not the abbreviated `TIMESTAMPTZ`.

## Fix

Use the full form in Flyway migrations:

```sql
-- Fails in H2 even with MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,

-- Works in both PostgreSQL and H2 MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE NOT NULL,
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

PostgreSQL documentation and tooling widely use `TIMESTAMPTZ` — it appears in pg_dump
output, official PostgreSQL tutorials, and StackOverflow answers. `MODE=PostgreSQL` in H2
creates an expectation that PostgreSQL-idiomatic SQL will work. The error message points
to a SQL syntax failure, not to a type-name unsupported issue, making diagnosis indirect.

**See also:** [GE-20260603-86f2a9](GE-20260603-86f2a9.md) — H2 does not support INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING | [GE-20260605-0ffc19](GE-20260605-0ffc19.md) — H2 does not support SIMILAR TO in CHECK constraints

**See also:** [GE-20260617-03c1a3](GE-20260617-03c1a3.md) — H2 MODE=PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT syntax limitations

entries.title: casehub-qhorus MessageService methods using Panache static calls bypass InMemory store in @QuarkusTest
'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths
entries.body:
'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths

### What is undocumented

A service class annotated `@Alternative` and named with "Reactive" may internally use a `blockingService` field (injected blocking CDI service) for write operations and `Entity.find()` (a Panache static) for read operations. CDI `@Alternative @Priority(1)` in-memory stores only intercept at CDI injection points — Panache statics go directly to the underlying database regardless of what alternatives are selected.

### Context

In Quarkus Qhorus (casehubio/qhorus), `ReactiveQhorusMcpTools`:
- `sendMessage()` delegates internally to `blockingMessageService` → `MessageService.send()` → `messageStore.put()` (uses the `MessageStore` CDI alternative, i.e. `InMemoryMessageStore` in tests)
- `getChannelTimeline()` delegates to `blockingGetChannelTimeline()` → `Message.<Message>find(...)` (Panache static — queries H2 directly, bypasses `MessageStore` entirely)

Result: messages written by `sendMessage()` land in `InMemoryMessageStore` (in-memory). Messages read by `getChannelTimeline()` come from H2 (empty in tests). The two paths never share data.

### Discovery

Only visible by reading the service implementation source. The class name and method signatures suggest a uniform reactive abstraction. Not mentioned in any documentation.

### Impact on tests

Tests that call `sendMessage()` then verify the timeline via `getChannelTimeline()` always see an empty timeline in test setups using `@Alternative` in-memory stores, even though the message was "successfully" posted.

### Fix / Workaround

Identify the actual data path for each operation you need to test:
1. Check which CDI service field the MCP tool method delegates to
2. Check whether that service uses CDI store injection or Panache statics
3. For end-to-end write→read tests: use the real H2 database (don't rely on in-memory stores for both sides)

Alternatively, file an upstream issue to make the read path use the same abstraction as the write path.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-66d997](GE-20260512-66d997.md) — Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores

entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(1) from an external JAR does not override a non-alternative bean in Quarkus — needs exclude-types + selected-alternatives

## Symptom

entries.title: casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore
entries.body:
casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore

### Symptom

Quarkus startup fails with:

```
jakarta.enterprise.inject.spi.DeploymentException: Found 2 deployment problems:
[1] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseExecutionHandler constructor
[2] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseCompletionService constructor
```

### Context

When a CaseHub harness adds `casehub-engine-blackboard` (required for SubCase lifecycle and M-of-N quorum) alongside `casehub-engine-persistence-memory`, the blackboard beans (`SubCaseExecutionHandler`, `SubCaseCompletionService`) need `SubCaseGroupRepository`. The in-memory implementation (`MemorySubCaseGroupRepository`) exists in persistence-memory but is `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — CDI does not activate `@Alternative` beans unless explicitly selected.

The same applies to `MemoryPlanItemStore` and `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore` from the same module.

### What was tried (didn't work)

- Adding Jandex index entries for persistence-memory — beans are discovered but not activated (they're @Alternative)
- Assuming engine-testing would provide @Priority(1) overrides — engine-testing has TestCaseMetaModelRepository, TestCaseInstanceRepository, TestEventLogRepository but NOT TestSubCaseGroupRepository or TestPlanItemStore

### Root cause

`casehub-engine-persistence-memory` ships all repository implementations as `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — they require explicit `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` to be activated. Engine-testing provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for CaseInstanceRepository and EventLogRepository but not for SubCaseGroupRepository or PlanItemStore.

### Fix

Add to `application.properties` (test or production):

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemorySubCaseGroupRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryPlanItemStore,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Append to existing `selected-alternatives` if other beans (e.g. `JpaLedgerEntryRepository`) are already listed.

### Why this is non-obvious

The error names `SubCaseGroupRepository` — but `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` IS on the classpath and IS Jandex-indexed. The CDI error doesn't mention that the bean exists but is an unselected `@Alternative`. You have to read the source of `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` to discover the `@Alternative` annotation, then check whether engine-testing provides an override (it doesn't for these SPIs).

## Variant — Runtime failure from implicit activation of remaining @Alternative beans

### Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` starts cleanly (no DeploymentException) because `casehub-engine-testing` provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for `CaseInstanceRepository` and `EventLogRepository`. But the first `startCase()` call in certain test classes fails with:

```
(RECIPIENT_FAILURE,8191) CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant: <uuid>
```

The error comes from `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository.update()` (persistence-memory) — the bean is implicitly activated by Quarkus Arc as the sole `@Alternative` without a competing non-alternative, but the activation path produces intermittent initialization-order failures.

### Context

When only `SubCaseGroupRepository` and `PlanItemStore` are in `selected-alternatives`, the remaining 4 `@Alternative` beans from persistence-memory rely on Arc's implicit fallback activation:
- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository`
- `InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository`
- `InMemoryEventLogRepository`
- `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore`

This implicit activation works for most test classes but fails intermittently when the test class is among the first to trigger `startCase()` in a fresh JVM — the `CaseStartedEventHandler` calls `caseInstanceRepository.update()` and the instance isn't found.

### Fix

Add all 4 remaining persistence-memory `@Alternative` beans to `selected-alternatives`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  ...,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryEventLogRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Verified in casehub-aml#58: 4 of 6 failing test classes fixed by explicit selection. 2 remaining failures (`AmlLayer7ResourceTest`, `AmlTrustRoutingAttestationTest`) may have a deeper root cause — investigation ongoing.

entries.title: CDI StartupEvent @Priority ordering: no-priority observer fires at 2500, after @Priority(10) — not before
entries.body:
CDI StartupEvent @Priority ordering: no-priority observer fires at 2500, after @Priority(10) — not before

## Symptom

A `@Observes StartupEvent` method with no `@Priority` annotation appears to run *before* another observer annotated with `@Priority(10)`. In reality, the opposite is true: the undecorated observer fires at the CDI default priority (2500), which is *much later* than `@Priority(10)`.

The bug manifests as a startup workaround that "seems to work" because the system falls back to a sensible default — masking the fact that the CDI-injected value never reached its target in time.

**Real case:** `ObjectMapperInjector @Observes StartupEvent` (no @Priority) was meant to inject a CDI-managed `ObjectMapper` into a static field on `CaseDefinitionYamlMapper` before case definitions loaded. `DefaultCaseDefinitionRegistry.onStart(@Priority(10))` loaded definitions eagerly. The injector fired at priority 2500 — *after* the registry — so the CDI ObjectMapper never participated in definition loading. The fallback `new ObjectMapper(new YAMLFactory())` worked well enough that the bug went unnoticed for months.

## Root cause

CDI event observer priority follows the same semantics as Java interceptor priority: **lower number = higher priority = fires first**. CDI 4.0 specifies the default observer priority as `javax.interceptor.Interceptor.Priority.APPLICATION + 500 = 2500`.

- `@Priority(10)` → fires very early (almost first)
- No `@Priority` → fires at 2500 (application-tier default)

A developer expecting "no annotation = fires in some undefined order near the observer" will be surprised that it always fires *after* any explicitly-prioritised observer.

## Fix

Either:

1. Give the startup injector an explicit `@Priority` lower than any observer it must run before:
   ```java
   void onStartup(@Observes @Priority(1) StartupEvent event) { ... }
   ```

2. Eliminate the static injection pattern entirely. If the observer's target is a CDI bean, inject the dependency directly rather than via a static setter:
   ```java
   @Inject @YamlMapper ObjectMapper objectMapper; // injected before any StartupEvent fires
   ```
   CDI field injection completes *before* any `StartupEvent` observers fire, regardless of priority.

## Why this is non-obvious

The natural reading of "no @Priority = unordered" aligns with how many other frameworks handle default ordering. CDI's explicit default of 2500 is buried in the spec and not surfaced by Quarkus tooling. A static setter that "works" despite wrong ordering hides the bug indefinitely — the fallback default happens to be functionally acceptable.

### Why this fix

Option 2 (direct CDI injection) is the correct design because it removes the static mutable state entirely. Option 1 merely papers over the ordering problem without fixing the architectural smell.

**See also:** [GE-20260522-5685ba](GE-20260522-5685ba.md) — synchronous CDI Event.fire() in @Observes StartupEvent propagates observer exceptions

entries.title: @IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs
entries.body:
@IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs

### Symptom

Adding a dev-profile-only CDI bean (e.g., a `CurrentPrincipal` for dev mode) via `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` causes startup failures — all beans from the non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` list disappear.

### Context

Quarkus profile-specific properties (`%dev.`, `%test.`, `%prod.`) completely **replace** their non-profiled counterparts. If the base `application.properties` has:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  com.example.JpaRepo,\
  com.example.JpaPlanItemStore,\
  com.example.JpaSubCaseGroupRepo
```

Adding `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` drops all three JPA alternatives in dev mode — CDI startup fails with unsatisfied dependency errors for every bean that was in the original list.

### Root cause

Profile-specific properties in Quarkus are full replacements, not merges. This is by design (SmallRye Config behaviour), but the failure mode is silent — no warning that the non-profiled value was dropped.

### Fix

Use bean-level annotations instead of `selected-alternatives`:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(150)  // beats OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)
@IfBuildProfile("dev")
public class DemoCurrentPrincipal implements CurrentPrincipal {
    @Override public String tenancyId() { return "demo-tenant"; }
    @Override public String actorId()   { return "demo-user"; }
}
```

**Requirements:**
1. The bean must live in a **Jandex-indexed JAR** (e.g., the application's own `runtime/` module). ArC discovers it automatically — no `selected-alternatives` entry needed.
2. `@IfBuildProfile("dev")` excludes the bean entirely from non-dev builds — no ambiguity in production.
3. `@Priority(150)` resolves over competing `@Alternative` beans (e.g., `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)`) via standard ArC priority resolution.

This is the same mechanism by which `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Alternative @Priority(100)` beats `MockCurrentPrincipal @DefaultBean` in production — without being listed in `selected-alternatives`.

### Why this is non-obvious

The natural instinct is `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=MyDevBean` — it looks like the standard profile-override pattern. The replacement behaviour is documented for SmallRye Config but not prominently flagged in the Quarkus CDI guide. The failure (dropped JPA alternatives) manifests as unrelated `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` errors that don't mention `selected-alternatives` at all.

### Why this fix

`@IfBuildProfile + @Priority` solves the problem at the bean level — no configuration property needed. The alternative (duplicating the full `selected-alternatives` list in every profile override) is fragile and drifts as new alternatives are added.

### Alternatives considered

- **Duplicate the full list in `%dev.`** — works but fragile; every new `@Alternative` bean must be added to every profile-specific override. Drifts silently.
- **Use `@UnlessBuildProfile("prod")` instead of `@IfBuildProfile("dev")`** — activates in both dev and test profiles, which may not be desired. Test profile has its own `FixedCurrentPrincipal @Priority(200)`.

entries.title: quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives default priority 1 creates tie with @Priority(1) subclass beans — two @ApplicationScoped instances with separate state
entries.body:
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives default priority 1 creates tie with @Priority(1) subclass beans — two @ApplicationScoped instances with separate state

### Symptom
`CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant` (or similar state-not-found error) despite the instance being saved moments earlier. The save and lookup use the same `CaseInstanceRepository` interface, but they resolve to different `@ApplicationScoped` bean instances with separate `ConcurrentHashMap` stores.

### Context
`casehub-engine-testing` provides `TestCaseInstanceRepository extends InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` with `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped`. This is the standard test harness — it auto-activates without `selected-alternatives`.

A consumer project adds `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` to `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in test `application.properties`, thinking it's redundant but harmless.

### What was tried (didn't work)
- Checking event bus threading and Vert.x serialization — object identity was intact
- Verifying `currentPrincipal.tenancyId()` consistency — MockCurrentPrincipal returns a constant
- Adding more beans to `selected-alternatives` — made it worse

### Root cause
Quarkus Arc assigns a **default priority of 1** to alternatives listed in `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` that don't have `@Priority`. This creates a priority tie:

- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository` — priority 1 (from `selected-alternatives` default)
- `TestCaseInstanceRepository` — priority 1 (from `@Priority(1)`)

Both are `@ApplicationScoped`, so CDI creates **two separate bean instances**. Each has its own `ConcurrentHashMap<UUID, CaseInstance> store`. If CDI resolves different injection points to different beans (or resolves non-deterministically), saves go to one store and lookups to another.

### Fix
Do not add parent classes to `selected-alternatives` when the engine-testing module already provides `@Priority(1)` subclasses:

```properties
# WRONG — creates priority tie with TestCaseInstanceRepository
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=...,io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository

# RIGHT — let engine-testing's @Priority(1) beans handle it
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=...
# (omit InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository, InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository, InMemoryEventLogRepository)
```

The engine-testing module exists specifically to provide auto-activating alternatives for test contexts. Adding the parent classes to `selected-alternatives` defeats the purpose.

### Why this is non-obvious
Adding a class to `selected-alternatives` feels like an explicit, high-confidence activation — surely it would "win" over a `@Priority(1)` subclass. But the default priority is also 1, creating an exact tie. The Quarkus docs mention the default priority but don't warn about ties with existing `@Priority(1)` alternatives on the classpath. The symptom (state not found) appears as a threading or serialization bug, not a CDI resolution issue.

entries.title: Three-tier Maven module structure — api/common/runtime
entries.body:
Three-tier Maven module structure — api/common/runtime

## Three-tier Maven module structure — api/common/runtime

**Topic:** Multi-module Maven structure for Quarkus extensions
**Alternatives exist:** yes — flat single-module, two-tier api/runtime, monolithic runtime

### The convention

Three tiers, ordered lightest to heaviest:

**Tier 1 — `api/` (pure Java):** Interfaces, records, enums, POJOs. No Quarkus, no CDI, no JPA. Any Java app can consume this tier regardless of framework.

**Tier 2 — `common/` or `core/` (CDI, no JPA):** Shared helpers and CDI beans. No JPA entities, no Flyway migrations, no datasource dependency.

**Tier 3 — `runtime/`, `engine/`, `app/` (full Quarkus):** JPA entities, Flyway migrations, REST resources, Quarkus config — everything.

Dependency direction: Tier 3 → Tier 2 → Tier 1. Never reversed.

### Why this style

JPA entities force every consumer to configure a datasource. CDI annotations in a Tier 1 module force all consumers to have Quarkus. Separating the three tiers means lightweight consumers (other pure-Java libs, tests using in-memory repos) can depend only on `api/` with zero runtime overhead and no datasource requirement.

### Trade-offs

More modules to navigate. SPIs that genuinely need external SDK types (Vert.x, Hibernate Reactive) must live in a tier that already depends on that SDK — this sometimes surprises contributors who add Quarkus types to `api/` then wonder why consumers break.

### When not to use it

Single-module projects or projects where all consumers are full Quarkus apps and a datasource-in-tests is acceptable across the board.

*Score: 12/15 · Included because: JPA/CDI contamination across module tiers is non-obvious; the three-tier boundary makes it structural rather than disciplinary · Reservation: none*

**See also:** [GE-20260515-ffde26](GE-20260515-ffde26.md) — optional Quarkus features: Jandex library module pattern

**See also:** [GE-20260501-b88737](GE-20260501-b88737.md) — parallel SPI interfaces in api/ and runtime/ diverge silently
