@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

## @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

### Stack
Quarkus 3.31.1, CDI 4.0

### Symptom
Multiple beans implement the same SPI interface. Each alternative is annotated with
`@Alternative @Priority(10)` as well as `@ApplicationScoped`. On Quarkus startup, the
application fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException` — all implementations are active
simultaneously.

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ContextDiffStrategy
and qualifiers [@Default]: [TopLevelContextDiffStrategy, JsonPatchContextDiffStrategy, 
NoOpContextDiffStrategy]
```

### Context
SPI pattern where one default bean and two `@Alternative` beans implement an interface.
The intent was to activate one alternative at a time via
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`.

### What was tried
Adding `@Priority(10)` alongside `@Alternative` — expected this to be a ranking hint
that would help CDI resolve ambiguity. Instead, `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is what
globally activates the bean in CDI 4.0, making all three implementations simultaneously
active and causing the ambiguous resolution error.

### Root cause
In CDI 4.0 (Jakarta EE 10), `@Alternative @Priority(n)` is a shorthand for globally
activating an alternative across the entire application — no `beans.xml` or
`selected-alternatives` config needed. When ALL alternatives carry this annotation,
they are ALL active, which breaks injection points expecting a single bean.

### Solution 1 — Remove @Priority, use selected-alternatives config
**Approach:** Strip `@Priority` from all alternatives; activate one via config.
**Pros:** Per-profile/per-module control without code changes; the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching.
**Cons:** Requires a config entry per activation; all alternatives default to inactive.

Remove `@Priority` from all `@Alternative` beans. Activate a specific alternative only
via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`:

```java
// WRONG — @Priority globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0
@Alternative
@Priority(10)
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }

// CORRECT — @Alternative only; activation controlled by config
@Alternative
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }
```

```properties
# application.properties — activate the desired alternative
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.MyAlternativeImpl
```

### Solution 2 — Use different @Priority values to establish precedence
**Approach:** Keep `@Alternative @Priority` but assign distinct priority values; CDI resolves to the highest.
**Pros:** No config entry needed; the default (lowest priority) is always active, the override (highest priority) wins when present on the classpath. Works naturally for optional-module patterns where a higher-capability implementation should auto-replace the baseline.
**Cons:** All alternatives are always globally active (the core CDI 4.0 behaviour). The "default" must have the lowest priority; adding a third implementation requires careful priority assignment.

```java
// Default — always active; lowest priority loses when a higher one is present
@Alternative
@Priority(0)
@ApplicationScoped
public class ClaimFirstStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }

// Override — higher priority wins; active only when on the classpath
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
public class SemanticWorkerSelectionStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }
```

CDI injects the `@Priority(1)` bean when both are present; falls back to `@Priority(0)` when only
the default module is on the classpath. No `beans.xml` or `selected-alternatives` required.

**When to choose Solution 2 over Solution 1:** When the override is an optional module (not always
on the classpath) and the default must always be present. Config-based activation (Solution 1)
requires an explicit entry even for the default case; priority-based resolution (Solution 2) makes
the default zero-configuration.

### Why this is non-obvious
The `@Priority` annotation is commonly used alongside `@Alternative` in CDI tutorials
and examples from the CDI 2.x era, where `@Priority` on an alternative was a *ranking*
hint. In CDI 4.0 the semantics changed — `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is now an
*activation* mechanism (equivalent to listing it in `beans.xml` with a priority). The
Quarkus docs for `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` don't warn about this interaction.

### Why this fix
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` gives per-profile/per-module control via config,
which is the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching (same as how `PanningStrategyLoopControl`
and other engine SPIs are activated). It doesn't require code changes to switch backends.

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** GE-20260427-97650e "CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface"
**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab — use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md)

### Update — Test-refactoring trap: two classes declaring the same @Alternative

During a test refactoring, if two `@QuarkusTest` classes *simultaneously* declare an
`@Alternative @Priority(2)` inner class implementing the same type, Quarkus boot fails
with `AmbiguousResolutionException` even though `mvn test-compile` passes cleanly. The
compile succeeds because both classes are syntactically valid; the CDI conflict only
surfaces at application startup.

entries.title: renamePsiElementProcessor Requires order="first" for getPostRenameCallback to Fire
entries.body:
renamePsiElementProcessor Requires order="first" for getPostRenameCallback to Fire

## renamePsiElementProcessor Requires order="first" for getPostRenameCallback to Fire

**ID:** GE-0080
**Stack:** IntelliJ Platform (any version), `plugin.xml` extension registration
**Symptom:** A custom `RenamePsiElementProcessor` is invoked — `prepareRenaming()` runs and `canProcessElement()` returns true — but `getPostRenameCallback()` never fires. The rename completes normally; no error is thrown. Custom post-rename logic is silently skipped.
**Context:** Any plugin registering a `RenamePsiElementProcessor` that relies on `getPostRenameCallback()`.

### Root cause

IntelliJ calls `getPostRenameCallback()` only on the "primary" processor — the one returned by `RenamePsiElementProcessor.forElement(element)`. This method iterates registered processors in registration order and returns the first whose `canProcessElement()` returns true. The bundled Java plugin registers a `JavaRenameRefactoringSupport` processor at default priority. Without explicit ordering, it wins the election and its `getPostRenameCallback()` (which returns null for most elements) runs instead of the custom processor's.

### Fix

Register with `order="first"` in `plugin.xml`:
```xml
<renamePsiElementProcessor
    implementation="com.example.MyRenameProcessor"
    order="first"/>
```

### Why non-obvious

`prepareRenaming()` is called on ALL processors whose `canProcessElement()` returns true — it appears the processor is working. Only `getPostRenameCallback()` (and `renameElement()` for the primary processor) is restricted to the primary. The distinction between "all processors" and "primary processor" is not documented in the `RenamePsiElementProcessor` Javadoc. The `order` attribute in `plugin.xml` is documented for some extension points but not called out as required here.

**See also:** GE-0079 (spurious space — same rename processor pipeline), GE-0081 (PsiElement invalidation — same rename lifecycle)

*Score: 14/15 · Included because: completely silent failure, 100% reproducible, anyone using getPostRenameCallback() will hit this · Reservation: none*

entries.title: casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore
entries.body:
casehub-engine-persistence-memory @Alternative beans need explicit selected-alternatives for SubCaseGroupRepository and PlanItemStore

### Symptom

Quarkus startup fails with:

```
jakarta.enterprise.inject.spi.DeploymentException: Found 2 deployment problems:
[1] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseExecutionHandler constructor
[2] Unsatisfied dependency for type io.casehub.engine.common.spi.SubCaseGroupRepository
    - injection target: SubCaseCompletionService constructor
```

### Context

When a CaseHub harness adds `casehub-engine-blackboard` (required for SubCase lifecycle and M-of-N quorum) alongside `casehub-engine-persistence-memory`, the blackboard beans (`SubCaseExecutionHandler`, `SubCaseCompletionService`) need `SubCaseGroupRepository`. The in-memory implementation (`MemorySubCaseGroupRepository`) exists in persistence-memory but is `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — CDI does not activate `@Alternative` beans unless explicitly selected.

The same applies to `MemoryPlanItemStore` and `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore` from the same module.

### What was tried (didn't work)

- Adding Jandex index entries for persistence-memory — beans are discovered but not activated (they're @Alternative)
- Assuming engine-testing would provide @Priority(1) overrides — engine-testing has TestCaseMetaModelRepository, TestCaseInstanceRepository, TestEventLogRepository but NOT TestSubCaseGroupRepository or TestPlanItemStore

### Root cause

`casehub-engine-persistence-memory` ships all repository implementations as `@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` — they require explicit `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` to be activated. Engine-testing provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for CaseInstanceRepository and EventLogRepository but not for SubCaseGroupRepository or PlanItemStore.

### Fix

Add to `application.properties` (test or production):

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemorySubCaseGroupRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryPlanItemStore,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Append to existing `selected-alternatives` if other beans (e.g. `JpaLedgerEntryRepository`) are already listed.

### Why this is non-obvious

The error names `SubCaseGroupRepository` — but `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` IS on the classpath and IS Jandex-indexed. The CDI error doesn't mention that the bean exists but is an unselected `@Alternative`. You have to read the source of `MemorySubCaseGroupRepository` to discover the `@Alternative` annotation, then check whether engine-testing provides an override (it doesn't for these SPIs).

## Variant — Runtime failure from implicit activation of remaining @Alternative beans

### Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` starts cleanly (no DeploymentException) because `casehub-engine-testing` provides `@Priority(1)` overrides for `CaseInstanceRepository` and `EventLogRepository`. But the first `startCase()` call in certain test classes fails with:

```
(RECIPIENT_FAILURE,8191) CaseInstance not found or wrong tenant: <uuid>
```

The error comes from `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository.update()` (persistence-memory) — the bean is implicitly activated by Quarkus Arc as the sole `@Alternative` without a competing non-alternative, but the activation path produces intermittent initialization-order failures.

### Context

When only `SubCaseGroupRepository` and `PlanItemStore` are in `selected-alternatives`, the remaining 4 `@Alternative` beans from persistence-memory rely on Arc's implicit fallback activation:
- `InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository`
- `InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository`
- `InMemoryEventLogRepository`
- `MemoryReactivePlanItemStore`

This implicit activation works for most test classes but fails intermittently when the test class is among the first to trigger `startCase()` in a fresh JVM — the `CaseStartedEventHandler` calls `caseInstanceRepository.update()` and the instance isn't found.

### Fix

Add all 4 remaining persistence-memory `@Alternative` beans to `selected-alternatives`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  ...,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseInstanceRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryCaseMetaModelRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.InMemoryEventLogRepository,\
  io.casehub.persistence.memory.MemoryReactivePlanItemStore
```

Verified in casehub-aml#58: 4 of 6 failing test classes fixed by explicit selection. 2 remaining failures (`AmlLayer7ResourceTest`, `AmlTrustRoutingAttestationTest`) may have a deeper root cause — investigation ongoing.

entries.title: @IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs
entries.body:
@IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs

### Symptom

Adding a dev-profile-only CDI bean (e.g., a `CurrentPrincipal` for dev mode) via `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` causes startup failures — all beans from the non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` list disappear.

### Context

Quarkus profile-specific properties (`%dev.`, `%test.`, `%prod.`) completely **replace** their non-profiled counterparts. If the base `application.properties` has:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  com.example.JpaRepo,\
  com.example.JpaPlanItemStore,\
  com.example.JpaSubCaseGroupRepo
```

Adding `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` drops all three JPA alternatives in dev mode — CDI startup fails with unsatisfied dependency errors for every bean that was in the original list.

### Root cause

Profile-specific properties in Quarkus are full replacements, not merges. This is by design (SmallRye Config behaviour), but the failure mode is silent — no warning that the non-profiled value was dropped.

### Fix

Use bean-level annotations instead of `selected-alternatives`:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(150)  // beats OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)
@IfBuildProfile("dev")
public class DemoCurrentPrincipal implements CurrentPrincipal {
    @Override public String tenancyId() { return "demo-tenant"; }
    @Override public String actorId()   { return "demo-user"; }
}
```

**Requirements:**
1. The bean must live in a **Jandex-indexed JAR** (e.g., the application's own `runtime/` module). ArC discovers it automatically — no `selected-alternatives` entry needed.
2. `@IfBuildProfile("dev")` excludes the bean entirely from non-dev builds — no ambiguity in production.
3. `@Priority(150)` resolves over competing `@Alternative` beans (e.g., `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)`) via standard ArC priority resolution.

This is the same mechanism by which `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Alternative @Priority(100)` beats `MockCurrentPrincipal @DefaultBean` in production — without being listed in `selected-alternatives`.

### Why this is non-obvious

The natural instinct is `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=MyDevBean` — it looks like the standard profile-override pattern. The replacement behaviour is documented for SmallRye Config but not prominently flagged in the Quarkus CDI guide. The failure (dropped JPA alternatives) manifests as unrelated `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` errors that don't mention `selected-alternatives` at all.

### Why this fix

`@IfBuildProfile + @Priority` solves the problem at the bean level — no configuration property needed. The alternative (duplicating the full `selected-alternatives` list in every profile override) is fragile and drifts as new alternatives are added.

### Alternatives considered

- **Duplicate the full list in `%dev.`** — works but fragile; every new `@Alternative` bean must be added to every profile-specific override. Drifts silently.
- **Use `@UnlessBuildProfile("prod")` instead of `@IfBuildProfile("dev")`** — activates in both dev and test profiles, which may not be desired. Test profile has its own `FixedCurrentPrincipal @Priority(200)`.

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

## @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

### Stack

### Symptom

```
and qualifiers [@Default]: [TopLevelContextDiffStrategy, JsonPatchContextDiffStrategy,
```

### Context

The intent was to activate one alternative at a time via

### What was tried

that would help CDI resolve ambiguity. Instead, `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is what

**Cons:** Requires a config entry per activation; all alternatives default to inactive.



**Cons:** All alternatives are always globally active (the core CDI 4.0 behaviour). The "default" must have the lowest priority; adding a third implementation requires careful priority assignment.

the default module is on the classpath. No `beans.xml` or `selected-alternatives` required.

and other engine SPIs are activated). It doesn't require code changes to switch backends.

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** GE-20260427-97650e "CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface"
**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab — use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md)

### Update — Test-refactoring trap: two classes declaring the same @Alternative

During a test refactoring, if two `@QuarkusTest` classes *simultaneously* declare an
`@Alternative @Priority(2)` inner class implementing the same type, Quarkus boot fails
with `AmbiguousResolutionException` even though `mvn test-compile` passes cleanly. The
compile succeeds because both classes are syntactically valid; the CDI conflict only
surfaces at application startup.

entries.title: Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException

### Symptom

After removing `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from one CDI bean, a different bean
silently becomes the active implementation. No `AmbiguousResolutionException`, no
warning, no log. Tests that depend on the removed bean's behaviour fail with
unrelated-looking assertion errors.

### Context

Three implementations of the same port interface coexist in the build:

| Bean | Annotations | Role |
|------|------------|------|
| `PrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @DefaultBean` | Layer 1 naive baseline |
| `QhorusPrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(1)` | Layer 3 qhorus messaging |
| `PrReviewCaseService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(2)` | Layer 5 engine (active) |

This is the CDI displacement pattern for progressive layer integration — each layer
adds an implementation; CDI priority determines which is active at runtime.

### What was tried (didn't work)

Removed `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from `PrReviewCaseService` to fix an anti-pattern
documented in ARC42STORIES §8 ("CDI priority gymnastics"). Expected: the plain
`@ApplicationScoped` bean would still displace the `@DefaultBean` baseline via
Quarkus's `@DefaultBean` semantics.

### Root cause

CDI resolution rules interact in a non-obvious way:

1. `@DefaultBean` beans are suppressed when any non-`@DefaultBean` bean exists ✅
2. Activated `@Alternative` beans (with `@Priority`) take precedence over regular beans ⚠️
3. After removing `@Alternative` from `PrReviewCaseService`, it becomes a regular bean
4. `QhorusPrReviewService` (`@Alternative @Priority(1)`) is still an activated alternative
5. **Rule 2 applies**: the activated alternative wins over the regular bean
6. `QhorusPrReviewService` becomes the active implementation — silently

The mental model "I'm just removing unnecessary annotations" is wrong. The annotations
are load-bearing: they participate in a priority chain where every bean's annotation
set matters.

### Fix

All implementations in the `@Alternative` chain must be addressed together, not one at
a time. To properly fix the anti-pattern:

- Either make ALL non-baseline implementations use `@DefaultBean` displacement
  (remove `@Alternative @Priority` from all of them and ensure only one non-`@DefaultBean` exists)
- Or keep the `@Alternative @Priority` chain intact and accept it as the mechanism
  for layer coexistence

Do not remove `@Alternative` from one bean without checking what other `@Alternative`
implementations exist for the same interface.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reason about CDI alternatives in pairs: "my bean vs the default."
The three-way interaction (DefaultBean + Alternative(1) + Alternative(2)) is rarely
encountered, and the silent resolution change (no exception, wrong bean) gives no
diagnostic signal. The symptom (test assertion failure on unrelated data) is
completely disconnected from the cause (CDI annotation change).

### Why this fix

The obvious approach — "just remove the anti-pattern annotation" — is wrong because
it treats the annotations as decoration rather than as a load-bearing priority chain.
The fix requires understanding the full set of implementations and their annotation
interactions before changing any single one.

### Alternatives considered

- Remove `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from only the active bean → silently breaks resolution (this gotcha)
- Remove `@Alternative` from ALL implementations and use `@DefaultBean` displacement → requires ensuring exactly one non-`@DefaultBean` impl, and retiring or `@DefaultBean`-ing the intermediate layers
- Keep `@Alternative @Priority` chain as-is → accepted as the working mechanism until a coordinated cleanup addresses all implementations together

**See also:** GE-20260626-c21b02 — @DefaultBean suppression by any Instance<T> peer, a different silent failure in the same CDI displacement space.

entries.title: Stateless multi-tier SLA escalation via candidateGroups — no state storage or decision serialization needed
@DefaultBean BlockingToReactiveBridge — wrap any blocking SPI as reactive, displaced by native async @Alternative
entries.body:
@DefaultBean BlockingToReactiveBridge — wrap any blocking SPI as reactive, displaced by native async @Alternative

## Context

You have a blocking SPI (`CaseMemoryStore`) in a zero-dep API module and need to expose a
reactive mirror (`ReactiveCaseMemoryStore`) so callers using Mutiny can also use the same
backends. Most blocking adapters don't need true async execution — wrapping them as
`Uni.createFrom().item(supplier)` is sufficient. But occasional adapters (e.g. a graph
database with native async traversal) do benefit from a true reactive implementation.

## What developers reach for instead

- Two independent SPIs with no connection — adapters implement both, duplicating logic
- Make the reactive SPI extend the blocking SPI — creates tight coupling and forces adapters
  to implement both interface families
- A single abstract class with default delegation — doesn't play well with CDI proxies

## The Technique

Define the blocking SPI in the zero-dep API module. Define the reactive SPI separately
(in the Quarkus module, because `Uni<>` depends on Smallrye Mutiny). Ship a
`@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` bridge class that injects the active blocking impl and
wraps each method:

```java
@DefaultBean
@ApplicationScoped
public class BlockingToReactiveBridge implements ReactiveCaseMemoryStore {

@Inject CaseMemoryStore delegate;   // whatever blocking impl is active

@Override
    public Uni<String> store(MemoryInput input) {
        return Uni.createFrom().item(() -> delegate.store(input));
    }

@Override
    public Uni<List<Memory>> query(MemoryQuery query) {
        return Uni.createFrom().item(() -> delegate.query(query));
    }

@Override
    public Uni<Void> erase(EraseRequest request) {
        return Uni.createFrom().voidItem().invoke(() -> delegate.erase(request));
    }
}
```

The CDI priority ladder then becomes:

| Tier | Class | Annotation | Active when |
|------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 0 | `NoOpCaseMemoryStore` | `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` | No adapter |
| — | `BlockingToReactiveBridge` | `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` | Always (wraps Tier 0–2) |
| 1 | Memori adapter | `@ApplicationScoped` | Memori dep declared |
| 2 | Mem0 adapter | `@Alternative @Priority(1)` | Mem0 dep declared |
| 3 | Graphiti native-reactive | `@Alternative @Priority(2)` on `ReactiveCaseMemoryStore` | Graphiti dep declared |

When Graphiti (Tier 3) is added, it overrides the `ReactiveCaseMemoryStore` binding directly
via its higher-priority `@Alternative` — the bridge's `@DefaultBean` yields automatically.
Tier 3 does NOT need to implement the blocking SPI at all.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The `@DefaultBean` on the bridge is the key: it means both the bridge AND any blocking
adapter can coexist on the classpath without CDI ambiguity. The bridge always yields to a
native-reactive `@Alternative` on `ReactiveCaseMemoryStore`, and the blocking adapter
displaces the no-op on `CaseMemoryStore`. Each priority rung resolves independently.

Without `@DefaultBean`, a plain `@ApplicationScoped` bridge would collide with any real
`ReactiveCaseMemoryStore` implementation (ARC sees two beans for the same type and fails at
startup).

**Note:** Do NOT add `@Blocking` to bridge methods. `ExecutionModelAnnotationsProcessor`
rejects it on plain CDI bean methods returning `Uni` — only framework entrypoints accept it.
Thread dispatch is the entrypoint's responsibility. (See GE-20260528-74914d.)

## See Also

- `GE-20260521-d72294` — blocking and reactive store interfaces with irreconcilable signatures; this bridge avoids the problem by not having one class implement both
- `GE-20260528-74914d` — `@Blocking` rejected on CDI bean methods returning `Uni`

**See also:** [GE-20260620-9d043b](GE-20260620-9d043b.md) — CDI @Decorator double-application through blocking-to-reactive bridge — idempotency guard required (the gotcha this pattern creates)

entries.title: Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries
entries.body:
Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries

AE memory entries written to the PATIENT domain must carry `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` (CTCAE grade name, e.g. "GRADE_3") and `MemoryAttributeKeys.OUTCOME` (lifecycle status: "REPORTED", "ESCALATED", "DSMB_ESCALATED") as **separate attributes**. Conflating them — storing `grade.name()` as `OUTCOME` — breaks `hasPriorGrade3OrAbove()` for escalation entries and produces a facts map without a `grade` key, violating the spec contract at `.patientContext.facts[].grade`. The `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` key is the canonical way to carry CTCAE grade through the memory store; `OUTCOME` is always a lifecycle or resolution status, never a grade.

entries.title: ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet().add() provides atomic idempotent registration without explicit locks
entries.body:
ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet().add() provides atomic idempotent registration without explicit locks

### Context

You need to register a resource (e.g. a backend in a registry, a channel in a gateway, a listener on a topic) exactly once per key, even under concurrent access from multiple threads. You want to avoid explicit locks, `synchronized` blocks, and the awkward semantics of `computeIfAbsent` when the computation has side effects.

### Technique

```java
private final Set<UUID> registeredKeys = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();

public void ensureRegistered(UUID key) {
    if (registeredKeys.add(key)) {   // atomic: exactly one thread returns true
        doRegistration(key);          // called exactly once per key
    }
}

public void deregister(UUID key) {
    registeredKeys.remove(key);      // subsequent ensureRegistered() will re-register
}
```

`ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet()` returns a `Set<K>` backed by a ConcurrentHashMap. `Set.add()` returns `true` if the element was actually added (was absent) and `false` if it was already present. Under concurrent access, exactly one thread gets `true` — all others get `false` and skip the side effect. No explicit locking required.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reach for `synchronized(this)` or `computeIfAbsent`. The `newKeySet().add()` pattern:
- Is 2 lines vs 5-10 for a `synchronized` block
- Returns the boolean signal the caller needs directly from `add()`
- Has guaranteed CAS semantics from the ConcurrentHashMap backing
- Clearly expresses intent: "register this key once, skip if already registered"

`computeIfAbsent` also works but its Javadoc warns that the computation must not modify the map — using it to trigger side effects (registration) is semantically awkward and potentially unsafe if the side effect is not pure. The `Set.add()` pattern has no such restriction.

### Constraints

The side effect in `doRegistration()` must be safe to call after `add()` returns but before other threads observe the registration in the set. If `doRegistration()` itself must be atomic with the `add()` (e.g. the registration step reads back the key), this pattern is insufficient — use a dedicated lock or a more explicit state machine.

entries.title: Lock-outside-transaction pattern for safe concurrent sequence assignment in CDI/JTA
nextSequenceNumber() row lock incidentally serializes the Merkle frontier update for same-subject concurrent saves
entries.body:
nextSequenceNumber() row lock incidentally serializes the Merkle frontier update for same-subject concurrent saves

### What Was Discovered

`JpaLedgerEntryRepository.save()` is `@Transactional`. The first DB write it makes is `LedgerSequenceAllocator.nextSequenceNumber()`, which executes:

```sql
INSERT INTO ledger_subject_sequence (subject_id, next_seq)
VALUES (CAST(?1 AS UUID), 1)
ON CONFLICT (subject_id) DO UPDATE SET next_seq = ledger_subject_sequence.next_seq + 1
```

In PostgreSQL, this `INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` acquires a write lock on the `ledger_subject_sequence` row for the given `subject_id`. That lock is held for the **entire duration** of the enclosing `@Transactional` transaction — which includes the Merkle frontier read-compute-replace cycle that comes later in the same `save()` call.

**Effect:** A second concurrent `save()` for the same `subject_id` blocks on the `INSERT ON CONFLICT` until the first transaction commits. By the time the second transaction proceeds, the first has committed its frontier update. The second reads the committed frontier and computes the correct next Merkle state.

This means the Merkle chain is **correct for concurrent JPA saves on the same subject** — not because of any explicit frontier locking, but because the sequence allocation lock incidentally serializes the entire pipeline.

### Why This Is Non-Obvious

The Merkle frontier update (`findBySubjectId → append → replace`) is a read-modify-write cycle with no `SELECT FOR UPDATE`. Inspection of the code suggests a race condition: two concurrent transactions could both read the same stale frontier and produce inconsistent chains. In practice, this race does not occur on PostgreSQL because the upstream sequence lock serializes them. The connection between sequence allocation locking and Merkle frontier safety is not documented anywhere in the codebase.

### The Merkle Serialization Invariant

Three facts must simultaneously hold for the implicit serialization to remain valid:

1. **`save()` is `@Transactional`** — the sequence lock must cover the frontier update.
2. **`nextSequenceNumber()` is the first per-subject serializing DB write in `save()`** — the lock must be acquired before the frontier read. Other writes (e.g. `actor_identity` tokenization for `ActorType.HUMAN`) may precede it but are per-actor, not per-subject.
3. **`JpaLedgerMerkleFrontierRepository.replace()` participates in the same transaction** — via `@Transactional(REQUIRED)` propagation.

If any of these changes — `save()` split into two transactions, sequence allocation moved to `REQUIRES_NEW`, `replace()` made `NOT_SUPPORTED` — the Merkle chain silently corrupts without any test or constraint catching it.

### InMemory Path

`InMemoryLedgerEntryRepository` does not benefit from this implicit serialization. It must use an explicit per-subject `synchronized` block covering sequence allocation through frontier update.

entries.title: casehub-core TaskDefinitionRegistry: safe to instantiate with new in unit tests — PoisonPillDetector is not used by register() or getForCaseType()
entries.body:
casehub-core TaskDefinitionRegistry: safe to instantiate with new in unit tests — PoisonPillDetector is not used by register() or getForCaseType()

### What's surprising

`TaskDefinitionRegistry` is annotated `@ApplicationScoped` but can be safely instantiated with `new TaskDefinitionRegistry()` in unit tests. Its `register()` and `getForCaseType()` methods use only `ConcurrentHashMap` internally. Despite having `@Inject PoisonPillDetector poisonPillDetector` as a field, `PoisonPillDetector` is only used in `ListenerEvaluator.evaluateAndCreatePlanItems()` — not in the registry itself.

### How to use

```java
TaskDefinitionRegistry registry = new TaskDefinitionRegistry();
registry.register(myStubPlugin, Set.of("starcraft-game"));
List<TaskDefinition> plugins = registry.getForCaseType("starcraft-game");
// Works without a Quarkus container
```

`register()` runs a cycle-detection DFS over `entryCriteria()` and `producedKeys()` — ensure stub plugins return disjoint key sets to avoid `CircularDependencyException`. Stub plugins implementing both `io.casehub.core.TaskDefinition` and the app-specific `TaskDefinition` interface can be registered directly.

### Root cause

`PoisonPillDetector` was added as an `@Inject` field to `TaskDefinitionRegistry` for quarantine support, but the quarantine check (`isQuarantined()`) is invoked only in `ListenerEvaluator`, not in the registry's own methods. The registry is effectively a thread-safe typed map with cycle detection.

### Why this is non-obvious

`@ApplicationScoped` CDI beans are conventionally assumed to require a container. The presence of `@Inject PoisonPillDetector` reinforces this assumption — nothing in the API or documentation indicates that the injected field is not used in the primary query methods. Developers attempting to test `TaskDefinitionRegistry`-aware code typically reach for `@QuarkusTest` (significant boot overhead) when a plain `new` instantiation suffices.

entries.title: Read-modify-write on thread-safe store is not atomic — concurrent writers lose updates
GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing
entries.body:
GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing

## Behaviour

`GreenMailExtension` extends `GreenMailProxy`. In `greenmail-junit5 2.1.3`, `GreenMailProxy.getGreenMail()` is declared `protected`:

```bash
$ javap -classpath greenmail-junit5-2.1.3.jar com.icegreen.greenmail.junit5.GreenMailExtension
...
protected com.icegreen.greenmail.util.GreenMail getGreenMail();
```

Attempting to call it from test code:

```java
GreenMail mail = GREEN_MAIL.getGreenMail(); // compile error: getGreenMail() has protected access
```

## Why This Matters

`GreenMail` (the bare class) has a `deliver(MimeMessage)` method and other useful internal APIs. The extension does not expose them. Since `getGreenMail()` is protected, you cannot retrieve the underlying instance to call them.

**Workaround:** In `@QuarkusTestResource` implementations that construct `GreenMail` directly (not via the extension), you hold the instance yourself as a static field — giving you full access to `deliver()`, `getManagers()`, etc.

## Why It's Undocumented

The Greenmail documentation and README make no mention of this access restriction. The Greenmail JUnit 5 guide shows the extension as a drop-in for the JUnit 4 Rule and implies equivalent functionality. The only way to discover the `protected` modifier is via `javap` on the jar.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-aa8445](GE-20260529-aa8445.md) — GreenMailExtension has no public deliver() method

entries.title: Security Audit — Principles
entries.body:
**Rate limiting** - prevent DoS attacks:
- Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints
- Throttle expensive operations
- Use backpressure for queue processing

**Least privilege** - minimize permissions:
- Run services with minimal required permissions
- Separate read/write database users
- Use fine-grained permission models

**Audit logging** - track security-relevant events:
- Log authentication attempts (success and failure)
- Log authorization failures
- Log access to sensitive data
- Include user ID, timestamp, action, resource

---

## Severity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Vulnerability_detected((Vulnerability detected))
    Exploitable_remotely_{Exploitable remotely?}
    Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_{Leads to data breach or RCE?}
    Requires_authentication_{Requires authentication?}
    CRITICAL[CRITICAL]
    Defense_in_depth_issue_{Defense in depth issue?}
    WARNING[WARNING]
    NOTE[NOTE]
    Vulnerability_detected --> Exploitable_remotely_
    Exploitable_remotely_ -->|yes| Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_
    Exploitable_remotely_ -->|no| Defense_in_depth_issue_
    Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_ -->|yes| CRITICAL
    Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_ -->|no| Requires_authentication_
    Requires_authentication_ -->|yes| WARNING
    Requires_authentication_ -->|no| CRITICAL
    Defense_in_depth_issue_ -->|yes| WARNING
    Defense_in_depth_issue_ -->|no| NOTE
```

---

## Common Pitfalls

| Mistake | Why It's Wrong | Fix |
|---------|----------------|-----|
| "It's only accessible to authenticated users" | Auth can be bypassed, always validate authorization | Check permissions even for authenticated endpoints |
| "Input validation on frontend" | Frontend can be bypassed | Always validate on backend |
| "This endpoint isn't public" | Security through obscurity fails | Protect all endpoints |
| "We'll fix it after launch" | Vulnerabilities get exploited quickly | Fix before production |
| Trusting environment variables blindly | Env vars can be exposed or leaked | Validate and sanitize env var contents |
| Using blacklist validation | Attackers find ways around blacklists | Use whitelist validation |
| Logging sensitive data for debugging | Logs leak to unauthorized parties | Never log passwords, tokens, PII |
| "Nobody knows this endpoint exists" | Attackers scan and enumerate | Assume all endpoints will be discovered |

---

## See Also

- `~/.hortora/garden/approaches/code-review.md` — General code review principles (safety, concurrency, testing)

entries.title: quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev without a real OIDC server
entries.body:
quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev without a real OIDC server

# quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev — allows OIDC service to run without a real OIDC server

### Context
Running a Quarkus service with `quarkus-oidc` and `@RolesAllowed` annotations in dev mode
without a real OIDC server (no DevServices, no Keycloak). All guarded endpoints return 401
for every request.

### Technique
Set in `application.properties`:
```properties
%dev.quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false
%dev.quarkus.oidc.enabled=false
%dev.quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false
```

`quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` activates `DevModeDisabledAuthorizationController`
(`@Alternative @Priority @Singleton` in `quarkus-security-runtime-spi`), which returns `false`
from `isAuthorizationEnabled()`. Both `EagerSecurityHandler` (REST path — checks at line 53
before processing the request) and `StandardSecurityCheckInterceptor` (CDI path — checks via
`controller.isAuthorizationEnabled()`) bail out early. `@RolesAllowed` is not enforced.

`quarkus.oidc.enabled=false` is also required — without it, OIDC intercepts requests before
the authorization check fires, returning 401 for missing tokens even with auth disabled.

### Why this is non-obvious
The natural dev-mode approaches are: (a) spin up Keycloak DevServices (60–90s cold start,
heavyweight), or (b) configure a real OIDC server. `quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode`
is not mentioned in the main OIDC documentation; it appears only in `quarkus-security-runtime-spi`
source as the activation mechanism for `DevModeDisabledAuthorizationController`. The interceptor
mechanism is two-level — disabling OIDC alone is insufficient because the authorization
interceptor fires independently of the authentication mechanism.

### Why this fix
Over disabling DevServices alone: `quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false` without the
auth flag still returns 401 — the security framework enforces `@RolesAllowed` against anonymous
identity regardless of whether an auth mechanism is registered. The auth flag is the only clean
way to bypass the enforcement layer itself.
