entries.title: Lock-outside-transaction pattern for safe concurrent sequence assignment in CDI/JTA
H2 2.x MVStore MERGE INTO is not concurrent-safe for first-inserts
entries.body:
H2 2.x MVStore MERGE INTO is not concurrent-safe for first-inserts

### Context

`MERGE INTO table USING (...) WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT` is used as an
atomic upsert — e.g. for sequence allocation tables that need "insert if absent,
update if present."

### Symptom

Under concurrent writes from multiple threads targeting the same new primary key,
one or more transactions throw:

```
Unique index or primary key violation: "PUBLIC.PK_<table> INDEX ..."
SQL statement: MERGE INTO <table> AS t USING ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...
```

The failure only occurs on first-insert for a new key. Subsequent writes (where
the row already exists) hit `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE` and succeed. Serial tests
pass; concurrent tests fail.

### Root Cause

H2 2.0 (July 2021) switched the default storage engine from **page-store**
(table-level locking) to **MVStore** (MVCC / row-level). Under MVStore, `MERGE INTO`
evaluates `WHEN NOT MATCHED` non-atomically:

1. Transaction A reads — row absent → plans INSERT
2. Transaction B reads — row absent → plans INSERT
3. Transaction A inserts → succeeds
4. Transaction B inserts → unique constraint violation (row now exists)

The conflict is not suppressed — it propagates as a `ConstraintViolationException`,
poisoning Transaction B's `EntityManager` and requiring a full transaction rollback.

Documentation and Javadoc written for H2 1.x stated "H2 uses table-level locking."
This was true for page-store. It is false for MVStore.

### Fix

Replace the single-statement `MERGE INTO` with two statements:

```java
// INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING — concurrent first-inserts are safe.
// H2 2.2+ in MODE=PostgreSQL supports the no-column-target form only:
// ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (not ON CONFLICT (col1, col2) DO NOTHING).
em.createNativeQuery(
    "INSERT INTO sequence_table (subject_id, tenancy_id, next_seq) " +
    "VALUES (CAST(?1 AS UUID), ?2, 1) " +
    "ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING")
    .setParameter(1, subjectId).setParameter(2, tenancyId).executeUpdate();

em.createNativeQuery(
    "UPDATE sequence_table SET next_seq = next_seq + 1 " +
    "WHERE subject_id = CAST(?1 AS UUID) AND tenancy_id = ?2")
    .setParameter(1, subjectId).setParameter(2, tenancyId).executeUpdate();
```

Both PostgreSQL and H2 2.2+ (in `MODE=PostgreSQL`) accept this SQL. The `UPDATE`
acquires a row lock held for the transaction duration, serialising concurrent writes.

### Why this is non-obvious

H2 2.0 silently changed the default storage engine — existing `MERGE INTO` code
that worked correctly for years under H2 1.x breaks only under concurrent load.
Serial unit tests always pass. The failure surfaces only in integration test suites
that exercise concurrent writes (e.g. Quartz workers, concurrent HTTP handlers).

The Javadoc claiming "H2 uses table-level locking" remained in the codebase long
after H2 2.x shipped, reinforcing the incorrect assumption.

### Why this fix

`MERGE INTO` evaluated in a single statement appears atomic but is not under MVCC.
The two-statement approach makes the sequence explicit: one statement that safely
handles concurrent first-inserts (DO NOTHING on conflict), and one that serialises
via row lock. This is identical to PostgreSQL's `INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING +
UPDATE` pattern and can be unified across both dialects with the same SQL.

### Alternatives considered

- **`LOCK_MODE=1` on H2 URL** — does not restore table-level locking for MVStore tables;
  the flag only affects the discontinued page-store engine.
- **`REQUIRES_NEW` inner transaction for first-insert** — adds a class and transaction
  boundary just for the initial-insert edge case; rejected as unnecessary complexity.
- **`SELECT FOR UPDATE` / pessimistic lock** — cannot lock a row that doesn't exist yet;
  fails silently on first-insert (same race condition).

**See also:** [GE-20260605-b734b3](GE-20260605-b734b3.md) — SQL-standard MERGE INTO USING WHEN MATCHED/NOT MATCHED for portable H2 + PostgreSQL atomic upsert (the technique that addresses this H2 limitation)

**See also:** [GE-20260618-0ed34c](GE-20260618-0ed34c.md) — H2 LOCK_MODE=1 has no effect with MVStore (failed workaround for this problem) | [GE-20260618-73a023](GE-20260618-73a023.md) — H2 JDBC getMetaData().getURL() drops connection properties

entries.title: H2 LOCK_MODE=1 has no effect with MVStore — concurrent MERGE races persist
entries.body:
H2 LOCK_MODE=1 has no effect with MVStore — concurrent MERGE races persist

## Symptom

Adding `LOCK_MODE=1` to the H2 JDBC URL to prevent concurrent first-insert races on a
shared table appears to succeed — no error, the application starts — but the concurrent
race condition continues unaffected. Two threads racing to INSERT the same primary key
still produce `JdbcSQLIntegrityConstraintViolationException`.

```
JDBC URL:
jdbc:h2:mem:testdb;MODE=PostgreSQL;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1;LOCK_MODE=1
```

```
Error (still observed with LOCK_MODE=1):
Unique index or primary key violation: "PUBLIC.PK_LEDGER_SUBJECT_SEQUENCE
INDEX ... VALUES ( /* key:1 */ UUID '...')"; SQL statement:
MERGE INTO ledger_subject_sequence AS t USING (...) AS s ...
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (subject_id, tenancy_id, next_seq) VALUES (...)
```

## Context

Triggered by concurrent `MERGE INTO` on a new row that both threads see as absent (MVCC
read-committed isolation). Common in `@QuarkusTest` suites where multiple Quartz workers
or CDI async observers write to the same H2 table simultaneously for the first time.

LOCK_MODE is a documented H2 parameter. The documentation does not clearly state that it
only applies to the discontinued page-store engine and is silently ignored in MVStore mode.

## What was tried

- `LOCK_MODE=1` in JDBC URL — no effect; race persists.
- `LOCK_MODE=2` — same; still silently ignored.
- Neither URL parameter nor `SET LOCK_MODE` SQL triggers an error; the setting is accepted
  but MVStore's MVCC engine does not honour it.

## Root cause

H2 2.0 switched the default storage engine from page-store to MVStore. MVStore uses MVCC
(multi-version concurrency control) with row-level locking, not table-level locking. The
`LOCK_MODE` URL parameter only controlled the page-store engine's locking behaviour.
In H2 2.x, MVStore always uses MVCC regardless of `LOCK_MODE`. The parameter is accepted
without error for backward compatibility but has no observable effect.

## Fix

Replace `MERGE INTO` with `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` + `UPDATE`. H2 2.2+
(released July 2023) added `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` support for `INSERT` statements,
including in `MODE=PostgreSQL`. The `ON CONFLICT` path is race-safe: if two transactions
simultaneously attempt the first insert, exactly one succeeds and the other silently does
nothing; both then proceed to the `UPDATE`, which serialises via the MVStore row lock.

```sql
-- Instead of:
MERGE INTO t USING (...) AS s ON t.key = s.key
  WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.val = t.val + 1
  WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (key, val) VALUES (s.key, 2)

-- Use:
INSERT INTO t (key, val) VALUES (CAST(? AS UUID), 1) ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;
UPDATE t SET val = val + 1 WHERE key = CAST(? AS UUID);
```

This is the same SQL as the PostgreSQL path — both H2 2.2+ and PostgreSQL support it, so
a single implementation works for both databases.

## Why this is non-obvious

`LOCK_MODE=1` is documented as enabling "table-level locking", which is exactly what is
needed to serialise concurrent MERGE statements. The parameter is silently accepted with no
error or warning when it has no effect. There is no runtime indication that the setting is
being ignored. The fix requires knowing that (a) H2 2.0 removed page-store, (b) MVStore is
MVStore is immune to LOCK_MODE, and (c) H2 2.2+ added ON CONFLICT support — none of these
are prominently documented together.

### Why this fix

`ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` + `UPDATE` produces identical SQL for both H2 and PostgreSQL,
eliminating the dialect split that caused the original bug. The `MERGE INTO` approach
assumed H2 would serialize via table locking — an assumption that was silently wrong for
five major releases.

**See also:** [GE-20260617-6b01c4](GE-20260617-6b01c4.md) — H2 2.x MVStore MERGE INTO is not concurrent-safe for first-inserts (the root problem LOCK_MODE=1 was meant to fix) | [GE-20260618-73a023](GE-20260618-73a023.md) — H2 JDBC getMetaData().getURL() drops connection properties | [GE-20260605-b734b3](GE-20260605-b734b3.md) — SQL-standard MERGE INTO for portable H2 + PostgreSQL

entries.title: ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent blocks all same-bucket keys while mapping function runs — unusable for network calls
entries.body:
ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent blocks all same-bucket keys while mapping function runs — unusable for network calls

## Symptom

Using `computeIfAbsent` to cache external provider contexts (e.g. fetching a public key from Vault Transit on first use) causes unexpected latency spikes. Under concurrent load, a thread loading one actor's context can block threads loading a different actor's context — even though the keys are completely unrelated.

In extreme cases with synchronised re-entry (the mapping function internally calls back into the map for a different key that hashes to the same bucket), the JVM may deadlock.

## Context

Per-actorId context cache in an `AgentSigner` implementation. First `sign()` call per actor triggers `loadContext(actorId)` which makes an HTTP call to Vault. Subsequent calls should be instant (cache hit).

```java
// WRONG — computeIfAbsent holds the bucket lock during the HTTP call
Optional<C> cached = contextCache.computeIfAbsent(actorId, id -> {
    return loadContext(id);  // HTTP call — may take hundreds of ms
});
```

## Root cause

`ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent` acquires an internal per-bucket lock (striped lock on the bin node) for the duration of the mapping function. While one thread is inside a slow mapping function:
- **All other threads needing any key in the same hash bucket are blocked** — not just the same key
- A second thread calling `computeIfAbsent` for a different key that happens to share the same hash bucket will block until the first completes
- If the mapping function itself calls back into the same map (re-entrant), the JDK **throws `IllegalStateException: Recursive update`** in Java 9+

The Javadoc says "the computation should be short and simple" but gives no hint that slow computations block an entire hash bucket.

## Fix

Use `get()` / `putIfAbsent()` instead. Accept duplicate loads on cold start (two threads for the same key both call `loadContext` — idempotent results are safe):

```java
// CORRECT — no bucket lock held during the network call
Optional<C> cached = contextCache.get(actorId);
if (cached == null) {
    Optional<C> loaded = loadContext(actorId);  // HTTP call, no lock held
    Optional<C> racing = contextCache.putIfAbsent(actorId, loaded);
    cached = racing != null ? racing : loaded;
}
```

`putIfAbsent` is atomic but only holds the lock for the map update itself (microseconds), not for the compute function. The trade-off: on cold start, two threads may both call `loadContext` for the same key. The second's result is discarded (the first writer wins). For idempotent loads (same Vault key → same result), this is safe.

## Why this is non-obvious

The name `computeIfAbsent` implies "compute and store if the key is missing" — developers naturally assume the lock scope is narrow (just the map update). The Javadoc recommends "short and simple" but doesn't say "or you block the entire hash bucket." The symptom (unrelated keys blocking each other) is completely surprising. Developers discovering the issue often suspect their HTTP client or Vault server, not the cache implementation.

`computeIfAbsent` is the correct and safe choice when the mapping function is fast and in-memory. The problem is specifically with slow or blocking mapping functions.

### Why this fix

`putIfAbsent` releases the lock before the network call even starts. The duplicate-load race is the accepted trade-off: two threads load the same key concurrently, one result is discarded. For caches where loading is idempotent and deterministic (fetching a public key from a KMS), this is always safe and preferable to blocking the bucket.

entries.title: `synchronized(key.intern())` causes JVM-wide global pool contention across unrelated instances
ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent() + .add() is not atomic with concurrent remove() — futures can be permanently lost

results.query_text: datasource.active|hibernate-orm.active|extension.*deactivat|build.time.*deactivat

entries.title: quarkus.datasource.active=false is runtime-only — Hibernate build step still demands a configured datasource
entries.body:
quarkus.datasource.active=false is runtime-only — Hibernate build step still demands a configured datasource

### Symptom
`io.quarkus.runtime.configuration.ConfigurationException: Datasource '<default>' is not configured` at Quarkus augmentation time, despite setting `quarkus.datasource.active=false` and `quarkus.hibernate-orm.active=false` in `application.properties`.

### Context
You have `@Entity` classes on the classpath (from a Quarkus extension runtime module) and want to boot without any real datasource — e.g. using `@Alternative @Priority(100)` in-memory stores that beat the JPA stores. Setting `datasource.active=false` seems like the right approach.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. `quarkus.datasource.active=false` + `quarkus.hibernate-orm.active=false` — both are runtime properties; the `HibernateOrmProcessor#configurationDescriptorBuilding` build step runs before runtime and demands a datasource when it sees entities.
2. `quarkus.hibernate-orm.enabled=false` — disables entity scanning at build time, but JPA store beans still inject `EntityManager`. With no `EntityManager` bean produced, you get `UnsatisfiedResolutionException`.
3. Excluding JPA stores via `quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.example.repository.jpa.*` — cascades into `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` for any bean that injects the excluded stores (e.g. a `CrossTenantProducer` that injects `CrossTenantWorkItemStore`). The dependency chain is deeper than the direct excludes.

### Root cause
`quarkus.datasource.active` and `quarkus.hibernate-orm.active` are **runtime** properties — they deactivate beans at runtime but do not affect Quarkus augmentation (build-time). The `HibernateOrmProcessor` build step scans for `@Entity` annotations and validates that a matching datasource exists. This happens before any runtime property is evaluated.

### Fix
Provide a dummy H2 datasource that satisfies build-time validation. Disable Flyway so no migrations run. Let Hibernate generate schema from annotations (never used for actual data):

```properties
quarkus.datasource.db-kind=h2
quarkus.datasource.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:mem:dummy;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1
quarkus.hibernate-orm.schema-management.strategy=drop-and-create
quarkus.flyway.enabled=false
```

The `@Alternative @Priority(100)` in-memory stores handle all actual persistence. The H2 datasource exists but carries no data.

### Why this is non-obvious
The property name `quarkus.datasource.active=false` strongly implies "don't use a datasource." A developer would reasonably expect it to work at build time. The distinction between build-time augmentation and runtime configuration is not surfaced in the error message — the exception says "Datasource not configured" without mentioning that `active=false` was set and ignored.

### Why this fix
The dummy datasource approach is the only one that avoids cascading CDI failures. Both `hibernate-orm.enabled=false` and `arc.exclude-types` break the CDI wiring graph in ways that require excluding progressively more beans. The dummy datasource keeps the entire CDI graph valid while letting the alternative stores win at runtime.

### Alternatives considered
- **`hibernate-orm.enabled=false`** — rejected because JPA store beans still inject `EntityManager`, which is absent when Hibernate is disabled. Would require all JPA stores to use `@IfBuildProperty` or similar conditional registration.
- **`arc.exclude-types` on JPA packages** — rejected because the dependency chain cascades. Any service bean that injects a store interface (where the only implementation was the excluded JPA store) fails with `UnsatisfiedResolutionException`.
- **No datasource at all** — not possible while `@Entity` classes are on the classpath and the Hibernate extension is present.

entries.title: quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists
entries.body:
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists

## quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists

**ID:** GE-20260420-45d53b
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.32.2

**What it does:** Setting `quarkus.datasource.reactive=false` in `application.properties` prevents Hibernate Reactive from attempting to acquire a reactive (Vert.x) connection pool at startup, even when `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache` is on the classpath and has registered reactive persistence units at build time.

**Where it works:** Test `application.properties`, per-profile properties, and main `application.properties`.

```properties
# Prevents FastBootHibernateReactivePersistenceProvider from failing
# when no reactive datasource is configured
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
```

### Why non-obvious
Nothing in the Quarkus extension developer docs mentions this property as a reactive-boot suppressor. The reactive extension activates at build time based on classpath scanning; suppressing it at runtime via a datasource flag is counter-intuitive. The natural search path (Hibernate Reactive docs, `@Alternative`, `quarkus.hibernate-reactive.*` properties) doesn't lead here. Found by trial and error.

**See also:** GE-20260420-33b95f (specific H2+test scenario), GE-20260420-daf5dc (optional dep activation gotcha), GE-20260420-dcec35 (extension forces HR boot even with @Alternative)

entries.title: quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache in an extension forces Hibernate Reactive to boot for all consumers — @Alternative does not prevent it
entries.body:
quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache in an extension forces Hibernate Reactive to boot for all consumers — @Alternative does not prevent it

## quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache in an extension forces Hibernate Reactive to boot for all consumers — @Alternative does not prevent it

**ID:** GE-20260420-dcec35
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.32.2, quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache

**Symptom:** Adding `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache` to a Quarkus extension's runtime `pom.xml` causes `FastBootHibernateReactivePersistenceProvider` to try to initialise on every consumer app startup — even those with no reactive datasource configured. Error: `Unable to find datasource '<default>' for persistence unit '<default>': No pool has been defined`.

Three test classes fail that previously passed: the two using `@TestProfile` (which restart Quarkus) and the one with `@TestTransaction` that triggers JTA coordination with all registered persistence units.

### Root cause
The Hibernate Reactive Panache extension scans for `PanacheRepositoryBase` implementations at **build time** (AOT). Any class implementing the interface — even an `@Alternative` bean — causes the extension to register a reactive persistence unit for the associated entity. `@Alternative` suppresses CDI bean activation but does NOT suppress the build-step registration. At runtime, Hibernate Reactive tries to find a reactive (Vert.x) pool for the registered persistence unit and fails if none is configured.

### Fix
Add to the consumer's test `application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
```

This prevents Hibernate Reactive from claiming a reactive pool at runtime, even though the persistence unit was registered at build time.

### Why non-obvious
The error message points to a missing datasource pool — suggesting the fix is to configure a datasource, not to disable reactive. Marking the `PanacheRepositoryBase` bean as `@Alternative` is the obvious CDI workaround and feels correct, but `@Alternative` only controls CDI injection — it has no effect on Quarkus build steps. The fix (`quarkus.datasource.reactive=false`) suppresses the runtime pool lookup entirely, not via CDI.

### Alternatives considered
- `@Alternative` on reactive beans — does not prevent build-step registration; persistence unit still created
- `quarkus.hibernate-reactive.active=false` — property not recognised in Quarkus 3.32.2
- Testcontainers + PostgreSQL — works but adds heavy test infrastructure; unnecessary when H2 suffices

**See also:** GE-20260420-45d53b (fix: quarkus.datasource.reactive=false), GE-20260420-daf5dc (optional dep variant of same issue)

entries.title: Quarkus QuarkusTestProfile.getConfigProfile() REPLACES %test profile — production application.properties becomes active, Dev Services cannot inject URLs
entries.body:
Quarkus QuarkusTestProfile.getConfigProfile() REPLACES %test profile — production application.properties becomes active, Dev Services cannot inject URLs

## Symptom

A `@QuarkusTest` annotated with `@TestProfile` (whose `getConfigProfile()` returns a custom name,
e.g. `"reactive-pg"`) starts the application but either:
- The datasource bean is deactivated: *"Bean is not active: AgroalDataSource — URL is not set"*
- Or the wrong datasource URL is used (e.g., the production H2 file URL rather than the test
  in-memory URL), causing Flyway or Hibernate to fail with a driver mismatch

## Context

Production `application.properties` contains a datasource URL without a profile prefix:

```properties
quarkus.datasource.qhorus.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:file:~/.app/qhorus
```

The test uses `QuarkusTestProfile.getConfigProfile()` returning `"reactive-pg"` to activate
PostgreSQL-specific config. Quarkus Dev Services is expected to provide the PostgreSQL URL.

## What was tried (didn't work)

**Attempt 1** — Set `%reactive-pg.quarkus.datasource.qhorus.jdbc.url=` (empty string) to clear
the H2 URL so Dev Services can activate:

Dev Services does start the container. But Quarkus **deactivates the Agroal datasource bean
at augmentation time** because `jdbc.url` resolves to an empty string. The bean is marked
inactive before Dev Services can inject a replacement at runtime. Error: *"Datasource 'qhorus'
was deactivated automatically because its URL is not set."*

**Attempt 2** — Set `devservices.enabled=true` explicitly without any URL override:

Dev Services starts the container. But the production H2 URL (`jdbc:h2:file:...`) is still
active — Dev Services does **not** override a configured URL even when `devservices.enabled=true`
is set explicitly. Flyway connects to the H2 URL with the PostgreSQL driver → driver mismatch
failure.

## Root cause

`QuarkusTestProfile.getConfigProfile()` **replaces** the `%test` profile entirely — it does
NOT add to it. When `getConfigProfile()` returns `"reactive-pg"`:

- Active config: production `application.properties` (no profile prefix) + `%reactive-pg.*` properties
- **NOT active**: `%test.*` properties (the `%test` profile is replaced, not extended)

This means the production datasource URL (`quarkus.datasource.qhorus.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:file:...`)
is visible, while the test profile's H2 in-memory URL (`%test.quarkus.datasource.qhorus.jdbc.url`)
is not.

**Dev Services config injection priority is lower than profile config.** Dev Services injects
URLs as a late-binding runtime config source. Any explicitly configured URL — whether from
production config, profile config, or `getConfigOverrides()` — takes precedence and blocks
Dev Services injection.

**Empty-string is not "absent".** Setting `jdbc.url=` explicitly tells Quarkus the URL is
configured (to empty). Quarkus deactivates the Agroal bean because the configured URL is
empty. This happens at augmentation time; Dev Services cannot reverse it at runtime.

## Fix

Use `QuarkusTestResourceLifecycleManager.start()` to provide the real JDBC+reactive URLs.
Its return value is applied at the **highest config priority** — above system properties,
profile config, and production config. It is available before Quarkus augmentation.

```java
public class PostgresTestResource implements QuarkusTestResourceLifecycleManager {
    private PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres;

@Override
    public Map<String, String> start() {
        postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:17-alpine")
                .withDatabaseName("mydb").withUsername("user").withPassword("pass");
        postgres.start();
        String reactiveUrl = "vertx-reactive:postgresql://" + postgres.getHost()
                + ":" + postgres.getMappedPort(5432) + "/" + postgres.getDatabaseName();
        return Map.of(
                "quarkus.datasource.qhorus.jdbc.url",     postgres.getJdbcUrl(),
                "quarkus.datasource.qhorus.reactive.url", reactiveUrl,
                "quarkus.datasource.qhorus.username",     postgres.getUsername(),
                "quarkus.datasource.qhorus.password",     postgres.getPassword(),
                "quarkus.flyway.qhorus.migrate-at-start", "true");
    }

@Override
    public void stop() { if (postgres != null) postgres.stop(); }
}
```

Annotate the IT class with `@QuarkusTestResource(value = PostgresTestResource.class, restrictToAnnotatedClass = true)`.

Dependencies needed: `org.testcontainers:testcontainers-postgresql` (version managed by
Quarkus BOM). For reactive calls inside tests, also add `io.quarkus:quarkus-test-vertx`
and use `@RunOnVertxContext + UniAsserter` (Panache.withTransaction() requires a Vert.x
context the JUnit thread does not have).

## Why this is non-obvious

The Quarkus documentation states that test `application.properties` is read for
`@QuarkusTest`. It does not prominently document that `getConfigProfile()` replaces (not
extends) the test profile, making production config active. The natural assumption is that
the test profile properties always apply; they don't when a custom config profile is set.

Dev Services appears to work (the container starts and logs success) but still fails to
inject the URL — the failure is silent until Hibernate/Flyway fails with a confusing
driver mismatch or empty-URL error.

**See also:** GE-20260422-13f53b — `@TestProfile` restarts not inheriting test datasource
config (broader treatment via `getConfigOverrides()`). GE-20260516-e137f6 — `getConfigOverrides()`
replaces `%test.*`-prefixed config for that profile.

entries.title: Quarkus: JPA entity in a dependency artifact forces datasource config on ALL downstream consumers
entries.body:
Quarkus: JPA entity in a dependency artifact forces datasource config on ALL downstream consumers

## Symptom

After adding a module that contains JPA `@Entity` classes as a compile dependency, `@QuarkusTest` classes in downstream modules fail at startup with:

```
ConfigurationException: Datasource '<default>' is not configured.
To solve this, configure datasource '<default>'.
```

This happens even if the downstream module uses only in-memory repository implementations and never interacts with the new dependency's entities.

## Context

Triggered when a module (e.g. `quarkus-ledger`) bundles JPA entity classes in its main artifact. Any module that takes this as a compile dependency — even just to use a utility class like `LedgerTraceIdProvider` — inherits all entity classes on its test classpath. Hibernate ORM's Quarkus extension scans ALL entity classes on the classpath and registers them in the default persistence unit. If no datasource is configured, startup fails.

## What was tried

- Setting `quarkus.flyway.migrate-at-start=false` in test `application.properties` — has no effect because `migrate-at-start` is a build-time fixed property baked into the jar's `META-INF/microprofile-config.properties`.
- Setting `quarkus.hibernate-orm.enabled=false` — disables Hibernate ORM entirely, which breaks test discovery for `@QuarkusTest` classes that depend on any ORM-scoped CDI beans.
- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types` to exclude the problematic beans — does not work for extension-registered beans (registered at augmentation time, not CDI discovery time).

## Root Cause

Quarkus scans the full classpath for `@Entity`-annotated classes at build/augmentation time. Any entity class on the classpath — regardless of which module it came from — is registered in the default persistence unit. There is no built-in scoping mechanism to limit entity discovery to a specific module or jar.

The architectural violation is bundling JPA entity classes alongside domain model/SPI code in a single artifact. The correct pattern: keep domain model (plain POJOs) and JPA entities in separate modules.

## Fix

**Short-term (per affected test module):** Add an H2 datasource configuration to the test `application.properties` so Hibernate can start, plus a `NoOpLedgerEntryRepository` stub (or equivalent) as `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped` to satisfy CDI:

```properties
# application.properties (test)
quarkus.datasource.db-kind=h2
quarkus.datasource.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:mem:testdb;MODE=PostgreSQL;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1
quarkus.datasource.username=sa
quarkus.datasource.password=
quarkus.hibernate-orm.schema-management.strategy=drop-and-create
quarkus.flyway.migrate-at-start=false
```

```java
@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped
class NoOpLedgerEntryRepository implements LedgerEntryRepository {
  // no-op implementations of all methods
}
```

**Long-term (architectural fix):** Split the offending artifact into:
- `<name>-api` — domain POJOs + SPI interfaces only, zero JPA dependency
- `<name>` or `<name>-hibernate` — JPA entity classes + Flyway migrations

Downstream consumers take `<name>-api` only. The `quarkus-work-api` module is the canonical correct example of this split.

## Why this is non-obvious

The error message says "configure datasource" but the root cause is a transitive dependency bundling entity classes. The module you added was specifically chosen for a non-persistence feature (e.g. a trace ID provider). The natural expectation is that only the entities YOU define affect your persistence unit. There is no warning at dependency declaration time that adding a module will force datasource configuration in your tests.

**See also:** GE-20260423-fce720 "quarkus-work-core FilterRule JPA entity requires a datasource — modules using in-memory persistence fail startup"

**See also:** [GE-20260512-a3838e](GE-20260512-a3838e.md) — transitive hibernate-reactive-panache causes H2 test startup failure

entries.title: casehub-qhorus activates quarkus-hibernate-reactive unconditionally — fails with JDBC H2 at startup
entries.body:
casehub-qhorus activates quarkus-hibernate-reactive unconditionally — fails with JDBC H2 at startup

## Symptom

Quarkus fails at startup with an error about a reactive Vert.x pool or reactive datasource not being configured, even though the application only uses JDBC H2 and `casehub.qhorus.reactive.enabled=false` is set.

```
Failed to start quarkus: Reactive datasource 'qhorus' requires a reactive driver
io.quarkus.runtime.QuarkusBindException: ...Vert.x pool...
```

## Context

Any Quarkus application that adds `casehub-qhorus` as a Maven dependency will trigger this failure if it does not provide a reactive datasource. The `casehub.qhorus.reactive.enabled=false` build-time flag exists in qhorus but does **not** suppress the Quarkus hibernate-reactive extension from activating — it only controls qhorus's own reactive messaging layer.

## Root Cause

`casehub-qhorus` has a compile-scope dependency on `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache`. When the deployment JAR of that extension is on the classpath, Quarkus activates the reactive persistence layer unconditionally at augmentation time — before any `@IfBuildProperty` guards in qhorus's own processors can suppress it. The result is that Quarkus tries to create a reactive Vert.x pool for every configured datasource, including the default H2 JDBC datasource, which has no reactive driver.

## Fix

Add these properties to `application.properties` (or `src/test/resources/application.properties` for tests):

```properties
# Suppress reactive datasource activation — casehub-qhorus pulls in
# quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache unconditionally; this prevents Quarkus
# from trying to wire a reactive Vert.x pool to JDBC datasources.
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
quarkus.datasource.qhorus.reactive=false
```

**Do NOT add `casehub.qhorus.reactive.enabled=false`** — this key was removed from the qhorus config model in a later 0.2-SNAPSHOT build. Adding it now causes `ConfigValidationException` at startup, a hard failure with a misleading error message. The two standard Quarkus properties above are sufficient.

## ⚠️ Deprecated property (added 2026-05-17)

`casehub.qhorus.reactive.enabled=false` was a valid workaround in earlier qhorus 0.2-SNAPSHOT builds but was **removed from the qhorus config model** by qhorus 0.2-SNAPSHOT (circa 2026-05). Adding it to `application.properties` now causes:

```
io.smallrye.config.ConfigValidationException: SRCFG00014: The config property casehub.qhorus.reactive.enabled
with the config value 'false' was converted to null from the converter
```

This is a hard startup failure with a misleading error — it looks like a config parsing problem, not a stale property name. Remove this key from any application.properties file where it was previously added.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The error message says "reactive datasource not configured" — pointing at the datasource config, not at qhorus. A developer troubleshooting this naturally adds reactive datasource config rather than suppressing reactive entirely. The qhorus build-time flag (`casehub.qhorus.reactive.enabled`) sounds like it should solve the problem, but it operates at the application layer, not the Quarkus augmentation layer.

### Why this fix

Setting `quarkus.datasource.reactive=false` is the correct Quarkus mechanism to suppress reactive pool creation per datasource. The two-property combination is required because qhorus uses a named datasource (`qhorus`) in addition to the default — both must be suppressed independently.

## Structural Fix

Protocol PP-20260514-f41258 prescribes the correct extension-level fix:
mark `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache` as `<optional>true</optional>` in the
runtime pom; in the deployment processor replace the config flag with
`Capabilities.isPresent(Capability.HIBERNATE_REACTIVE)` as the BooleanSupplier;
produce `ExcludedTypeBuildItem` for reactive classes when capability is absent and
for blocking counterparts when it is present. Classpath presence becomes the single
source of truth — no consumer-side suppression needed.

Tracked as casehub-qhorus#141. Once that ships:
- The two-property workaround is obsolete
- Remove reactive suppression from: claudony app/test, devtown app/test, aml app/test, clinical app/test

**See also:** [GE-20260420-cbd0fa](../quarkus/GE-20260420-cbd0fa.md) — Quarkus Hibernate Reactive @QuarkusTest cannot use H2

entries.title: Detect live JDBC dialect via DatabaseMetaData instead of @ConfigProperty(db-kind) for named-datasource compatibility
entries.body:
Detect live JDBC dialect via DatabaseMetaData instead of @ConfigProperty(db-kind) for named-datasource compatibility

## Detect live JDBC dialect via DatabaseMetaData instead of @ConfigProperty(db-kind)

**ID:** GE-20260616-e04575
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.x, Hibernate ORM, JDBC

### Context

A Quarkus extension supports both H2 (dev/test) and PostgreSQL (production). The extension
must choose between two incompatible SQL branches at runtime. The intuitive approach reads
the `quarkus.datasource.db-kind` config property. This works for the default datasource but
silently breaks when the consumer configures a named datasource (e.g. `casehub.ledger.datasource=myds`):
the named datasource's db-kind lives at `quarkus.datasource."myds".db-kind`, not
`quarkus.datasource.db-kind`, so the property always returns the default `"h2"`.

### Technique

Read the database product name directly from the live JDBC connection via `DatabaseMetaData`.
Cache the result since the database product never changes at runtime:

```java
private volatile Boolean postgresql = null;

private boolean isPostgresql() {
    Boolean result = postgresql;
    if (result == null) {
        result = em.unwrap(Session.class)
                .doReturningWork(conn -> conn.getMetaData()
                        .getDatabaseProductName()
                        .toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)
                        .contains("postgresql"));
        postgresql = result;
    }
    return result;
}
```

This works correctly for both default and named datasources — the `EntityManager` is always
bound to the correct connection regardless of naming.

**Precondition:** must be called inside a `@Transactional` method so the `EntityManager`
has an active connection.

**Thread safety:** `volatile Boolean` single-check idiom is safe for immutable results —
two threads racing on first call both compute the same value and either write wins.

### Alternatives considered

- **`@ConfigProperty(name = "quarkus.datasource.db-kind")`** — works for default datasource only; silently returns `"h2"` default for named datasources, causing wrong SQL dialect to be selected in production.
- **`em.getEntityManagerFactory().unwrap(SessionFactory.class).getJdbcServices().getJdbcEnvironment().getDialect()`** — exposes Hibernate internal APIs that are less stable and require additional unwrapping; also no clear advantage over JDBC metadata.
- **Restructuring to JPQL** — not always possible when dialect-specific SQL is required for correctness (e.g. H2 has no `ON CONFLICT` syntax at all).

### Why this is non-obvious

The `@ConfigProperty` approach is documented, idiomatic, and works in all normal cases.
Named datasource support in Quarkus uses a dynamic config key pattern that isn't
immediately visible — the developer sees the injection succeed (the property has a default)
and never realises the wrong value is being used. The failure only appears in production
when a PostgreSQL SQL statement is executed against an H2 connection, or vice versa.

The `em.unwrap(Session.class).doReturningWork()` API is a Hibernate-internal extension
not mentioned in Quarkus documentation, so most developers would never look there.

entries.title: Transitive hibernate-reactive-panache on classpath causes H2 test startup failure — disable reactive datasource in test config
entries.body:
Transitive hibernate-reactive-panache on classpath causes H2 test startup failure — disable reactive datasource in test config

## Transitive hibernate-reactive-panache on classpath causes H2 test startup failure — disable reactive datasource in test config

### Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` suite fails to start entirely with a datasource configuration error. The module does not use reactive persistence directly. Removing an unrelated dependency fixes the startup failure.

### What was tried (didn't work)

- Removing explicit reactive dependencies from the module's pom — problem persists because the dependency is transitive
- Configuring the reactive datasource URL for H2 — H2 has no native reactive driver; this path has no fix

### Root cause

If any transitive dependency pulls `hibernate-reactive-panache` onto the classpath, Quarkus detects it and attempts to configure a reactive datasource — even when the module itself doesn't use reactive persistence. H2 has no native reactive driver, so startup fails with a datasource configuration error.

### Fix

Add this to `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
%test.quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
```

This disables reactive datasource configuration for the test profile only, without affecting production configuration.

### Why this is non-obvious

The module doesn't use reactive persistence — the failure appears unrelated to reactive at all. The root cause is a transitive classpath entry, not the module's own dependencies. The error message points to datasource configuration, not to the reactive extension, making the connection to `hibernate-reactive-panache` non-obvious.

## Platform-Specific Fix

The casehub-qhorus instance of this gotcha is structurally fixed by
PP-20260514-f41258 (casehub-qhorus#141) — see GE-20260508-492336 for detail.
This general gotcha remains valid for any other project with a transitive
quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache dep not yet addressed by the protocol.

**See also:** [GE-20260429-21e6cf](GE-20260429-21e6cf.md) — JPA entity in dependency forces datasource config on all downstream consumers

**See also:** [GE-20260519-f0967f](GE-20260519-f0967f.md) — Quarkus reactive SPI test shim: resolve Uni injections without a Vert.x datasource

**See also:** [GE-20260605-e42cc5](GE-20260605-e42cc5.md) — @Blocking on plain @ApplicationScoped method causes Quarkus test classloader failure (different root cause, same symptom class)

entries.title: quarkus.datasource.active=false is runtime-only — Hibernate build step still demands a configured datasource
entries.body:
quarkus.datasource.active=false is runtime-only — Hibernate build step still demands a configured datasource

### Symptom
`io.quarkus.runtime.configuration.ConfigurationException: Datasource '<default>' is not configured` at Quarkus augmentation time, despite setting `quarkus.datasource.active=false` and `quarkus.hibernate-orm.active=false` in `application.properties`.

### Context
You have `@Entity` classes on the classpath (from a Quarkus extension runtime module) and want to boot without any real datasource — e.g. using `@Alternative @Priority(100)` in-memory stores that beat the JPA stores. Setting `datasource.active=false` seems like the right approach.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. `quarkus.datasource.active=false` + `quarkus.hibernate-orm.active=false` — both are runtime properties; the `HibernateOrmProcessor#configurationDescriptorBuilding` build step runs before runtime and demands a datasource when it sees entities.
2. `quarkus.hibernate-orm.enabled=false` — disables entity scanning at build time, but JPA store beans still inject `EntityManager`. With no `EntityManager` bean produced, you get `UnsatisfiedResolutionException`.
3. Excluding JPA stores via `quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.example.repository.jpa.*` — cascades into `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` for any bean that injects the excluded stores (e.g. a `CrossTenantProducer` that injects `CrossTenantWorkItemStore`). The dependency chain is deeper than the direct excludes.

### Root cause
`quarkus.datasource.active` and `quarkus.hibernate-orm.active` are **runtime** properties — they deactivate beans at runtime but do not affect Quarkus augmentation (build-time). The `HibernateOrmProcessor` build step scans for `@Entity` annotations and validates that a matching datasource exists. This happens before any runtime property is evaluated.

### Fix
Provide a dummy H2 datasource that satisfies build-time validation. Disable Flyway so no migrations run. Let Hibernate generate schema from annotations (never used for actual data):

```properties
quarkus.datasource.db-kind=h2
quarkus.datasource.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:mem:dummy;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1
quarkus.hibernate-orm.schema-management.strategy=drop-and-create
quarkus.flyway.enabled=false
```

The `@Alternative @Priority(100)` in-memory stores handle all actual persistence. The H2 datasource exists but carries no data.

### Why this is non-obvious
The property name `quarkus.datasource.active=false` strongly implies "don't use a datasource." A developer would reasonably expect it to work at build time. The distinction between build-time augmentation and runtime configuration is not surfaced in the error message — the exception says "Datasource not configured" without mentioning that `active=false` was set and ignored.

### Why this fix
The dummy datasource approach is the only one that avoids cascading CDI failures. Both `hibernate-orm.enabled=false` and `arc.exclude-types` break the CDI wiring graph in ways that require excluding progressively more beans. The dummy datasource keeps the entire CDI graph valid while letting the alternative stores win at runtime.

### Alternatives considered
- **`hibernate-orm.enabled=false`** — rejected because JPA store beans still inject `EntityManager`, which is absent when Hibernate is disabled. Would require all JPA stores to use `@IfBuildProperty` or similar conditional registration.
- **`arc.exclude-types` on JPA packages** — rejected because the dependency chain cascades. Any service bean that injects a store interface (where the only implementation was the excluded JPA store) fails with `UnsatisfiedResolutionException`.
- **No datasource at all** — not possible while `@Entity` classes are on the classpath and the Hibernate extension is present.

entries.title: Quarkus: JPA entity in a dependency artifact forces datasource config on ALL downstream consumers
entries.body:
Quarkus: JPA entity in a dependency artifact forces datasource config on ALL downstream consumers

## Symptom

After adding a module that contains JPA `@Entity` classes as a compile dependency, `@QuarkusTest` classes in downstream modules fail at startup with:

```
ConfigurationException: Datasource '<default>' is not configured.
To solve this, configure datasource '<default>'.
```

This happens even if the downstream module uses only in-memory repository implementations and never interacts with the new dependency's entities.

## Context

Triggered when a module (e.g. `quarkus-ledger`) bundles JPA entity classes in its main artifact. Any module that takes this as a compile dependency — even just to use a utility class like `LedgerTraceIdProvider` — inherits all entity classes on its test classpath. Hibernate ORM's Quarkus extension scans ALL entity classes on the classpath and registers them in the default persistence unit. If no datasource is configured, startup fails.

## What was tried

- Setting `quarkus.flyway.migrate-at-start=false` in test `application.properties` — has no effect because `migrate-at-start` is a build-time fixed property baked into the jar's `META-INF/microprofile-config.properties`.
- Setting `quarkus.hibernate-orm.enabled=false` — disables Hibernate ORM entirely, which breaks test discovery for `@QuarkusTest` classes that depend on any ORM-scoped CDI beans.
- `quarkus.arc.exclude-types` to exclude the problematic beans — does not work for extension-registered beans (registered at augmentation time, not CDI discovery time).

## Root Cause

Quarkus scans the full classpath for `@Entity`-annotated classes at build/augmentation time. Any entity class on the classpath — regardless of which module it came from — is registered in the default persistence unit. There is no built-in scoping mechanism to limit entity discovery to a specific module or jar.

The architectural violation is bundling JPA entity classes alongside domain model/SPI code in a single artifact. The correct pattern: keep domain model (plain POJOs) and JPA entities in separate modules.

## Fix

**Short-term (per affected test module):** Add an H2 datasource configuration to the test `application.properties` so Hibernate can start, plus a `NoOpLedgerEntryRepository` stub (or equivalent) as `@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped` to satisfy CDI:

```properties
# application.properties (test)
quarkus.datasource.db-kind=h2
quarkus.datasource.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:mem:testdb;MODE=PostgreSQL;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1
quarkus.datasource.username=sa
quarkus.datasource.password=
quarkus.hibernate-orm.schema-management.strategy=drop-and-create
quarkus.flyway.migrate-at-start=false
```

```java
@Alternative @Priority(1) @ApplicationScoped
class NoOpLedgerEntryRepository implements LedgerEntryRepository {
  // no-op implementations of all methods
}
```

**Long-term (architectural fix):** Split the offending artifact into:
- `<name>-api` — domain POJOs + SPI interfaces only, zero JPA dependency
- `<name>` or `<name>-hibernate` — JPA entity classes + Flyway migrations

Downstream consumers take `<name>-api` only. The `quarkus-work-api` module is the canonical correct example of this split.

## Why this is non-obvious

The error message says "configure datasource" but the root cause is a transitive dependency bundling entity classes. The module you added was specifically chosen for a non-persistence feature (e.g. a trace ID provider). The natural expectation is that only the entities YOU define affect your persistence unit. There is no warning at dependency declaration time that adding a module will force datasource configuration in your tests.

**See also:** GE-20260423-fce720 "quarkus-work-core FilterRule JPA entity requires a datasource — modules using in-memory persistence fail startup"

**See also:** [GE-20260512-a3838e](GE-20260512-a3838e.md) — transitive hibernate-reactive-panache causes H2 test startup failure

entries.title: quarkus-work-core FilterRule JPA entity requires a datasource — modules using in-memory persistence fail startup
entries.body:
quarkus-work-core FilterRule JPA entity requires a datasource — modules using in-memory persistence fail startup

## Symptom

Quarkus startup fails in a module that uses in-memory persistence (no JDBC datasource):

```
ConfigurationException: Datasource '<default>' is not configured.
Persistence unit '<default>' defines entities [io.quarkiverse.work.core.filter.FilterRule],
but its datasource '<default>' cannot be found.
```

## Context

Occurs when `quarkus-work-core` is a transitive dependency (e.g. via `engine` module)
in a Quarkus test module that does not configure a datasource. `FilterRule` is a
`@Entity` (Hibernate ORM Panache) in quarkus-work-core and is discovered by Quarkus's
CDI/Hibernate scanning across all modules on the classpath.

## Root cause

`quarkus-work-core` bundles a `FilterRule` JPA entity and declares Hibernate ORM Panache
as a runtime dependency. Any module that transitively depends on `quarkus-work-core`
inherits the entity scanning, requiring a configured datasource even when the module
has no persistence of its own.

## Fix

Add to the affected module's test `application.properties`:

```properties
# Disable Hibernate ORM so quarkus-work-core's FilterRule entity
# does not require a datasource in modules using in-memory persistence.
quarkus.hibernate-orm.enabled=false
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The module didn't directly depend on `quarkus-work-core` — it arrived as a transitive
dependency through `engine`. The error message names `FilterRule` but gives no indication
of which JAR introduced it or why a datasource is needed in a module that never configured one.

**See also:** GE-20260429-21e6cf "Quarkus: JPA entity in a dependency artifact forces datasource config on ALL downstream consumers"

**See also:** [GE-20260513-74dc72](../casehub-work/GE-20260513-74dc72.md) — casehub-work requires io.casehub.work.runtime.filter in Hibernate scan packages

**See also:** [GE-20260618-08cb96](GE-20260618-08cb96.md) — Named Hibernate ORM PU requires both @PersistenceUnit qualifier AND packages config

entries.title: quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache in an extension forces Hibernate Reactive to boot for all consumers — @Alternative does not prevent it
entries.body:
quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache in an extension forces Hibernate Reactive to boot for all consumers — @Alternative does not prevent it

## quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache in an extension forces Hibernate Reactive to boot for all consumers — @Alternative does not prevent it

**ID:** GE-20260420-dcec35
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.32.2, quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache

**Symptom:** Adding `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache` to a Quarkus extension's runtime `pom.xml` causes `FastBootHibernateReactivePersistenceProvider` to try to initialise on every consumer app startup — even those with no reactive datasource configured. Error: `Unable to find datasource '<default>' for persistence unit '<default>': No pool has been defined`.

Three test classes fail that previously passed: the two using `@TestProfile` (which restart Quarkus) and the one with `@TestTransaction` that triggers JTA coordination with all registered persistence units.

### Root cause
The Hibernate Reactive Panache extension scans for `PanacheRepositoryBase` implementations at **build time** (AOT). Any class implementing the interface — even an `@Alternative` bean — causes the extension to register a reactive persistence unit for the associated entity. `@Alternative` suppresses CDI bean activation but does NOT suppress the build-step registration. At runtime, Hibernate Reactive tries to find a reactive (Vert.x) pool for the registered persistence unit and fails if none is configured.

### Fix
Add to the consumer's test `application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
```

This prevents Hibernate Reactive from claiming a reactive pool at runtime, even though the persistence unit was registered at build time.

### Why non-obvious
The error message points to a missing datasource pool — suggesting the fix is to configure a datasource, not to disable reactive. Marking the `PanacheRepositoryBase` bean as `@Alternative` is the obvious CDI workaround and feels correct, but `@Alternative` only controls CDI injection — it has no effect on Quarkus build steps. The fix (`quarkus.datasource.reactive=false`) suppresses the runtime pool lookup entirely, not via CDI.

### Alternatives considered
- `@Alternative` on reactive beans — does not prevent build-step registration; persistence unit still created
- `quarkus.hibernate-reactive.active=false` — property not recognised in Quarkus 3.32.2
- Testcontainers + PostgreSQL — works but adds heavy test infrastructure; unnecessary when H2 suffices

**See also:** GE-20260420-45d53b (fix: quarkus.datasource.reactive=false), GE-20260420-daf5dc (optional dep variant of same issue)

entries.title: Quarkus quarkus.hibernate-orm.<pu-name>.sql-load-script for named PU — import.sql only runs for the default PU
entries.body:
Quarkus quarkus.hibernate-orm.<pu-name>.sql-load-script for named PU — import.sql only runs for the default PU

### Symptom
Native SQL tables (not JPA entities) are missing after `database.generation=drop-and-create` even though `import.sql` exists in `src/test/resources`. The table was expected to be created by the SQL script.

### Context
A test uses a named Hibernate ORM persistence unit (e.g. `quarkus`) configured via `quarkus.hibernate-orm.qhorus.datasource=qhorus`. After switching to `drop-and-create` schema generation, a native SQL table (`ledger_subject_sequence`) that exists in a Flyway migration is no longer created — and `import.sql` doesn't create it either.

### Root cause
In Quarkus, `import.sql` is executed **only for the default (unnamed) persistence unit**. Named persistence units require a separate config property:

```properties
quarkus.hibernate-orm.<pu-name>.sql-load-script=<filename>.sql
```

The Quarkus docs mention `import.sql` for the default PU but do not prominently document the named-PU equivalent.

### Fix
Create a separate SQL file and configure it for the named PU:

```properties
# For the default PU — import.sql works automatically
# For a named PU (e.g. "qhorus"):
%test.quarkus.hibernate-orm.qhorus.sql-load-script=import-qhorus.sql
```

Place `import-qhorus.sql` in `src/test/resources/`:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ledger_subject_sequence (
    subject_id UUID NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    next_seq   INT  NOT NULL DEFAULT 1
);
```

### Why this is non-obvious
`import.sql` is the well-known Hibernate convention for post-schema SQL. Quarkus follows this convention for the default PU but uses a different mechanism for named PUs. The property name (`sql-load-script`) is documented in the Quarkus Hibernate ORM reference but not linked from the `import.sql` section, so developers naturally expect `import.sql` to apply universally.

**See also:** [GE-20260521-2b82e7](GE-20260521-2b82e7.md) — Panache.withTransaction() uses the default persistence unit silently | [GE-20260512-ea776c](GE-20260512-ea776c.md) — Quarkus named persistence units silently skip schema generation

**See also:** [GE-20260618-08cb96](GE-20260618-08cb96.md) — Named Hibernate ORM PU requires both @PersistenceUnit qualifier AND packages config

entries.title: quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists
entries.body:
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists

## quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists

**ID:** GE-20260420-45d53b
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.32.2

**What it does:** Setting `quarkus.datasource.reactive=false` in `application.properties` prevents Hibernate Reactive from attempting to acquire a reactive (Vert.x) connection pool at startup, even when `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache` is on the classpath and has registered reactive persistence units at build time.

**Where it works:** Test `application.properties`, per-profile properties, and main `application.properties`.

```properties
# Prevents FastBootHibernateReactivePersistenceProvider from failing
# when no reactive datasource is configured
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
```

### Why non-obvious
Nothing in the Quarkus extension developer docs mentions this property as a reactive-boot suppressor. The reactive extension activates at build time based on classpath scanning; suppressing it at runtime via a datasource flag is counter-intuitive. The natural search path (Hibernate Reactive docs, `@Alternative`, `quarkus.hibernate-reactive.*` properties) doesn't lead here. Found by trial and error.

**See also:** GE-20260420-33b95f (specific H2+test scenario), GE-20260420-daf5dc (optional dep activation gotcha), GE-20260420-dcec35 (extension forces HR boot even with @Alternative)

entries.title: Hibernate 6 SessionFactoryObserverForNamedQueryValidation throws at boot for schema-drifted JARs — quarkus property overrides don't suppress it
entries.body:
Hibernate 6 SessionFactoryObserverForNamedQueryValidation throws at boot for schema-drifted JARs — quarkus property overrides don't suppress it

## Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` fails at boot with:

```
jakarta.persistence.PersistenceException: Unable to build Hibernate SessionFactory
Caused by: org.hibernate.query.NamedQueryValidationException: Errors in named queries:
  Suppressed: org.hibernate.query.sqm.UnknownPathException:
    Could not resolve attribute 'scopeKey' of 'io.casehub.ledger.runtime.model.ActorTrustScore'
```

The error also appears as `ERROR` log lines before the exception:
```
HHH90003001: Error in named query: ActorTrustScore.findGlobalByActorId:
  org.hibernate.query.sqm.UnknownPathException: Could not resolve attribute 'scopeKey'
```

Hibernate throws this at `sessionFactoryCreated()` time — before any test runs.

## Root Cause

A cached `casehub-ledger:0.2-SNAPSHOT` JAR contains `@NamedQuery` annotations on `ActorTrustScore` that reference a field (`scopeKey`) not yet present on the entity class. This happens when the `@NamedQuery` strings were committed to the repo but the corresponding entity field wasn't added yet (or vice versa), and the local Maven cache holds a stale artifact version.

Hibernate 6 validates ALL named queries at `SessionFactory` build time via `SessionFactoryObserverForNamedQueryValidation` — a Quarkus Hibernate ORM extension class. Unlike Hibernate 5, there is no documented configuration key to skip this validation.

**Attempts that don't work:**
- `quarkus.hibernate-orm.validate-named-query=false` — not a valid Quarkus config key
- `quarkus.hibernate-orm.unsupported-properties."hibernate.query.validate_named_queries"=false` — also ineffective; the Quarkus observer runs after Hibernate's own validation hooks

## Fix

Install the affected artifact from local source:

```bash
mvn install -DskipTests -q --batch-mode -f /path/to/ledger/pom.xml
```

This overwrites the stale SNAPSHOT JAR in `~/.m2/` with a consistent version where named queries and entity fields match.

If local source is unavailable, force Maven to re-download the SNAPSHOT:
```bash
mvn dependency:resolve -U  # -U forces SNAPSHOT refresh
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

1. The error log says `HHH90003001` and mentions a named query — nothing suggests the problem is a stale JAR artifact. A developer investigates entity mappings, not Maven cache consistency.
2. The entity class on disk is correct; the problem is inside the compiled JAR from cache. Reading source code shows no issue.
3. The two Quarkus/Hibernate property overrides that should disable validation are both silently ignored — the validation happens in a Quarkus extension observer, not in Hibernate's standard configuration layer.
4. Tests pass on CI (which always downloads fresh artifacts) but fail locally on cached SNAPSHOTs — intermittent environment-specific failure with no clear signal.

entries.title: quarkus.mcp.server.tools.page-size controls tools/list pagination — undocumented default of 50 silently truncates large tool sets
entries.body:
quarkus.mcp.server.tools.page-size controls tools/list pagination — undocumented default of 50 silently truncates large tool sets

## `quarkus.mcp.server.tools.page-size` controls tools/list pagination — undocumented default of 50 silently truncates large tool sets

### Stack
quarkus-mcp-server 1.11.1 (Quarkiverse)

### What it is
`quarkus.mcp.server.tools.page-size` is a runtime configuration property in quarkus-mcp-server that controls how many tools are returned per `tools/list` MCP response. It is defined in `McpServerRuntimeConfig.java` as a nested interface:

```java
public interface Tools {
    @WithDefault("50")
    int pageSize();
    // ...
}
```

### How to use it

**Disable pagination** (return all tools in one response):
```properties
quarkus.mcp.server.tools.page-size=0
```

**Set a custom page size:**
```properties
quarkus.mcp.server.tools.page-size=100
```

Setting to 0 or any negative number disables pagination entirely — all registered tools are returned in a single `tools/list` response regardless of count.

### Where it's not documented
As of quarkus-mcp-server 1.11.1, this property does not appear in the main Quarkiverse documentation for the extension. It is only discoverable by:
- Reading the `McpServerRuntimeConfig.java` source
- Noticing that `tools/list` returns fewer tools than expected (no warning is emitted)
- Decompiling the deployment JAR

### Discovery path
Found by: diagnosing silent tool truncation (49 Qhorus + 8 Claudony = 57 total tools, response showing exactly 50 alphabetically-sorted tools). Traced through `ToolMessageHandler` → `fetchPage()` → `serverConfig.tools().pageSize()` → `@WithDefault("50")` in `McpServerRuntimeConfig`.

### Related config
The same nested config likely controls pagination for other MCP feature types (prompts, resources) via similar `pageSize()` methods. The naming convention is `quarkus.mcp.server.<feature-type>.page-size`.

**See also:** GE-20260501-50a9f4 — quarkus-mcp-server silently caps tools/list at 50 | GE-20260414-fa6489 — @ToolArg undocumented required and defaultValue attributes

entries.title: Synthesised delegation methods need explicit `public` when overriding interface methods
Parallel agents with domain-split substitution tables for large-scale consistent project-wide renames
entries.body:
Parallel agents with domain-split substitution tables for large-scale consistent project-wide renames

## What Developers Normally Do

Write sed scripts or use IDE bulk find-replace to rename project-wide symbols.
For renames with exclusion rules (e.g. "rename our package prefix but not
third-party deps that share the same prefix"), sed scripts require complex
negative lookaheads and still miss contextual distinctions. IDE replace-all
has no concept of "ignore this pattern when it appears in a dependency
declaration."

## The Technique

Split files by domain concern and dispatch one parallel agent per domain,
each given an explicit substitution table and explicit "DO NOT change" rules:

```
Agent 1: Java source files
  — substitution table: quarkus-ledger → casehub-ledger, etc.
  — exclusion rule: do NOT change io.quarkiverse.X where X is not our extension

Agent 2: Core documentation (DESIGN.md, integration-guide.md, etc.)
  — same substitution table

Agent 3: Historical plan/spec docs, root files, HANDOFF
  — same substitution table

Agent 4: Blog entries (frontmatter), examples, settings files
  — same substitution table
```

Use `isolation: "worktree"` on each agent. Changes auto-commit and merge back
into main. Run a final grep verification pass to catch any stragglers.

```
Agent({ isolation: "worktree", prompt: """
  Fix these exact substitutions in Java files only:
    quarkus-ledger → casehub-ledger
    io.quarkiverse.ledger → io.casehub.ledger (ONLY when it's our package, NOT third-party)
  Exclusion: do NOT change io.quarkiverse.opentelemetry or any other third-party quarkiverse lib.
  Final verification: grep to confirm zero remaining matches.
"""})
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The instinct is to write sed scripts or use IDE bulk replace. Three things
make the agent approach superior for renames with exclusion rules:

1. **Contextual exclusion** — agents read the surrounding code and can
   distinguish `io.quarkiverse.ledger` (our old package) from
   `io.quarkiverse.opentelemetry` (a third-party dep). sed requires brittle
   negative lookaheads that break on unforeseen patterns.

2. **Domain splitting** — splitting by file type (Java / docs / plans / blog)
   rather than by feature lets each agent operate on a homogeneous set with
   a consistent grammar, reducing false positives.

3. **Implicit validation** — asking each agent to run a final grep
   verification at the end catches its own misses without a separate pass.

The parallel dispatch (single message, four Agent tool calls) keeps the total
elapsed time similar to a single sed pass, while producing more reliable
results on a 60+ file rename with non-trivial exclusion rules.

**See also:** GE-0166 (parallel agents for codebase comparison), GE-20260420-374a25 (parallel implementation subagents for disjoint tasks)

**See also:** [GE-20260420-374a25](GE-20260420-374a25.md) — dispatch parallel implementation subagents within subagent-driven-development

entries.title: WEIGHTED_MAJORITY attestation aggregation silently masks contradicting verdicts on the same entry
entries.body:
WEIGHTED_MAJORITY attestation aggregation silently masks contradicting verdicts on the same entry

### Symptom
Capability trust scores remain unchanged (e.g. 11/12 = 0.917) regardless of how many FLAGGED attestations are written against an actor's decisions. Investigation naturally targets the query layer, computation logic, or score persistence — all of which are correct.

### Context
Bayesian Beta trust scoring with a four-pass pipeline (capability → dimension → cap×dim → global). Attestations are aggregated per (entryId, capabilityTag) group before scoring. An incident feedback system writes a FLAGGED attestation against an existing entry that already holds a SOUND attestation from the original review.

### What was tried (didn't work)
- Checked query layer (findAllEvents, findAttestationsForEntries) — fetches all verdicts correctly
- Checked TrustScoreComputer.compute() — handles FLAGGED by incrementing beta correctly
- Checked PerActorTrustComputer delegation — passes data through unchanged
- Checked JPA upsert — updates existing score rows correctly
- Checked TrustScoreSource/TrustGateService — reads directly from repository
- All unit tests with FLAGGED on separate entries pass

### Root cause
`TrustScoreCalculator.computeAll()` runs `buildEffectiveAttestations()` which groups attestations by (entryId, capabilityTag) and aggregates via `AttestationAggregator.weightedMajority()`. When an entry has SOUND(confidence=1.0) + FLAGGED(confidence=0.9):
- positive = 1.0, negative = 0.9
- `positive >= negative` → verdict = SOUND
- confidence = |1.0 - 0.9| / 1.9 ≈ 0.053
- Weight in compute() = decayWeight × 0.053 ≈ 0 → neither alpha nor beta increment meaningfully

The FLAGGED attestation is not ignored — it is aggregated into a near-zero-weight SOUND, which is worse: it cancels the original SOUND's contribution too.

### Fix
Use raw attestations (not aggregated) for the capability pass. Each individual attestation contributes to alpha/beta independently — SOUND increments alpha, FLAGGED increments beta, both on the same entry. The dimension pass already used raw attestations; this brings the capability pass into alignment.

```java
// Before (broken): used effectiveAttestations (pre-aggregated)
final Map<String, Map<UUID, List<LedgerAttestation>>> byCapabilityAndEntry =
        effectiveAttestations.stream()
                .filter(a -> !CapabilityTag.GLOBAL.equals(a.capabilityTag))
                ...

// After (fixed): uses rawAttestations
final Map<String, Map<UUID, List<LedgerAttestation>>> byCapabilityAndEntry =
        rawAttestations.stream()
                .filter(a -> a.capabilityTag != null
                        && !CapabilityTag.GLOBAL.equals(a.capabilityTag))
                ...
```

Note the added null guard on `capabilityTag` — raw attestations don't pass through `buildEffectiveAttestations` which normalizes nulls to GLOBAL.

### Why this is non-obvious
The aggregation step sits between data fetch and computation. Both endpoints work correctly in isolation. The symptom (score unchanged) points at the computation or query layer. The aggregation is designed to reduce noise from multiple attestors — it silently converts the sequential-evidence case (SOUND then FLAGGED over time) into the concurrent-vote case (competing opinions) without any log message or signal.

### Why this fix
Raw attestations are the right model for capability scoring because attestations on the same entry arrive at different times as sequential evidence (review outcome, then incident feedback), not as concurrent competing votes. The aggregation model was designed for the N-attestors-same-time case. Trade-off: N attestors on the same entry now contribute N× weight instead of 1× consensus weight — an idea has been logged to explore a hybrid approach.

### Alternatives considered
- Changing the WEIGHTED_MAJORITY tie-break from `>=` to `>` — still produces SOUND for the 1.0 vs 0.9 case; doesn't fix the core issue
- Adding a temporal-awareness flag to the aggregator — overengineered for the current use case; the raw-attestation approach is simpler and matches the dimension pass
- Processing attestations individually with no aggregation anywhere — would affect the global pass which intentionally uses aggregation via GlobalScoreStrategy SPI

entries.title: Declare ARC42STORIES.MD as primary architecture record at bootstrap — LAYER-LOG.md becomes the source-of-truth draft
entries.body:
Declare ARC42STORIES.MD as primary architecture record at bootstrap — LAYER-LOG.md becomes the source-of-truth draft

When a CaseHub harness app bootstraps `ARC42STORIES.MD`, update `CLAUDE.md` in the same commit to declare `ARC42STORIES.MD` as the primary architecture record and `LAYER-LOG.md` as the source-of-truth draft that feeds it. Also add redirect headers to `docs/DESIGN.md` and workspace `DESIGN.md` pointing to `ARC42STORIES.MD` sections. Do not retire or delete `LAYER-LOG.md` until `ARC42STORIES.MD` has been reviewed and verified complete against all source documents (quality gate: `arc42stories-post-generation-quality-gate.md`). The reference implementation is `casehubio/clinical#54` commit `581b439`.

entries.title: Scan Java class bytecode in a JAR for annotation presence using Python zipfile + constant-pool string search
entries.body:
Scan Java class bytecode in a JAR for annotation presence using Python zipfile + constant-pool string search

## Technique

When you need to verify whether a class in a compiled JAR carries a specific
annotation (e.g. `@Alternative`, `@Default`, `@ApplicationScoped`) without
access to source code or a Java decompiler:

```python
import zipfile

jar = '/path/to/library.jar'
with zipfile.ZipFile(jar) as z:
    data = z.read('com/example/MyClass.class')
    if b'Alternative' in data:
        print('Has @Alternative')
    if b'Default' in data:
        print('Has @Default')
```

The annotation type names are stored as UTF-8 constants in the class file constant
pool. A simple `bytes in bytes` search is sufficient for presence detection —
no bytecode parser needed.

## Extended use: list all internal string constants

To see everything a class references (useful for understanding delegation chains):

```python
import zipfile

jar = '/path/to/library.jar'
with zipfile.ZipFile(jar) as z:
    data = z.read('com/example/MyClass.class')
    strings = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(data):
        if data[i:i+1] == b'\x01':  # CONSTANT_Utf8 tag
            length = (data[i+1] << 8) | data[i+2]
            s = data[i+3:i+3+length]
            try:
                decoded = s.decode('utf-8')
                if len(decoded) > 3 and '/' in decoded:
                    strings.append(decoded)
            except:
                pass
            i += 3 + length
        else:
            i += 1
    for s in sorted(set(strings)):
        print(s)
```

This reveals method signatures, type references, and field descriptors — enough
to map a class's full dependency graph from bytecode alone.

## Why this is non-obvious

The standard approach for inspecting class metadata is `javap -verbose`, which
requires extracting the class file from the JAR first and running a separate JDK
tool. The Python `zipfile` approach works directly on the JAR without any Java
toolchain present, and is scriptable in the same session that needs the answer.
It's particularly useful when working with SNAPSHOT JARs that have no published
source or Javadoc.

**See also:** [GE-20260601-ad3154](GE-20260601-ad3154.md) — scan Java class bytecode in a JAR for annotation presence using Python zipfile + constant-pool string search

entries.title: ARC42STORIES.MD lives in the project repo, not the workspace
entries.body:
ARC42STORIES.MD lives in the project repo, not the workspace

ARC42STORIES.MD is the architecture record for a CaseHub application — equivalent to DESIGN.md before the migration. Like DESIGN.md, it belongs in the project repo root (e.g. `casehubio/devtown`), not the workspace. The workspace holds methodology artifacts (handovers, blog, plans, snapshots); architectural documentation belongs alongside the code where contributors can read it. When the migration from DESIGN.md to ARC42STORIES.MD happened, several repos accidentally placed the file in the workspace — audit with: find the file under `~/claude/casehub/<repo>/` (project) vs `~/claude/public/casehub/<repo>/` (workspace).

entries.title: Run three-check quality sweep before closing ARC42STORIES.MD migration issue
entries.body:
Run three-check quality sweep before closing ARC42STORIES.MD migration issue

After generating ARC42STORIES.MD from LAYER-LOG.md, three checks are mandatory before closing the migration issue. (1) Run `gh issue view N --repo casehubio/REPO` for every issue reference in §12 — closed issues must be removed from Active Risks; (2) Run `find . -name "ClassName.java"` for every class in §9.4 Key files sections — class names from design docs may not exist in production; (3) Open each service implementation and verify CDI annotations match the document — `@ApplicationScoped` may have evolved to `@Alternative @Priority(N)` when competing implementations were introduced. The devtown migration found 10+ documentation errors using these three checks. See garden entries GE-20260601-b0eabf, GE-20260601-c09f71, GE-20260601-85afd0 for the specific gotchas each check catches.

results.query_text: In-memory store aggregate methods must not delegate to scan because scan applies pagination which silently truncates aggregation results

entries.title: Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries
entries.body:
Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries

AE memory entries written to the PATIENT domain must carry `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` (CTCAE grade name, e.g. "GRADE_3") and `MemoryAttributeKeys.OUTCOME` (lifecycle status: "REPORTED", "ESCALATED", "DSMB_ESCALATED") as **separate attributes**. Conflating them — storing `grade.name()` as `OUTCOME` — breaks `hasPriorGrade3OrAbove()` for escalation entries and produces a facts map without a `grade` key, violating the spec contract at `.patientContext.facts[].grade`. The `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` key is the canonical way to carry CTCAE grade through the memory store; `OUTCOME` is always a lifecycle or resolution status, never a grade.
