entries.title: @LookupIfProperty for conditional CDI bean activation via Instance<> — cleaner than inert-bean pattern
entries.body:
@LookupIfProperty for conditional CDI bean activation via Instance<> — cleaner than inert-bean pattern

### Context
A Quarkus application ships multiple provider implementations of an SPI (e.g. `DeviceProvider`) in one Docker image. Each provider should be independently enableable via config (`provider.enabled=true/false`). Disabled providers must be invisible to `Instance<DeviceProvider>` iteration — not just "present but returning empty results."

### What a skilled developer would normally do
Guard in `@PostConstruct`: check `enabled`, skip initialization if false. Make every method return a no-op result (`discover()` → empty list, `dispatch()` → FAILED, `status()` → DISCONNECTED). This is the "inert-bean" pattern — the bean exists but does nothing.

Problems with inert beans:
- The bean IS discovered by `Instance<DeviceProvider>` — callers see a broken provider
- `@PostConstruct` still runs (and may fail if config properties are missing)
- Every method needs guard logic
- SmallRye Config still validates all `@ConfigMapping` properties at startup (see GE-20260626-e0ecaf)

### Technique
Use `@LookupIfProperty` from Quarkus ARC:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@LookupIfProperty(name = "casehub.iot.homeassistant.enabled", stringValue = "true")
public class HomeAssistantProvider implements DeviceProvider { ... }
```

When `enabled` is absent or `false`:
- The bean is never instantiated
- `@PostConstruct` never runs
- `Instance<DeviceProvider>` iteration skips it entirely
- No guard logic needed in any method

Combined with `Optional<String>` on all `@ConfigMapping` properties (GE-20260626-e0ecaf), this allows disabled providers to exist on the classpath without causing config validation failures or appearing in the provider registry.

### Why this is non-obvious
`@LookupIfProperty` is less well-known than `@IfBuildProperty` (which is build-time only and prevents runtime reconfiguration). The naming — "lookup if" vs "if build" — doesn't clearly convey the difference. Many developers reach for `@IfBuildProperty` first, discover it prevents runtime toggling in Docker images, and then fall back to the inert-bean pattern instead of finding `@LookupIfProperty`.

entries.title: LangChain4j EmbeddingSearchRequest.filter() pushes domain/metadata filters into Qdrant pre-scoring
entries.body:
LangChain4j EmbeddingSearchRequest.filter() pushes domain/metadata filters into Qdrant pre-scoring

## LangChain4j EmbeddingSearchRequest.filter() pushes domain/metadata filters into Qdrant pre-scoring

**Stack:** LangChain4j 1.x, Qdrant

Pass a `Filter` to `EmbeddingSearchRequest.builder().filter()` to apply a metadata
payload filter in Qdrant *before* vector similarity scoring. `QdrantFilterConverter`
translates it to a gRPC `FieldCondition`. Only entries matching the filter participate
in the similarity computation, so the `maxResults` limit is respected correctly.

### Context

A post-retrieval Java stream filter on `embeddingStore.search(request).matches()` is
incorrect at scale. With `maxResults=8` and a corpus of 100 domain=tools entries plus
5 domain=jvm entries, a search with domain=jvm may return 8 tools entries (all more
similar to the query), the Java filter removes them all, and the caller receives 0
results — silently.

### The technique

```java
import dev.langchain4j.store.embedding.filter.Filter;
import dev.langchain4j.store.embedding.filter.comparison.IsEqualTo;

Filter domainFilter = (domain != null && !domain.isBlank())
        ? new IsEqualTo("domain", domain)
        : null;

var request = EmbeddingSearchRequest.builder()
        .queryEmbedding(queryEmbedding)
        .maxResults(maxResults)
        .filter(domainFilter)   // null = no filter
        .build();

List<SearchResult> results = embeddingStore.search(request).matches().stream()
        .map(...)               // no post-retrieval filter needed
        .toList();
```

### Filter chain (verified from bytecode)

`IsEqualTo("domain", value)` → `EmbeddingSearchRequest.filter()` → `QdrantEmbeddingStore.search()` → `QdrantFilterConverter.buildEqCondition()` → Qdrant gRPC `FieldCondition` (server-side payload filter).

Available filter types (all in `dev.langchain4j.store.embedding.filter.comparison`):
`IsEqualTo`, `IsNotEqualTo`, `IsIn`, `IsNotIn`, `IsGreaterThan`, `IsLessThan`,
`ContainsString`. Logical combinators: `And`, `Or`, `Not` (in `...filter.logical`).

`InMemoryEmbeddingStore` (used in `@QuarkusTest` mocks) also honours the filter —
tests behave correctly without a real Qdrant instance.

### Why this is non-obvious

A developer adding domain filtering naturally reaches for Java stream `.filter()` on
the returned matches. The `EmbeddingSearchRequest.filter()` API exists but is not
prominently documented for the Qdrant integration. The correctness issue (limit
truncation at scale) only becomes visible with a corpus large enough that
same-domain entries don't dominate the top-N by similarity.

entries.title: DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService is a non-obvious CDI dependency of SignalReceivedEventHandler — excluding it causes deployment failure
entries.body:
DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService is a non-obvious CDI dependency of SignalReceivedEventHandler — excluding it causes deployment failure

## Symptom

CDI deployment fails when `SignalReceivedEventHandler` is active but
`DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService` is excluded via `quarkus.arc.exclude-types`.
The error names `WorkerExecutionRecoveryService` as unsatisfied — not `SignalReceivedEventHandler`
— so the connection between the two is non-obvious.

## Context

When building a test profile that needs `SignalReceivedEventHandler` active (e.g. to test
case completion driven by `CaseHubRuntime.signal()`), you cannot simply remove the handler
from the exclude list. `SignalReceivedEventHandler` injects `WorkerExecutionRecoveryService`,
which is only satisfied by `DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService`. Both must be active together.

## Root Cause

`SignalReceivedEventHandler.onSignalReceived()` has a recovery path:

```java
return recoveryService
    .loadOrRestoreCaseInstance(event.caseId())
    .chain(instance -> applySignal(instance, event));
```

If the case instance is not in cache, it delegates to `WorkerExecutionRecoveryService`.
The injection point is eager — CDI validates it at augmentation time regardless of whether
the recovery path is actually hit.

## Fix

In your test profile's `quarkus.arc.exclude-types`, either:

1. **Include both handlers** — remove both `SignalReceivedEventHandler` AND
   `DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService` from the exclude list simultaneously.

2. **Exclude both** — if you don't need signal handling, exclude both.

Never activate one without the other.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The class is called `SignalReceivedEventHandler` — the name gives no indication it needs
recovery infrastructure. `DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService` is primarily associated
with the worker execution recovery path, not with signal handling.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-b5723e](GE-20260529-b5723e.md) — casehub-engine (full runtime) as compile dep causes 31+ CDI deployment failures

Quarkus CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module dependencies
entries.body:
Quarkus CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module dependencies

## Behaviour

In a Quarkus multi-module Maven project, if a plain library module (no Quarkus Maven plugin, `packaging=jar`) contains `@ApplicationScoped` CDI beans and is added as a `<dependency>` of the Quarkus application module, those beans are NOT discovered by CDI. Injection of these beans at runtime fails with "Unsatisfied dependency for type X".

## Context

Occurs when splitting a single-module Quarkus app into a library module + Quarkus app module. The library beans compile fine and the JAR is on the classpath, but Quarkus CDI ignores them.

## Root Cause

Quarkus CDI bean discovery only indexes classes in JARs that are either: (a) the application itself, (b) have a Jandex index (`META-INF/jandex.idx`), or (c) are explicitly listed in `quarkus.index-dependency`. Plain JARs without Jandex are not scanned.

## Fix

Add to `claudony-app/src/main/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib-core
```

Or add the Jandex Maven plugin to the library module's `pom.xml`:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>io.smallrye</groupId>
    <artifactId>jandex-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.2</version>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <goals><goal>jandex</goal></goals>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>
```

## Why Undocumented

The Quarkus docs for multi-module projects mention the Jandex plugin as an option but do not clearly state that without it (or `index-dependency`), CDI beans in library JARs are silently ignored. The error message "Unsatisfied dependency" doesn't hint at the indexing problem — it looks like the bean simply doesn't exist.

**See also:** [GE-20260421-03fa72](GE-20260421-03fa72.md) — Jandex Maven plugin approach to same problem; [GE-20260423-a01832](GE-20260423-a01832.md) — @Alternative test JARs same root cause; [GE-0133](GE-0133.md) — CDI silently ignores @ApplicationScoped beans in third-party JARs without Jandex
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — same CDI scanning root cause for @Alternative beans in test-helper JARs

**See also:** GE-20260428-9311f8 — indexed beans cause CDI collision (opposite problem)

entries.title: @All List<T> in Quarkus/ArC gives testable multi-bean injection without Instance<T>
entries.body:
@All List<T> in Quarkus/ArC gives testable multi-bean injection without Instance<T>

## Context
When building a registry or routing layer that needs all CDI beans of a type
(e.g. routing to the right `Connector` by id), the natural CDI approach is
`Instance<T>`. But `Instance<T>` is a CDI interface with many abstract methods
— difficult to stub in plain JUnit tests without a full container.

## What developers normally do
Inject `Instance<T>`, iterate with `.stream()`, or use `@InjectMock` inside a
`@QuarkusTest` — both require a CDI container.

## The technique
Inject `@All List<T>` instead. ArC populates it at startup with all discovered
beans. In tests, pass `List.of(...)` directly to the constructor — no CDI
context, no mocking of `Instance<T>`, no `@QuarkusTest` needed.

```java
// Production: ArC populates this automatically
ConnectorService(@All List<Connector> connectors) {
    this.registry = connectors.stream()
        .collect(Collectors.toMap(Connector::id, Function.identity()));
}

// Test: plain JUnit, no CDI
new ConnectorService(List.of(new SlackConnector(), new TeamsConnector()));
```

## Why this is non-obvious
`Instance<T>` is the CDI standard; `@All` is ArC-specific and not prominently
documented for this use case. The standard guidance says to use `@QuarkusTest`
for CDI testing. `@All List<T>` sidesteps the container requirement entirely
for patterns where the bean set is static at startup.

## Constraints
Requires `io.quarkus.arc.All`. Only works when the bean set is consumed at
construction time (static registry). Not suitable if beans are added/removed
at runtime.

**See also:** [GE-20260604-2f0889](GE-20260604-2f0889.md) — @Alternative @Priority(N) stubs appear in @All List<T> alongside original bean — not as replacements (gotcha when combining these patterns)

**See also:** [GE-20260529-ab148d](GE-20260529-ab148d.md) — multiple CDI constructors without @Inject → UnsatisfiedResolutionException (related CDI injection gotcha)

**See also:** [GE-20260609-75e00d](GE-20260609-75e00d.md) — package-private test constructor + @PostConstruct null guard technique

**See also:** [GE-20260614-efee3b](GE-20260614-efee3b.md) — SyntheticBeanBuildItem without addInjectionPoint compiles but fails at runtime

entries.title: @ApplicationScoped bean present in JAR bytecode is invisible to Quarkus ARC without a Jandex index
entries.body:
@ApplicationScoped bean present in JAR bytecode is invisible to Quarkus ARC without a Jandex index

## Symptom

ARC reports `"Unsatisfied dependency for type com.example.SomeBean"` at augmentation time. `javap -p -verbose` on the `.class` file inside the JAR confirms `@ApplicationScoped` is present as a `RuntimeVisibleAnnotation`. The class is on the classpath.

## Context

A third-party or internal library JAR has `@ApplicationScoped` (or other CDI scope) beans but was not built with the `jandex-maven-plugin` and ships no `META-INF/jandex.idx`. Quarkus ARC relies on Jandex for efficient bean discovery — without an index it does not scan the JAR by default.

## Root cause

Quarkus ARC uses Jandex as its primary bean discovery mechanism. JARs without a Jandex index and without `beans.xml` are treated as non-bean-archives and skipped entirely. The CDI annotations in bytecode are present but ARC never reads them.

## Fix

Add an explicit index-dependency entry in `application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib
```

Quarkus will then build an in-memory Jandex index for that JAR at augmentation time, discovering all CDI beans.

If the JAR should only be indexed in production (not in `@QuarkusTest`), use the `%prod.` profile prefix:

```properties
%prod.quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
%prod.quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib
```

And add a separate test-scope entry to `src/test/resources/application.properties` if needed there.

## Verifying the JAR

```bash
python3 -c "
import zipfile
jar = 'path/to/mylib.jar'
with zipfile.ZipFile(jar) as z:
    has_jandex = any('jandex' in n for n in z.namelist())
    has_beans = any('beans.xml' in n for n in z.namelist())
    print(f'jandex={has_jandex}, beans.xml={has_beans}')
"
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The error "Unsatisfied dependency" implies the class doesn't exist or isn't a CDI bean — it says nothing about indexing. `javap` shows the annotation is genuinely in the bytecode. There is no Quarkus warning at augmentation time that a JAR was skipped due to missing Jandex.

**See also:** [GE-20260522-adb5cd](GE-20260522-adb5cd.md) — moving bean from app module to library JAR breaks CDI discovery | [GE-20260427-c2b84f](../quarkus/GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — @Alternative beans in test-helper jar invisible without Jandex

**See also:** [GE-20260601-7a3b38](GE-20260601-7a3b38.md) — @DefaultBean implementations invisible when supertype JAR has no Jandex index

**See also:** [GE-20260609-18a0b1](GE-20260609-18a0b1.md) — quarkus.index-dependency for a JAR that ships META-INF/jandex.idx double-scans it (opposite problem: don't add if already indexed)

**See also:** [GE-20260615-c234fc](GE-20260615-c234fc.md) — @DefaultBean in module without quarkus-arc is silently ignored

entries.title: CDI bean ordering non-determinism causes silent registry key collision — symptoms mimic scheduler contamination
entries.body:
CDI bean ordering non-determinism causes silent registry key collision — symptoms mimic scheduler contamination

### Symptom
12 @QuarkusTest tests fail with ConditionTimeoutException — cases start (RUNNING)
but workers never execute and cases never reach COMPLETED. Failures appear
test-ordering-dependent: passing in isolation, failing in the full suite. Symptoms
are indistinguishable from Quartz scheduler contamination or event bus saturation.

### Context
Two CaseHub beans — one Java-defined, one YAML-defined — both register under the
same registry key (namespace, name, version). The registry uses `ConcurrentHashMap`
with "first wins" semantics: whichever CDI bean's `getDefinition()` is iterated first
via `Instance<CaseHub>` determines the active definition for that key. Quarkus ARC
does not guarantee `Instance<T>` iteration order across JVM instances.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. Investigated Quartz scheduler contamination — stale jobs, event bus saturation
2. Checked LoopControl, AgentRoutingStrategy, and WorkerExecutionManager for shared state
3. Grepped for thread-safety issues in the JQ evaluator

### Root cause
The YAML definition used flat-context JQ expressions (`.status == "processing"`)
while the Java definition used panels-convention expressions (`.working.status == "processing"`).
When the YAML definition registered first, its expression was evaluated against a context
where data lived under `.working.status`. The JQ evaluator correctly returned `false` —
the expression was wrong for that context shape. No error, no warning — silent mismatch.

### Fix
1. Give each CaseHub bean a unique (namespace, name, version) key
2. Add fail-fast duplicate detection at startup in `registerKnownDefinitions()` —
   throws IllegalStateException naming both colliding beans
3. Add CaseKeyUniquenessTest — explicit regression guard scanning all beans

### Why this is non-obvious
Every symptom pointed to scheduler contamination: timeouts, workers not executing,
test-ordering dependency. The actual cause (registry key collision + CDI ordering
non-determinism) is invisible without tracing through the expression evaluation chain
with targeted debug logging. The JQ evaluator worked correctly — it evaluated the
wrong expression against the right data, returning `false` as expected.

### Why this fix
Fail-fast at startup is stronger than a dedicated test alone: every @QuarkusTest in
every module becomes an implicit collision detector. The explicit test provides
diagnostic messages naming both beans for fast resolution.

### Alternatives considered
- Warn-and-skip on duplicate keys (original behaviour) — silent, causes exactly this bug
- Structural equality check on definitions instead of identity — too permissive; two
  beans producing structurally identical definitions is still a maintenance hazard
- Making CDI bean ordering deterministic — not possible without Quarkus ARC changes

**See also:** [GE-20260612-ce4271](GE-20260612-ce4271.md) — MutableCurrentPrincipal @ApplicationScoped state leaks between @QuarkusTest classes

entries.title: Quarkus CDI does not scan @Alternative beans in third-party test jars without explicit index config
entries.body:
Quarkus CDI does not scan @Alternative beans in third-party test jars without explicit index config

## Symptom

`@Alternative @Priority(1)` beans from a `test`-scoped dependency are not selected by CDI in `@QuarkusTest` runs. The default (non-alternative) beans are used instead. No error is thrown — the wrong beans silently win.

## Context

Occurs when a library ships a `-testing` artifact (e.g. `quarkus-mylib-testing`) containing `@Alternative` in-memory implementations intended for use in consumer test suites. Adding the artifact as a `<scope>test</scope>` dependency is not sufficient.

## Root Cause

Quarkus CDI only scans jars that are explicitly indexed. Third-party jars without a Jandex index (or without explicit `quarkus.index-dependency` config) are not scanned for CDI beans, even if the jar is on the classpath and the beans are annotated correctly.

## Fix

Add an index-dependency entry in `src/test/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib-testing.group-id=io.example
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib-testing.artifact-id=quarkus-mylib-testing
```

Adjust `group-id` and `artifact-id` to match the testing jar's Maven coordinates.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The dependency is on the classpath. The beans have the correct `@Alternative @Priority(1)` annotations. No exception is thrown. The library's documentation typically says "add the testing dependency" without mentioning the index requirement. The failure mode — wrong beans used silently — is identical to not having the dependency at all, which misleads diagnosis toward annotation or scope issues.

**See also:** GE-0133 — CDI silently ignores beans in jars without Jandex index (general case) | GE-20260421-03fa72 — optional module jar missing Jandex index | GE-20260417-c59817 — quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives activates @Alternative beans

**See also:** [GE-20260424-59906a](GE-20260424-59906a.md) — same CDI scanning root cause for non-alternative beans in library JARs
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — @Alternative beans in test-helper jar — producer-side Jandex fix | [GE-20260427-62d3ab](GE-20260427-62d3ab.md) — @Alternative @Priority(1) inner bean spy technique

**See also:** GE-20260427-987198 — quarkus.arc.exclude-types suppresses CDI beans from library JARs

entries.title: static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper
entries.body:
static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper

## Symptom

An SSE endpoint (or any endpoint) that serialises `SessionResponse` objects silently returns `[]` (empty array) instead of the actual workers. No exception is visible to the caller — the exception is caught internally and the fallback value is returned. The server logs may show a `com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException` at DEBUG level.

## Context

`SessionResource` in Claudony had:
```java
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();
```

`SessionResponse` contains `Instant createdAt` and `Instant lastActive`. Serializing a `List<SessionResponse>` through this `MAPPER` threw:
```
com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException:
Java 8 date/time type `java.time.Instant` not supported by default:
add Module "com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype:jackson-datatype-jsr310"
```

The exception was caught in the `buildCaseSnapshot` helper and the fallback `"data: []"` was returned silently.

## Root Cause

`new ObjectMapper()` creates a bare instance with no modules registered. Quarkus configures its CDI-managed `ObjectMapper` bean with `JavaTimeModule` (and other modules like `Jdk8Module`) automatically. A static field bypasses this configuration.

## Fix

Replace the static field with CDI injection:

```java
// WRONG — no JavaTimeModule
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();

// CORRECT — uses Quarkus-configured instance with all modules
@Inject
ObjectMapper MAPPER;
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The class had a `static final` `ObjectMapper` that worked fine for other uses (simple POJOs without time fields). The failure only surfaced when the snapshot function started serialising `SessionResponse` with `Instant` fields. Because the exception was caught with a fallback, the endpoint returned valid JSON (`[]`) — not an error — making it very hard to notice.

entries.title: Jackson readValue() silently accepts trailing content unless FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS is enabled
entries.body:
Jackson readValue() silently accepts trailing content unless FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS is enabled

## Context

When using `ObjectMapper.readValue()` to parse a JSON string, Jackson does NOT reject trailing non-JSON content by default. The parse succeeds and the trailing content is silently discarded.

```java
// This succeeds silently — "extra text" is ignored
Map<String, Object> result = mapper.readValue(
    "{\"key\": \"value\"} extra text here",
    Map.class
);
// result = {"key": "value"}
```

## Symptom

A detection method intended to reject `{"type":"DONE","content":"ok"} explanatory text` accepts it as valid JSON and extracts `{"type":"DONE","content":"ok"}` — the trailing text never causes an error.

The practical failure: an LLM output like `{"type":"DONE","content":"Task complete"} I've now finished the task.` is silently classified as a JSON speech act rather than falling through to bracket-prefix detection, producing unexpected DONE classification when the agent was narrating.

## Root Cause

Jackson's `JsonParser` stops at the end of the first complete JSON value and considers the parse successful. `readValue()` wraps this and makes the trailing content invisible. This is the specified behaviour — Jackson is designed for streaming contexts where trailing content is normal.

## Fix

Enable `DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS` on the `ObjectMapper`:

```java
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper()
        .enable(DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS);
```

With this flag set, `readValue("{\"key\":\"val\"} extra", Map.class)` throws `JsonParseException: Trailing token (of type START_OBJECT) found after value`. Catching the exception and falling through to the next detection tier is then correct.

## Why this is non-obvious

Jackson's docs describe `FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS` as an option to opt into, not a default-off footgun. The lenient default exists for good reasons (streaming, multi-value streams), but it's invisible when you have a one-shot string parse and expect strict behaviour. The feature was added in Jackson 2.9 — older documentation doesn't mention it. Without knowing to look for it, the obvious assumption is that `readValue()` of a complete JSON string validates the full input.

### Why this fix

`FAIL_ON_TRAILING_TOKENS` is the canonical solution — it targets exactly this behaviour without affecting normal parsing. Alternative: use `JsonParser` directly and check `parser.nextToken() == null` after parse, but this requires more boilerplate and is less obvious to readers.

### Alternatives considered

- **Use `JsonParser` directly** — check `parser.nextToken() == null` after reading the value. More boilerplate; requires try-with-resources pattern and is less discoverable.
- **Check `input.trim().endsWith("}")` before parsing** — fragile; fails for arrays, nested objects, and JSON with trailing whitespace.
- **Regex pre-check** — would reject valid multi-line JSON. Not viable.

entries.title: Canonical CloudEvent adapter pattern — 7 rules for CDI async adapters
entries.body:
With `.whenComplete()`: exception preserved on the stage → `thenCompose` skips its lambda
→ exception surfaces to SmallRye → message nacked → retried or dead-lettered.

Rule 3 applies **only in pure fire-and-forget dispatch** (the `CompletionStage` is not
returned or chained further).

CDI `@ObservesAsync` adapters are always fire-and-forget.

SmallRye `@Incoming` consumers return the stage for ack/nack signalling — they are not.

### Alternatives considered

- **Let exceptions propagate from `@ObservesAsync`** — rejected; CDI swallows them silently on the managed executor thread
- **Use `@Observes` (sync) instead of `@ObservesAsync`** — rejected; would block the original event producer's thread
- **Static ObjectMapper** — rejected; isolates serialisation config, breaks when app registers custom serializers
- **SEVERE log level for failures** — rejected; serialisation failure of a single event is degraded, not catastrophic; SEVERE causes alert fatigue

**See also:** [GE-20260423-daef97](GE-20260423-daef97.md) — CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync — fireAsync() required (rule 1 of the canonical adapter pattern) | [GE-20260531-e1ce47](GE-20260531-e1ce47.md) — CDI @Observes and @ObservesAsync are separate delivery channels | [GE-20260428-f075ef](GE-20260428-f075ef.md) — Race-free CompletableFuture per-item pattern for CDI async event tests

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: casehub-work IllegalStateExceptionMapper silently maps IllegalStateException to HTTP 409
entries.body:
casehub-work IllegalStateExceptionMapper silently maps IllegalStateException to HTTP 409

### Symptom

REST endpoint returns HTTP 409 Conflict instead of the expected 500 Internal Server Error.
Happens when application code throws `IllegalStateException` for an infrastructure failure
(poll timeout, misconfiguration, unresolvable dependency).

### Context

Any application that depends on `casehub-work` and throws `IllegalStateException` from a
REST-invoked code path.

### Root cause

`casehub-work` JAR ships `io.casehub.work.runtime.api.IllegalStateExceptionMapper` — a
`jakarta.ws.rs.ext.ExceptionMapper<IllegalStateException>` that maps every
`IllegalStateException` to HTTP 409 Conflict. The mapper is registered globally via CDI
and affects any `IllegalStateException` thrown during a REST request in the application,
not just ones thrown from casehub-work code itself.

### Fix

Use a different exception type for infrastructure failures that should produce 500:

```java
// ❌ Produces HTTP 409 — mapped by casehub-work's IllegalStateExceptionMapper
throw new IllegalStateException("Agent did not reply for correlationId=" + id);

// ✅ Produces HTTP 500 — no mapper for generic RuntimeException
throw new RuntimeException("Agent did not reply for correlationId=" + id);
```

Alternatively, register a `@Priority(1)` exception mapper that overrides casehub-work's
for specific exception subtypes, or use a custom exception class that extends
`RuntimeException` directly.

### Why this is non-obvious

The mapper is shipped inside a transitive JAR (`casehub-work-0.2-SNAPSHOT.jar`) and is
nowhere documented. The symptom (409 instead of 500) gives no hint that an exception
mapper is involved — 409 Conflict is semantically about resource state, so developers
naturally investigate their domain logic rather than exception infrastructure. The 5-second
delay before the 409 (from a poll timeout) makes the connection even harder to spot.

Merging a branch with `--delete-branch` auto-closes any PR targeting that branch
entries.body:
Merging a branch with `--delete-branch` auto-closes any PR targeting that branch

## Merging a branch with `--delete-branch` auto-closes any PR targeting that branch

**ID:** GE-0175
**Stack:** GitHub (all versions)
**Symptom:** PR #B (targeting branch A) is silently closed — not retargeted — when branch A is merged and deleted. No warning, no retargeting prompt.
**Context:** Stacked PRs (B → A → main). Merging A with `--delete-branch` immediately closes B because its base branch no longer exists.

**Root cause:** GitHub closes PRs whose base branch is deleted rather than retargeting them — it cannot safely determine the new base. The PR must be manually recreated.

**Fix:**

Option 1 — Retarget before deleting:
```bash
gh pr merge A --merge          # no --delete-branch
gh pr edit B --base main
git push origin --delete A
```

Option 2 — Recreate after the fact:
```bash
gh pr create --base main --head B --title "..." --body "..."
```

**Prevention:** With stacked PRs, always retarget dependent PRs before deleting the base branch.

*Score: 11/15 · Non-obvious GitHub behaviour; error is silent with no warning · Reservation: GitHub-specific, may change if GitHub adds retargeting logic*

entries.title: quarkus.http.auth.permission.*.policy=deny on /* blocks ALL requests — @RolesAllowed becomes unreachable dead code
entries.body:
quarkus.http.auth.permission.*.policy=deny on /* blocks ALL requests — @RolesAllowed becomes unreachable dead code

### Symptom
Every endpoint returns 403. Always. Regardless of authentication status, role, or `@RolesAllowed` annotation. The application is completely non-functional.

### Context
When adding OIDC + `@RolesAllowed` to a Quarkus application, a natural instinct is to add a catch-all deny policy so missing annotations fail closed:
```properties
quarkus.http.auth.permission.default.paths=/*
quarkus.http.auth.permission.default.policy=deny
```

### What was tried (didn't work)
The above configuration. Also tried adding `/q/*` health exemptions — the deny policy still blocks all application endpoints.

### Root cause
`quarkus.http.auth.permission.*` policies operate at the **Vert.x HTTP filter layer** — before the JAX-RS container processes the request and before CDI security interceptors run. A `deny` policy on `/*` rejects all requests at that filter, regardless of authentication status, role, or any `@RolesAllowed` annotation. `@RolesAllowed` is never evaluated — it is unreachable dead code.

Additionally, `quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` (which suppresses `@RolesAllowed` evaluation in dev mode) does **not** suppress HTTP permission policies — those are at the `io.quarkus.vertx.http.runtime.security` layer, not the CDI authorization controller layer. Dev mode is also completely broken.

### Fix
Do not use `quarkus.http.auth.permission.*.policy=deny` as a catch-all. Use the JAX-RS-layer mechanism instead:
```properties
quarkus.security.deny-unannotated-members=true
```
This operates inside the JAX-RS container after authentication. `@RolesAllowed` annotations remain fully functional. It is suppressed in dev mode by `auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` (same CDI authorization controller layer).

### Why this is non-obvious
The two security layers (Vert.x HTTP filter vs CDI/JAX-RS interceptor) are not clearly distinguished in the Quarkus documentation. Both use `quarkus.` config prefixes. The HTTP permission approach looks like a standard deny-by-default pattern and produces no startup warning — the 403s only appear at runtime.
